My dogs follow me around the house, no matter what I’m doing. Even at dinner time, they would be by my side keeping a close eye on what I’m doing. I was having pork for dinner the other night, and in the corner of my eye, I could see my furry friends watching me in the hope of getting a piece of the meat or even the bones. But this got me wondering, can dogs eat pork bones? Is it safe for them to do so? No, dogs should not eat pork bones because the bones can easily splinter and crack when they chew on them, and this can lead to various problems when swallowed. If a dog swallows a splintered or cracked pork bone, then it can lead to intestinal blockages, and it can even damage the esophagus or intestines. In some cases, a dog might even choke on a pork bone when he attempts to swallow it, especially if it is a small dog. What To Do If Your Dog Has Eaten A Pork Bone As a dog parent, I know it can be very difficult to keep an eye on a dog and monitor its every move. Sometimes, they get into situations that we always try to avoid, like eating something that they should not eat. If your dog has eaten a pork bone, or if he has just started chewing on it, take it away from him immediately. If you suspect that he has swallowed a piece of the bone, then it is very important that you contact a vet immediately. You need to monitor your dog closely for signs that show that he is not feeling well due to swallowing the bone, and might be suffering from an intestinal blockage. These signs include: • Vomiting • Loss of Appetite • Irregular Bowel Movements • Excessive Drooling As mentioned, it is a good idea to consult with a vet immediately, especially if your dog is showing any of the signs mentioned above. Can Dogs Eat Pork Rib Bones? No, dogs should not eat pork rib bones because the bones can break easily, and this can cause serious harm to a dog when swallowed. Swallowing a splintered or cracked pork rib bone can damage a dog’s mouth, esophagus, or intestines and it can even be a choking hazard. So, it is best to keep your pup clear of pork rib bones. What Bones Are Safe For Dogs? The good news is that there are other types of bones that your dog can safely enjoy. In fact, a lot of raw uncooked bones are safe for dogs as long as they are big enough so that your dog can’t swallow them. Cow or Bison bones are the safest bones for your dog to eat, and your pup can safely chew, eat, and digest them. It is important though that you still monitor your dog closely when giving him any type of bone. There is always a risk of the bone getting stuck in his throat, or being too hard to chew, which can cause damage to his teeth. If you’re concerned about your dog’s teeth getting damaged from eating bones, then you should consider giving him raw chicken, turkey, lamb, or beef bones as these are fairly soft and easy to chew on. What Are The Benefits Of Feeding Bones To Your Dog? Feeding your dog the right type of bones can actually be very healthy for him. Bones are, of course, rich in calcium, and this helps with the formation of bones and teeth, nerve impulse transmission, cell signaling, muscle contract, and blood coagulation in dogs. Chewing on a bone can also provide mental stimulation to dogs, and it is known for helping dogs with anxiety, which in turn helps with high blood pressure and heart disease. But, as mentioned previously, there are always risks involved in feeding your dog bones. If you are not sure whether or not to give your dog bones, talk to a vet to get some clarity. As you can see, you should never feed your dog pork bones. These bones can easily break or splinter and can cause serious problems to your dog when he swallows them. The biggest problem with swallowing a cracked pork bone is that it can cause intestinal blockages, and this can cause your pup to become very ill. Small dogs might even choke when attempting to swallow a pork bone. If you suspect that your pup has swallowed a splintered pork bone, then get in touch with a vet immediately. | <urn:uuid:00e4a1f8-3642-48f2-b25e-502ae55bb516> | https://jackrussellterrierdog.com/can-dogs-eat-pork-bones/ | mlfoundations/dclm-baseline-1.0-parquet | What is the Minimalist 50:20:30 Budget System (and how to use it) The 50:20:30 Budget is gaining popularity, particularly among minimalist populations. This budget helps you break down your goal spending into 3 categories making it easier to keep to your budget than a budget which is broken down into 5 or more groups. Keep reading to learn more about the 50:20:30 budget and how to use it! This post may contain affiliate links What is the 50:20:30 Budget? The 50:20:30 budget is a simplified budget utilizing 3 categories: essentials, savings, and personal. I have been using this strategy on and off since I graduated high school and it has been the most effective budget strategy I have used yet! It is not only easy to use and simple but encompasses all possible spending without much categorical overlap to worry about. There are no excuses. 50 | Essential Spending The first rule or spending category is essential spending. This category refers to your bills and any other expenditures that cannot be avoided and occurs regularly. This might include topics such as housing, utilities, groceries, transportation, insurance, electricity, internet, phone bill, etc. These bills do not include pleasure subscriptions, or anything extravagant and unnecessary. Remember that your essential spending is half of your income, so spend it wisely! 20 | Savings The second budget rule is that 20% of your income is to be saved. This might include a savings account, emergency fund, retirement fund, or it might go toward paying off debt. Building up a savings is incredibly essential in case of emergencies, debt issues, retirement, etc. I can personally attest that my savings have saved me, financially, on several occasions! If you aren’t currently saving money regularly, open a savings account and get started today! 30 | Personal Spending The final rule of 50:20:30 budgeting is that you get to spend 30% on anything else. This includes all recreational or entertainment purchases. This money can be set aside to go on vacation; it can also include clothing, hobbies, home decorating, gym membership, recreational, and entertainment expenditures. Here is where you can go crazy (so long as you remain within your budget) because this category is all you. Whatever your heart desires, this is the part of your budget you utilize to get it. Be responsible, but so long as you remain within 30%, you’re golden! Are Your Finances Ruining Your Life? How do I use the 50:20:30 Budget? What makes the 50:20:30 budget so successful is the fact that it is minimal and simple. It’s easy to remember the rules, and it’s easier to keep track of, regardless of how you track your spending. My favorite way to track my spending is through Mint. Mint connects to all of my accounts, allows me to set my budget, savings goals, and even pay my bills. I highly recommend checking out Mint, its free! Another great way to use this budget is to keep an excel spreadsheet of your spending or keep a written record in your planner or journal. Just knowing what the budget is, is not enough, you must now track your spending and be sure to adhere to the budgeting rules. Initially bringing back memories of the Doklam standoff in June 2017, the recent clashes resulting in the deaths of 20 Indian soldiers have seemed to hearken back to the more serious clashes, such as those at Nathu La in 1967 and even the 1962 border war. As the tensions continue, analysts and commentators have provided numerous unconfirmed reasons for the renewed standoff and what has been seen by some as surprising Chinese aggression. This analysis has seen a growing focus on domestic factors, such as a potential weakening of President Xi’s position in the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) after COVID-19 or precipitating actions in India’s foreign policy as possible causes for the standoff. However, this article argues that the primary reason for China’s aggression is security and strategic concerns related to India’s road and infrastructure construction along the LAC rather than domestic factors. Explanations for the Current Border Standoff The primary reason given for the current India-China border standoff(s) is India’s building of roads and infrastructure along the LAC. China’s more favorable topography, metaled roads and other infrastructure to deploy troops and military equipment in the event of a war with India, has given it a strategic advantage on its side of the border. India is well aware of this disadvantage and under Prime Minister Narendra Modi, has prioritized building infrastructure to negate China’s advantage and is fast catching up. China perceives Indian construction, especially the nearly complete 255 kilometer Darbuk-Shyokh-Daulat Beg Oldie (DBO) (DSDBO) all-weather road, as attempts to mitigate China’s strategic and military edge along the disputed border, which prompted China to try to prevent India from further road building. China perceives Indian construction, especially the nearly complete 255 kilometer Darbuk-Shyokh-Daulat Beg Oldie all-weather road, as attempts to mitigate China’s strategic and military edge along the disputed border, which prompted China to try to prevent India from further road building. The DSDBO road connects Leh in Ladakh to the DBO military airbase, and allows troops and equipment to move to the border much faster. Control over the DSDBO road requires control over a ridgeline of the Galwan valley—the site of the deadly clashes on June 15—and control of the valley would provide access to Aksai Chin (where the Tibet-Xinjiang highway passes). West of Daluat Beg Oldie is Gilgit-Baltistan, part of Pakistan Administered Kashmir and part of the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC). This has been one of the main reason’s for India’s objections to CPEC as it claims it is passing through disputed territory. China is anxious that India can use its strategic leverage in this area to disrupt CPEC which could have a disastrous impact on Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) and China’s socio-economic and political stability. As the flagship project of the BRI launched by Xi Jinping and with ties to China’s own security concerns in Xinjiang, threats to CPEC may also be viewed as threatening China’s unity, sovereignty, and territorial integrity concerns. The DSDBO road also raises China’s apprehensions regarding Aksai Chin, which China occupied after the India-China war in 1962. The Indian government’s revocation of Article 370 and statements on restoring Pakistan administered Kashmir and Aksai Chin into the Indian federation have exacerbated China’s distrust of India and India’s intentions. Additional analysis for causes of the border conflict has pointed towards recent Indian actions as a source of China’s resentment, such as support for an independent inquiry into the origins of COVID-19 and sending two members of parliament to Taiwanese President Tsai Ing-wen’s virtual swearing-in ceremony in May. As recent chair of the World Health Organization (WHO), a separate argument is that China is also trying to pressure India not to back a change in Taiwan’s status in the World Health Assembly (WHA). However, India has been one of more than 100 countries calling for an independent inquiry. On Taiwan, India has held to a “one China” policy and shown reluctance to support Taiwan’s WHA bid. Finally, some have also pointed to India’s recent change in foreign direct investment laws meant to prevent “opportunistic takeovers” of Indian companies which is reportedly aimed at China. However, while China has invested around USD $30.7 billion in India between 2005-2019, this is still a fraction of its global investments of approximately USD $2 trillion. Domestic factors affecting Foreign Policy A separate argument has looked at China’s aggressive posturing at the border in the context of growing assertiveness in Chinese foreign policy in the wake of COVID-19. Indeed, there is precedent for China using external conflicts to distract from domestic issues—take for instance the 1962 border war in the wake of Mao’s disastrous “Great Leap Forward” setting him on a precarious position within the CCP. Similarly, one of the reasons cited for Deng Xiao Ping going to war with Vietnam in 1979 was diverting attention from China’s harsh economic conditions. There is no doubt that Xi’s botched tackling of COVID-19 in the initial stages has undermined his position in the CCP and punctured his regime legitimacy built on technocratic competence. It has “underlined the worst facets of the Chinese command and control system of governance since President Xi assumed power,” and poses a significant threat to Xi’s personal legacy. Some have pointed to the current border standoff as part of a more aggressive foreign policy driven by fears of international perceptions of weakness in the wake of COVID-19. However, the LAC is a different story. The logistics required for a build-up of military equipment and amassing troops at a high-altitude and in multiple locations along the LAC starting as early as April indicate that plans for a greater military presence in the region may have been in the works for some time. Further, China’s downplaying of the crisis, and media reports indicating that China wants to deescalate suggest China is not trying to use the current standoff to galvanize nationalism to buttress domestic public support. While China has in recent years mobilized anti-U.S. or anti-Japan sentiment for domestic support, it has not done the same with India. Since the end of the Cold War and especially in the twenty first century, China has used nationalism against the United States or Japan and to support ambitions to its East. The Chinese government has for instance fanned nationalist sentiments against Japan as part of the Senkaku/Diaoyu Islands dispute, and used the 1999 NATO bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade to fan anti-U.S. sentiment. More recently, China has blamed the United States for the U.S.-China trade war and used nationalism against the United States to divert attention from the economic slowdown as Chinese netizens have voiced anger at U.S. attempts to contain China’s rise. The logistics required for a build-up of military equipment and amassing troops at a high-altitude and in multiple locations along the LAC starting as early as April indicate that plans for a greater military presence in the region may have been in the works for some time. Although there is compelling evidence that China has used external conflicts to drum up nationalism domestically, India does not seem to fall into this category—particularly as China remains focused on competition with the United States and territorial disputes to its East. While the nationalist Global Times—which caters more to an international audience—has published in-depth stories condemning Indian actions on the border, other Chinese language publications such as Xinhua, China Daily, and the People’s Daily mention the dispute in short reports in the back pages. Chinese netizens appear far more focused on Taiwan and Hong Kong which are considered more pressing issues. China does not use nationalism against India because it does not consider India as a major security threat or a peer competitor. China’s main competition is with the United States, which it aims to surpass economically and militarily by 2049. India, on the other hand, is considered an inferior power due to the gaps in capabilities, such as economic and military power, between the two countries. China has not shied away from conveying this asymmetry to India in diplomatic meetings and interactions, and through government mouth pieces like People’s Daily, the Global Times and others. Simply put, China believes India is not worthy of great power status. China has, however, started paying more attention to India due to the latter’s burgeoning strategic relations with the United States. Plans for road infrastructure near the LAC, and the Modi government’s more nationalist stance have only deepened China’s distrust as well as concern and suspicion of India’s aims on the border. An Indian Army spokesperson stated that Indian media’s “speculative and unsubstantiated reporting about these [military and diplomatic] engagements” is not helpful in resolving the border issue or current standoff. Similarly, specious diagnosis/analysis of the current India-China border standoffs and skirmishes may lead to policies or actions that further deteriorate bilateral relations, create misperceptions, and exacerbate mistrust between the two countries. As India considers its China policy for the future, an understanding of the motivations behind Chinese actions is critical. Conclusions that China is acting out of domestic weakness or in retaliation against India’s foreign or economic decisions, may risk leading “hawks” in each country gaining a bigger voice in decision-making or contributing to more military adventurism. A hardening of the positions in the two countries is likely to have negative repercussions not only on the India-China bilateral relationship but also for regional and even global security and stability. To effectively coach your team, you will need items that help your team practice. Practice drills can help to improve both the physical fitness and overall skill when playing an actual cricket match. Your coaching equipment should be updated or replaced as needed. It’s important to show your team that they are playing with quality equipment. It teaches that that only a quality effort is acceptable. 1. Bat and ball. The cricket bat and ball are used by your players to practice the game of cricket. While on the field, complete cricket drills with your team that involve bowling and using the bat. This way, all of your cricket players will develop the skills necessary to be successful. 2. Whistle. A whistle is essential cricket coaching equipment. Use it to communicate with your players while they’re on the field. Let them know what various whistle combinations mean, such as one short blow for stop or two short blows for huddle up. 3. Target. A target will help improve the accuracy of your cricket players. Whenever they are in control of the cricket ball, they should always be aiming for a target during the practice drills. Watch your players as they aim for the target and correct their form immediately afterward. 4. Video camera. Recording your cricket players as they complete practice drills, such as bowling drills, can be a huge asset when coaching cricket. The cricket coaching equipment you need will vary based on the type of camera you use, but a standard video camera with a screen and playback function should be sufficient. Show your players their tapes, as well as those of exceptional drills completed by their teammates. 5. Uniforms. Encourage your cricket players to wear their team uniforms during practice. This helps them get used to playing while wearing the official uniforms. It can also help to promote a spirit of camaraderie and togetherness that will help you coach a more successful cricket team. Chalice Lighting We light this chalice to shine on our time together. In its light we celebrate the relationships and understanding we are creating in this place and time. May our sharing be deep. Introductions Tell us about yourself in 4 or 5 sentences. Here are some possibilities. ``` I grew up in … I have been … (teacher, parent, engineer, artist, caregiver, salesperson …) I am interested in … (gardening, reading, social justice, painting … ) My family or support system … (partner, children, friends ) In this covenant group I hope … ``` Silence, holding ourselves and each other in silent support Business * Covenant Group Basics. * Zoom Basics (Speaker / Gallery view, Rename, Muting, Chat. Speaking in turns.) The Covenant I commit myself: * to come to meetings when I possibly can, knowing that my presence is important to the group * to let the facilitator know if I will be absent or need to quit * to share responsibility for good process by watching how much time I take to speak, noticing what is going on for others, and considering problems if they occur * to do the reading and thinking about the topic ahead of time * to not gossip about what is shared in the group, and tell only my own story to others * to honor the safety of the group by listening to what others share with an open heart * to refrain from cross-talk, judging, giving advice, or advocating a specific view * to share as deeply as I can when it is my turn and to stay on the "I-message." Shared Readings The human soul does not want to be fixed, it wants simply to be seen and heard. Parker J. Palmer You will never be able to escape from your heart. So it's better to listen to what it has to say. Paulo Coelho When we are committed to doing the work of love we listen even when it hurts. bell hooks Autism makes you listen louder. Christine Robinson Introducing the Process for Deep Sharing/Deep Listening Rounds of Sharing. There are two rounds of sharing. During each round, one person speaks at a time. Speakers share from the heart as deeply as they are willing, reflecting on the topic and their feelings, thoughts, and experiences. The job of listeners is to listen deeply, keeping their hearts open. Round 1. Each person in the group takes about five minutes to reflect aloud on the topic and the preparation materials. They speak without interruption. The facilitator starts. Everyone who wants to speak gets one turn, in any order they choose. Anyone may pass. As each person finishes speaking, there are a few breaths of silence. Then the next person speaks. People can speak in any order. Round 2. This is an opportunity to share additional reflections and thoughts about what others have shared. Speakers still focus on their own thoughts, feelings, and experiences and remain mindful not to judge another's sharing, give advice, or advocate a position. Everyone who wants to speak gets a turn, and everyone is mindful of time so that each person has an opportunity to speak. Deep Listening and Sharing Round 1. Share reflections on listening and the preparation materials. Round 2. As time allows, share additional thoughts or reflect on what others have shared. Closing Circle Extinguishing the Chalice We extinguish this flame and we remember the warmth of our community, the light of our wisdom, the generosity of our sharing. We keep these in our heart until we meet again. Bell or Chime Announcements | <urn:uuid:bb1493bc-9cab-47b3-8c1e-7f439a1febcf> | https://uuabq.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Listening-Gather-2021.pdf | HuggingFaceFW/finepdfs-edu | Property of a currency whereby two units can be substituted in place of one another. Fungibility means that two units of a currency can be mutually substituted and the substituted currency is equal to another unit of the same size. For example, two $10 bills can be exchanged and they are functionally identical to any other $10 bill in circulation (although $10 bills have unique ID numbers and are therefore not completely fungible). Gold is probably a closer example of true fungibility, where any 1 oz. of gold of the same grade is worth the same as another 1 oz. of gold. Haven is fungible due to the nature of the currency which provides no way to link transactions together nor trace the history of any particular XHV. 1 XHV is functionally identical to any other 1 XHV. Fungibility is an advantage Haven has over Bitcoin and almost every other cryptocurrency, due to the privacy inherent in the Haven blockchain and the permanently traceable nature of the Bitcoin blockchain. With Bitcoin, any BTC can be tracked by anyone back to its creation coinbase-transaction. Therefore, if a coin has been used for an illegal purpose in the past, this history will be contained in the blockchain in perpetuity. This lack of fungibility means that certain businesses will be obligated to avoid accepting BTC that have been previously used for purposes which are illegal, or simply run afoul of their Terms of Service. Currently some large Bitcoin companies are blocking, suspending, or closing accounts that have received Bitcoin used in online gambling or other purposes deemed unsavory by said companies. Haven has been built specifically to address the problem of traceability and non-fungibility inherent in other cryptocurrencies. By having completely private transactions Haven is truly fungible and there can be no blacklisting of certain XHV, while at the same time providing all the benefits of a secure, decentralized, permanent blockchain. | <urn:uuid:b2ff12ef-50c3-48ca-9682-b9218ed4da27> | https://havenprotocol.org/knowledge/fungibility/ | HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu | The Athabasca tar sands of northern Alberta contain an estimated 175 to 200 billion barrels of recoverable oil — the largest known hydrocarbon deposit ever discovered. This estimate is based on using existing technologies. Using newer technologies, as much as 2.5 trillion barrels of oil might be recovered — but the costs would be enormous. Development of the tar sands has already begun, with three large companies — Suncor, Syncrude, and Albian — producing large quantities of crude, and six more massive projects in the early stages. As the soaring price of oil makes these resources financially more viable, many other leases are being explored. An estimated US$100 billion is expected to be spent on tar sands development over the next 20 years. Though this may sound like a few lifetimes of oil, there are serious social and environmental issues related to the extraction of this bitumen: the provincial boom-and-bust cycle that is being created, including labour, infrastructure and materials shortages; the water intensivity and resulting pollution; and the significant volumes of natural gas it requires. These concerns raise the question of whether the current rate of expansion (planned growth from one million barrels a day to six million barrels a day, most of it destined for export) is reasonable or sustainable. In terms of the social costs, the dramatic expansion of the tar sands development has created labour shortages, exacerbated by infrastructure shortages in Fort McMurray, that increase the cost of living in the area. The boom is also causing materials and construction costs to shoot up across the province. The rapid pace of extraction has also led to inequities between the provinces, with Alberta in an embarrassment of riches, part of which is being thrown at local infrastructure spending, escalating the boom. In the absence of any plan, provincially or nationally, for pacing the development to maximize the jobs and returns to the province over the long term, there is instead a bonanza, using foreign workers and union-busters in the short term, while offering royalty holidays. The natural gas situation is even less sustainable. Conventional natural gas has already peaked and is on the decline. This is a key source of power generation for Alberta. As the tar sands consume the gas, the province is already returning to coal for power, and losing value-added jobs in the petrochemicals sector. As that clean source of fuel expires, the tar sands are turning to non-conventional gas sources, all of which have higher social and environmental costs: the Mackenzie Valley pipeline, coal-bed methane, and in-situ (extraction of the gas from the bitumen). There has not been adequate public debate on the impacts of coal-bed methane, in spite of the huge landscape impacts of the well-intensive drilling and pipelines needed, and the likely impact on water tables in an area already short on water. The in situ strategy is no better, as it has huge carbon dioxide emissions, while the tar sands operations are already among Canada’s largest industrial polluters.Most of the oil to be taken from the tar sands will go to the United States. In effect, the Athabasca deposits will be the centrepiece of a new continental energy grid. Its main purpose will be to provide a secure supply of fuel for the American industrial and military machines. Canadians are already paying a steep price for feeding the voracious American addiction to the dwindling world reserves of oil and gas. Given that the rapidly increasing exports of Canada’s oil and gas to the U.S. puts our own energy security as a nation in jeopardy; that Canada, despite being a petroleum-producing country, is already forced to import nearly half of the oil its people need; that Canada has less than a 10- year supply of conventional oil and natural gas remaining; that most of the tar sands oil is earmarked for export to the U.S., and most of the natural gas from the North is also intended for the U.S. market or to fuel extraction of the tar sands crude — the continuation of current energy policies is clearly not in the national interest. There may be ways of developing the tar sands that could contribute to Canada’s long-term energy needs, including the inevitable transition to renewable energy sources. But, shockingly, there is no coherent national or provincial energy policy to address this need. Nor is the federal or any provincial government conducting the research or public consultation needed to formulate policies for meeting the world energy crisis — or even for assuring an adequate ongoing supply of energy for Canadians. To be viable, any decisions or policies on energy we arrive at should be based on a complete understanding of all aspects of the tar sands development. It is a project that brings with it enormous economic, social, and ecological costs, as well as raising concerns about our role as suppliers of oil and gas — and soon electricity and even water — to feed the ravenous U.S. appetite for Canada’s resources. This report makes the following recommendations to both federal and provincial governments: Athabasca tar sands development - Place a moratorium on further expansion of the tar sands development until a public inquiry is conducted and completes its report on the social and environmental impacts of this mega-energy project, appropriate provincial plans are in place to maximize jobs over the long term and address the provincial energy and water needs as well as sustainably pace the development, and there has been proper national debate on the implications for Canada’s peace-promotion policy and national energy security; - Rescind direct government subsidies to the oil industry in the tar sands, and replace the minimal 1% royalty rate with a more realistic higher rate; - Place a moratorium on construction of the proposed Mackenzie gas pipeline until outstanding Aboriginal land claims, threat of permafrost damage, and the waste of energy involved in using gas to fuel the production of another form of energy have been satisfactorily settled; and - Transfer to the government of the Northwest Territories the constitutional powers to set its own fair rate of royalties on the extraction of natural gas in its jurisdiction. National Energy Policy Measures - Give the National Energy Board a clear mandate to oversee Canada’s overall energy needs, and arm it with the policy and regulatory tools required to carry out this mandate; - Restore the previous practice of maintaining a national 25-year supply of oil and gas reserves to meet domestic needs; - Take steps to increase Canadian and provincial ownership of the oil and gas industry in Canada (including stopping the sell-off of Petro-Canada) and develop new forms of public ownership and control; - Obtain (like Mexico) an exemption from NAFTA’s proportional sharing clause, or, failing that, withdraw completely from NAFTA; - Conduct a public review of energy price setting practices with a view to restoring the capacity of our governments to regulate energy prices and, if necessary, roll them back; and - Initiate, with the provinces, a national strategy and timetable for reducing dependence on fossil fuels, conserving our remaining supplies of oil and natural gas, and massively increasing investment in renewable energy alternatives such as solar and wind power. | <urn:uuid:51bfba30-0a59-4479-a0d0-2afff4765412> | http://www.parklandinstitute.ca/fuelling_fortress_america | HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu | Syracuse's Christmas Music Station Irene Cara Irene Cara Irene Cara is best known as a singer of movie themes, though she has also maintained an acting career since childhood. Raised by Latin American parents in New York City, she made her Broadway debut in 1967 (at age eight) in the musical Maggie Flynn, and returned to the stage several years later with... Share Email Bookmark | <urn:uuid:fee20ba3-5946-4efa-b3e1-98406e0b892a> | http://www.y94fm.com/iplaylist/artist/16290/ | mlfoundations/dclm-baseline-1.0-parquet | What is Synthetic Marijuana and Is it Legal in New Hampshire? Synthetic Marijuana Synthetic Marijuana In the last few years, a new drug has gained in popularity, particularly with youth. It has also generated a great deal of attention and concern from law enforcement authorities, the medical community, and drug enforcement personnel. The drug is essentially a synthetic form of marijuana commonly known as Spice. It looks plant based like marijuana, but contains a number of chemical compound additives. The drug resembles potpourri to the point that it is often referred to as potpourri on the street. Sold in packets or small jars, it is labeled and known by names such as: K2, Yucatan Fire, Moon Rocks, Skunk and other harmless sounding names. The likeness between Spice and actual marijuana is the primary source of the danger it can pose for users looking for a marijuana substitute or marijuana-like high. It can be smoked, made into a drink, or even mixed with marijuana. The plant based ingredients can range from lettuce or other random plant materials that do not pose any real or known hazards. Spice, or synthetic marijuana is often marketed as a natural substance because of the plant based ingredients. However, it is not natural. The synthetic additives make the effects of Spice very unpredictable. Health Risks The synthetic additives used in the dug can affect everyone differently and have been shown to be more potent that the cannabinoid compounds found in marijuana. There are also a wide variety of chemicals that can added. This means users have no definitive way of knowing what they are ingesting or smoking. The potency or presence of those chemicals can vary from packet to packet or brand to brand. While the mind altering effects common with marijuana are typically felt with Spice, other unexpected effects can be present and lead to health problems. Some Spice users have experienced anxiety, paranoia, hallucinations, vomiting, seizures, rapid heartbeat, kidney damage, and even heart attacks. The number of calls to poison control centers and emergency room visits related to Spice have risen dramatically in the last few years. This increase in calls and medical emergencies also helped to raise public awareness of the drug and its dangers. Legal Action The government has taken steps to ban the use of Spice; which was initially very easy to buy in stores, such as gas stations and convenience stores. It was difficult to regulate a few years ago because it was labeled as “not for human consumption”. Also, as the DEA acted to ban one chemical found in Spice, that chemical would be quickly replaced with another synthetic compound. This would render the ban useless. However, despite the struggles to keep up with the technicalities and alterations manufacturers would implement to avoid regulation, the DEA banned Spice drug in July 2012. Up until then, it could be bought anywhere by anyone. With the federal ban, the chemical additives in Spice are classified as a controlled substance and therefore, it is an illegal drug. As with any controlled substance, the possession, manufacture and sale of such substance is against the law and carries criminal penalties. By Attorney Richard Monteith | <urn:uuid:ebc92c01-0c0d-4d3b-b722-33668417db29> | https://attorneymonteith.com/what-is-synthetic-marijuana-and-is-it-legal-in-new-hampshire/ | mlfoundations/dclm-baseline-1.0-parquet | RACIAL OPTICS AND ITS INFLUENCE ON TEACHER CURRICULAR DECISION-MAKING IN SECONDARY SOCIAL STUDIES by JULIE MUHLENFELD-JOHNSON CORY CALLAHAN, COMMITTEE CHAIR PRENTICE CHANDLER LAURA EDWARDS TRACEY HODGES LISA MATHERSON ELIZABETH WILSON A DISSERTATION Submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of Doctor of Philosophy in the Department of Curriculum and Instruction in the Graduate School of The University of Alabama TUSCALOOSA, ALABAMA 2022 Copyright Julie Kristin Muhlenfeld-Johnson 2022 ALL RIGHTS RESERVED ABSTRACT Social studies teachers represent various political, racial, and ideological perspectives. It remains unclear how social studies teachers choose curricula and balance the racial representation they present and the implications for students' racial lenses and understanding of race in the past and present. This qualitative study will have an impact on scholars as it will contribute to the existing data on the impact of teachers' race, racial lenses and perspectives, and racial pedagogical content knowledge on their curricular choices. The data collection for this study includes three extensive critical phenomenological interviews with nine participants, each reflecting on their biographical information, personal and professional experiences, reflections of their racial lens via journaling, and a document analysis of their curricula. Additionally, a survey of 6th-12th grade social studies teachers was distributed nationwide to collect data supporting the research on teachers' racial perspectives and their curricular decision-making. The findings indicate that while most secondary social studies teachers have progressive or antiracist racial optics, they often do not convey this mentality and positionality in their curricular choices, due to a variety of reasons. Scholars could use the data from this study to examine the influences of race consciousness on social studies classrooms and understand its impact on curricular decision-making as well as the long-term effects on the development and perception of America's narrative. Keywords: secondary social studies; racial optics; white social studies; critical whiteness; curricular decision making; antiracist; phenomenological interview DEDICATION Written and dedicated to my former students for what they taught me and inspired in me, and to my future students for what I hope to teach and inspire in them. | Abbreviation | Description | |--------------|-------------| | BIPOC | Black, Indigenous, and People of Color | | CWT | critical whiteness theory | | CRT | critical race theory | | K-12 | kindergarten through 12th grade education | | NCSS | National Council for the Social Studies | | WSS | white social studies | ACKNOWLEDGMENTS Great accomplishments of any one individual are rarely, if ever, a solitary effort. ........................................... 165 Figure 19 Wyatt’s Curriculum Rubric Score ............................................. 166 Figure 20 Racial Optics of Participants ..................................................... 172 CHAPTER 1 INTRODUCTION The first inauguration of the United States’ first President of Color in 2008 brought on a decade of resurgence and exposure of white supremacy, policy brutality, and viral videos of everyday (re: white) Americans demonstrating overt racism (if even unintentional) against Black Americans\(^1\). Subsequently, civil rights movements like #BlackLivesMatter\(^2\) and #ICantBreathe\(^3\) emerged in response, which then swung the pendulum back and led to the election of President Trump – bringing on further division due to his frequent controversial commentary on issues of race, gender, and nationalism. In 2020, multiple historic events occurred, including the first impeachment of President Donald Trump, the deaths of George Floyd\(^4\) and Breonna Taylor\(^5\). --- \(^1\) The writing guidelines from the *Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association* (7th ed.) indicate that because racial and ethnic groups are proper nouns, they should be capitalized. However, many critical scholars capitalize ‘Black’ and ‘People of Color’ but not ‘white’ to acknowledge the intentional community grouping of People of Color and challenge the supremacy of whiteness in American society and social studies. I stand with these scholars (Allen, 2012a, 2012b; Appiah, 2020; Crenshaw, 1988; Du Bois, 1971; Gibson, 2020; Hawkman, 2019, 2020; Price, 2019; Tanner, 2016) and publishers (AP News, Chicago Tribune, Columbia Journalism Review, Los Angeles Times, NBC News, The New York Times, USA Today, and The Wall Street Journal) and maintain that until Black, Indigenous, and People of Color receive equal treatment as whites politically, socially, economically, and educationally, I will elevate them above whites grammatically in my writing to establish and call attention to their value. I will not capitalize ‘white’ unless it begins a sentence or appears within a direct quotation. \(^2\) The Black Lives Matter organization formed in 2015 in the United States in response to the killing of an unarmed Black teenager Trayvon Martin by a community watch citizen George Zimmerman. The group has gained global recognition for calling attention to the ongoing slaughter of unarmed and undeserving Black people by non-Black Americans without legal repercussion. \(^3\) ‘I can’t breathe’ is a slogan used by Black Lives Matter and commemorate the final words of Eric Garner, an unarmed Black man killed in 2014 by New York City police. \(^4\) Unarmed George Floyd was murdered by Minneapolis officers during his arrest (handcuffed and on the ground). His death sparked global outrage and riots in protest to the ongoing murder of Black people by police. \(^5\) Breonna Taylor was a Black woman who was fatally killed by police in her home during a botched police raid. Her death was protested by the masses along with George Floyd’s during 2020 around the world. (amongst many others) which resulted in violent and nonviolent protests and counterprotests throughout the summer; and a presidential election dramatized by the ongoing and outspoken denial of the validated results by President Trump and many Republicans. These events all occurred during the global COVID-19 pandemic, which caused the deaths of nearly half a million Americans alone within its first nine months. With the country reeling from these events, 2021 began with an angry mob of pro-Trump supporters who broke past police lines and vandalized the United States Capitol on January 6. Inspired by the direct instruction of President Trump at a rally earlier that day, the members of the angry mob called for the lives of Congresspeople and Vice-President Pence\(^6\). The words of the President and the actions of those who stormed the Capitol resulted in the unprecedented second impeachment of a sitting president before he left office two weeks later, and a Congressional investigation in 2022. The commentary he made throughout his presidency on topics such as legal and undocumented immigrants, Black Lives Matter and police brutality, the defense of neo-Nazi groups protesting the removal of Confederate monuments, and Muslim travel bans exposed the continued and ongoing racial tension within the country and revealed how racism exists in nearly every aspect of U.S. society and politics. Whether he is wholly responsible or simply an agent of exposure will not be the topic under examination in this study. Rather, this study will address the social effects of Trump’s presidency – (i.e., his mannerisms, rhetoric, and policies) and its impact on the divisiveness Americans now experience. This polarization is largely based on how Americans see themselves, each other, and their country’s \(^6\) The mob and President Trump placed blame on some Congresspeople and Vice President Pence for upholding Congress’ confirmation of the election results (Beckett, 2021; Givhan, 2021). past and present. These factors have another commonality: U.S. secondary level students learn about them in their social studies education. **Background** To many, the election and reelection of America’s first Black Commander-in-Chief, President Obama, indicated the move toward – or arrival at – post-racial status in the United States (Dawson & Bobo, 2009; Love & Tosolt, 2010). President Obama’s election was a monumental step toward progress; however, the simultaneous ongoing exposure of racism within the country provided evidence against any claims of the United States as post-racial. The #BlackLivesMatter movement began in 2013 in response to the killing of an unarmed minor, Trayvon Martin, and the subsequent acquittal of his murderer. Additional police killings of unarmed Black men, women, and children caused pain and anger in the country to spread to other ongoing racial injustices, such as acknowledging and revering proslavery Confederate soldiers with monuments, flying their flag despite the Confederacy’s secession from and war with the United States, and the whitewashing of U.S. history in social studies (An, 2020; Chandler, 2009; Chandler & Branscombe, 2015; Hawkman, 2019, 2020). The counterresponse to all these actions showed the ongoing existence of white supremacy within the United States. Black, Indigenous, and People of Color (BIPOC) watched as white Americans defended, excused, or dismissed the racism of other white Americans with counterprotests in support of police with #BlueLivesMatter or #AllLivesMatter. *New York Times* journalist and Pulitzer Prize winner Nikole Hannah-Jones led a nonviolent, academic-based response to frame race education by introducing *The 1619 Project* curriculum. *The 1619 Project* provides social studies teachers and students with a complete and accurate historical curriculum for teaching about Black people within the United States starting with their forced enslavement and relocation in 1619. President Trump responded to the curriculum with an Executive Order, tasking *The 1776 Commission* to provide approaches to ‘ensure patriotic education’ funded via the Department of Education (Trump, 2020b). Both BIPOC and white Americans listened as President Trump referred to Nazi-flag-flying white supremacists as ‘very nice people’ as they protested in Charlottesville (Phelps, 2019). Some Americans (many white) considered these actions necessary, legal, and appropriate (O’Neil, 2018). However, many others found the words ‘repugnant’ and ‘horrific’ inadequate for describing these actions. More than 150 years have passed since the liberation of Enslaved People of African Descent; however, white Americans still struggle to not only eradicate their white supremacy but to even accept that it still exists. For this, the field social studies may hold some responsibility. **Problem Statement** The controversy over implementing new curricula, whether *The 1619 Project*, *The 1776 Commission*, or any other – has shown how some materials may be included while others not. President Trump’s public criticism and dismissal of critical race theory and teaching history with multiple perspectives (Trump, 2020a) have contributed to the further polarization of the country and led to state bans and legislation limiting and regulating how teachers present race. Additionally, many secondary social studies teachers struggle to address controversial issues in their classrooms and teach students how to resolve differing opinions (Hawley et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2017; Lintner, 2018). Thus, continued polarization within the country stems from secondary social studies curriculum. Social studies teachers represent a wide variety of political (Clark et al., 2020), racial representation (United States Department of Education, 2019), and ideological perspectives (Pajares, 1992; Richardson, 1996). It remains unclear how social studies teachers choose the curricula they teach in class, balance the racial representation they present, and address the implications they have on their students’ racial lenses and understanding of race in the past and present. Scholars have shared that many teachers – mainly white- regard race as a controversial topic (Gindi & Erlich, 2017; King et al., 2018; Lintner, 2018). Additionally, controversial topics and issues are increasingly not taught in social studies (Alongi et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2017; Lintner, 2018). I posit that the absence of learning how to disagree amicably and intelligently on controversial topics and neglecting to represent BIPOC equally while teaching the United States’ story have contributed to the racial strife collectively experienced in the United States. Secondary social studies teachers, scholars, and students need more awareness of their racial lenses, and the lenses via which they present U.S. history. Such awareness in social studies classrooms could be a means of balancing out rather than contributing to the racial strife in the United States. **Significance of the Study** This study will impact scholarly researchers, as it will contribute to the existing data on the impact of a teacher’s racial lens and racial pedagogical content knowledge on curricular choices. Scholars could use the data from this study to examine the influences of racial lenses on social studies classrooms and understand curricular decision-making. Similarly, policymakers could benefit from this knowledge when reexamining and determining the learning standards for students and teacher education programs. Regardless of the study’s outcomes, social studies teacher education programs must include race analysis and impact. The actual data on students’ understanding of race (Duncan et al., 2020) will be the approach used in this study to inform policymakers of the appropriate and needed learning outcomes for secondary social studies students and preservice teachers. The stakeholders impacted by this study will include practitioners, students, and members of society. Teachers of all subjects could use this study’s findings to become more aware of their racial optics and racial pedagogical content knowledge. This study’s literature review includes the various aspects of the construction and implementation of critical whiteness and how to critically examine personal perceptions and content. Teachers could convey such knowledge directly to their students. Teachers must become aware of critical whiteness theory (CWT) and the phenomenon of white social studies; in turn, students will learn from educators whose critical pedagogies (Giroux, 1992) have undergone appropriate reflection. Students who take on educators’ knowledge base that has undergone critical examination and diversification will apply that lens to their behavior, which will ideally have a ripple effect throughout generations. **Purpose Statement** According to the National Council for the Social Studies (NCSS), social studies educators must teach and shape students’ perceptions to help ‘young people make informed and reasoned decisions for the public good as citizens of a culturally diverse, democratic society in an interdependent world’ (National Council for the Social Studies [NCSS], 2020a, para. 3). However, educators have received their education in a system focused on white normativity. Therefore, they may struggle to even be aware of the racial lens they may subconsciously pass onto their students. Because eighty percent of educators are white (Geiger, 2018), the spread of white normativity in a system where it has not *intentionally* been eradicated is likely (Manfra & Bolick, 2017). Similarly, nearly 77% of the U.S. population is white (United States Census Bureau Quick Facts, 2019), and therefore it is equally likely that white supremacy/white normativity is a phenomenon in which many do not know they participate due to the related phenomenon of white privilege. The teaching of social studies in the United States is the direct means of teaching students the history of BIPOC and their various struggles for equality. Therefore, members of the social studies community must intentionally and systematically eradicate white normativity from curricula and instruction. All social studies teachers need to self-reflect and evaluate their methodology for areas of practice that might contain messages of white supremacy. The results of such self-reflection could be to eliminate white supremacy from the mentality of the country’s white youth. In theory, the eradication of white supremacy can occur as soon as one generation and for all subsequent generations going through the U.S. public education system. The nature of the content area in teaching civic education, U.S. history, and varying cultures requires the social studies community – teachers, curriculum writers, teacher educators, and researchers – to take on this responsibility. This study will be the first step in this work by focusing on how secondary social studies teachers’ racial lenses could contribute to their curricular decision-making. Chapter 1 has provided a context of race relationships within the 21st-century United States. The introduction also indicates the responsibility of secondary social studies educators to teach a racially-balanced perspective of U.S. history. Chapter 2 includes a review of the literature related to the study’s theoretical framework of CWT. The literature review also addresses curricular decision-making. Chapter 3 consists of the methodology of this proposed qualitative phenomenological interview study, followed by the data analysis. Chapters 4, 5 and 6 presents the three major results of this study, and Chapter 7 concludes with a discussion of the findings, their implications and recommendations for future research and action. Definitions It is essential to clarify the specific usage of several terms that describe white people in application of this study. Included here are the varying definitions for the terms *whiteness*, *white normativity*, *whitewashing*, *white supremacy*, and *white superiority*. Because these terms can be easily confused or conflated, it is essential to take note of their similarities and differences, and what differentiates one from the next, as they will each be distinctly used throughout this study. **Whiteness** *Whiteness* indicates a white person’s skin color in relation to racial identity. In 2003, the Human Genome Project indicated that race is not genetic (Chou, 2017). They concluded that skin color is not a biological attribute, thus making race a social construct (Bonham, n.d.; Chou, 2017). *Whiteness* is a frame of mind impacted by white skin on societal standing or worldview. What makes whiteness a unique quality is that white skin does not indicate belonging to a community of people (not to be confused with ethnicity or heritage), unlike communities that are based on skin color or ethnicity such as the Black community, the Latine\(^7\) community, or the Asian community. **White Normativity** *White normativity* presents white skin color as the standard and nonwhite skin color as the ‘other’ or marginal. White normativity occurs either intentionally or unintentionally via --- \(^7\) While the identity naming of Hispanic, Chicano/a/x, and Latino/a/x people is highly nuanced in preference based on the individual, I utilize the term *Latine* throughout this study to convey an overall inclusivity of ancestral and nonbinary designation and identity. The term *Latine* utilizes the Spanish ‘e’ to replace the ‘a’ and ‘o’ to convey gender neutrality. The term *Latinx* has been considered by some to be problematic because it can be seen as ‘language imperialism by imposing a new English word onto a Spanish word.’ (Campos, 2021) as the letter ‘x’ does not ‘honor Spanish pronunciation’ (McGee, 2022). I do entirely support the Latine community to utilize whichever word with which they most identify; but as a white woman, I feel it necessary to remain as inclusive to people of Hispanic and Latin-American decent and reverent to the Spanish language as I am able. people, corporations, policymakers, and others who present those with white skin as the ‘average’ or ‘normal’ visual seen by members of the public. *Whitewashing* is a similar activity of strategically placing white people as the dominant standard. Whitewashing is not new or unique to the United States. For example, whitewashed artwork of Jesus Christ from as early as the sixth century, such as *Christ Pantocrator*, shows Him with pale white skin (Traverso, 2018), a precedent that is often replicated, even today, with the addition of blue eyes. However, Jesus’s birthplace in the Middle East and his Jewish heritage (Swartwood House, 2020) indicate that He was a Person of Color, likely with dark hair, skin and eyes. **Whitewashing** Whitewashing has a long history in the United States via advertising, Hollywood, music, and history. Whitewashing is a tool used to create the false narrative of white normativity. *White normativity* indicates that white people are shown, depicted, credited, and heard more frequently. Therefore, members of society see whiteness as a regular occurrence; anyone nonwhite is, therefore, irregular. Kenneth and Mamie Clark scientifically proved this phenomenon’s existence when they studied Black children in the 1930s and 1940s. They found that Black girls chose white dolls from a selection of dolls ranging in skin color because they believed the white dolls to be pretty and good. Such findings indicated the harmful effects of internalized racism on young children (Patterson, 2001). The Clarks’ study contributed to the *Brown v. Board of Education* case. Sociological, educational, and psychological researchers have widely referred to the study as well. **White Privilege** While the term *white privilege* has reemerged as a cultural buzz phrase, it has deeper roots than McIntosh’s seminal 1989 essay that provided the definition used by current generations\(^8\). Du Bois (1935) described the ‘psychological wage’ (p. 597) that poor white people experienced higher social status than Black people. Du Bois (1935) contended that the benefit of their whiteness required the devaluation of Black people, that the universe belonged to white people, and that to be white is to benefit. There is a need to recognize that although the word *privilege* is part of this term, it by no means indicates that white people do not struggle or experience financial, legal, or social hardship. Rather, *privilege* indicates that white skin correlates with normativity or general ease over nonwhite skin in the United States and global society and refers to the default benefit of white skin over that of nonwhite skin. *White privilege* indicates that white skin has the advantage over nonwhite. This is why the Clarks’ study was significant, because it showed that even little girls felt that to choose white skin means a certain benefit or ease within society. This ease could take place during traffic stops, easily finding hair care products in the store, a person’s success not being considered a ‘credit to their kind,’ or receiving immediate suspicion when shopping in expensive stores or applying for loans (McIntosh, 1989). Acknowledging one’s white privilege is essential to be racially conscious and a step toward anti-racism, a topic presented later in this chapter. **White Superiority** While the concept of white supremacy has specific connotations and historical imagery, *white superiority* is not white supremacy. The goal of white superiority is not to eradicate all People of Color or a works towards a white-only world. For this study, the operational definition of white superiority is the mindset in which white people exist at the top of a false and socially- --- \(^8\) I have included a portion of McIntosh’s essay in Appendix F which lists various criteria for identifying white privilege. constructed racial hierarchy. The hierarchy knowingly or unknowingly presents white people as the default, normal, and standard, with all other racial groups less than, nonnormative, and nonstandard. The construction of this definition for this study occurred based on several scholarly works (Bell, 1987, 1988; Chandler, 2009; Kendi, 2019; Moroney, 2018). Hierarchy is the element that differentiates white superiority from white normativity (Bhandaru, 2013; Ward, 2008). Recognizing personal bias via the lens of white superiority is an essential step toward antiracism and balancing the whitewashed version of history that many Americans accept as the entire truth. **White Supremacy** White supremacy, with its robust history, reemerged on center stage when the white successor of the United States’ first Black president supported and defended the white supremacists and Confederates who fought in favor of slavery (Phelps, 2019). *White supremacy* is the belief in the hierarchical dominance of whiteness above all other races, *and* that whites must manifest their dominance socially, economically, and politically. The presentation of white supremacy can be subtle, and recognizing it requires looking for intention and meaning in more than overt signs such as Nazi flags and white hoods\(^9\). *White supremacy* can also be actively and overtly committing acts or expressions of bigotry and hate against nonwhite people. Like white supremacy, racism is an intentional and explicit phenomenon, however, research has indicated that racism and racial bias can also be implicit and subconscious phenomena (Project Implicit, 1998; Quillian, 2008; Warren, 2020). --- \(^9\) The white supremacy organization Klu Klux Klan (KKK) famously dons white hoods and robes for their meetings and when committing hate crimes against People of Color. The generations who remember Klu Klux Klan cross-burnings or Nazism in the 1940s have specific notions of white supremacy. However, society has reappropriated white supremacy via subconscious thought and unexamined intent (Saad, 2020). White supremacy is the mentality that to be non-white is to be problematic or the more subtle notion of an *us versus them* in society. White supremacy is to set apart – and to elevate -- oneself *because of* their skin tone. The subtly exists in the process of thought versus action; simply believing these concepts without the intention of speaking them out loud can still result in subconscious display (Project Implicit, 1998). Scholars have developed several continuums to measure and evaluate whiteness and racism. Amongst the most popular ones are *The 8 White Identities* by Barnor Hesse of Northwestern University (Appendix A); *Spectrum: From Non-Racist to Anti-Racist Advocate* adapted from works by James and Hodson (Appendix B), and the Stuart Center’s *Racism Scale* (Appendix C). No scale is a means of conclusively and absolutely determining a person’s level of racism or acknowledgment of their privilege. However, using scales enables people to become more informed and reflective of positionality. **Research Questions** The study has four guiding research questions: 1. How have current secondary U.S. history teachers experienced learning about race? 2. How do current high school U.S. history teachers interpret their own racial optics? 3. How have these U.S. history teachers included or omitted their racial optics from their curriculum? 4. How have these teachers been guided on decentralizing the white narrative from American history or including diversity in their curriculum, if at all? CHAPTER 2 REVIEW OF LITERATURE Understanding this study’s framework will require considering the rationale and purpose for social studies education in the United States in the past and present. Prior to examining the literature that informs this study’s theoretical framework and objective, it is essential to first understand the context of learning about United States history in secondary social studies classrooms. Historical Background of Social Studies Education In its rawest and most original form, social studies was one of the primary and most important subjects for school children at the country’s founding, even before the origin of the concept of ‘social studies.’ The government’s success after its newly earned independence required training citizens to (a) become strong and competent leaders and (b) be loyal to the nation to avoid a similar rebellion like that which just resulted in the nation’s birth (Green, 1990; Urban & Wagoner, 2014). Framers, including Benjamin Franklin, Thomas Jefferson, and Benjamin Rush, desired young citizens and would-be leaders of the democracy to have a good grasp of history, citizenship, morality, economy, geography, and government, particularly concerning the text familiar to all Americans: The Constitution. Although formalized public education was originally a benefit intended for white males, there were instances of common and special schools for the education (but not the leadership training) of minority groups like females, People with Disabilities, and BIPOC (Urban & Wagoner, 2014). The formalization of social studies as a content area occurred in the early 20th century via the National Education Association Commission on the Reorganization of the Secondary School and their 1916 Report of the Committee on Social Studies (Nelson, 2001). The 1916 report indicated that the purpose of social studies was the ‘cultivation of good citizenship’ (Dunn, 1916, p. 9). The creation of the National Council for the Social Studies (NCSS) in 1921 provided a curricular-instructional blueprint for social studies that the 1916 report did not (Thornton, 2017). NCSS officials spent the following decades molding social studies into more than just history education (Nelson, 2001). However, some scholars continued to advocate distinguishing between learning history and geography and learning social studies as a grouping of various subjects that all contribute to citizenship education. This battle continues into modern research and philosophy (Evans, 2006). Evans (2006) described subsequent movements in his book *The Social Studies Wars*, identifying five major camps in the social studies arena: - traditional historians: supported history at the core of social studies; popularized in the 1890s - social scientists: supported teaching the social science disciplines; popularized in the 1960s - social efficiency educators: support standardized techniques from business and industry to schooling and a scientifically constructed approach aimed at preparing students for various life roles - social meliorists: support reflective/issues-centered curriculum focused on social problems; popularized by Dewey in the first half of the 20th century • social reconstructionists/critical pedagogues: support social studies in schools to have a direct impact on the transformation of U.S. society Ultimately, Evans described the social studies wars as a reflection of the cultural and political divides in the United States, which have been long-term trends and not easily rectified. Regardless of the preferred approach or outcomes, there is a need to consider the contributions of theorists regarding the purpose and method of social studies. In the book *Democracy and Education*, Dewey (1916/2011) discussed the function and necessity of social studies education, specifically history and geography. Dewey indicated the need to move from studying facts and names to making connections between the past and the present: History deals with the past, but this past is the history of the present. An intelligent study of the discovery, explorations, colonization of America, of the pioneer movement westward, of immigration, etc., should be a study of the United States as it is today: of the country we now live in. (Dewey, 1916/2011, p.118) This stance enabled 20th- and even 21st-century social studies advocates to engage in rote memorization and connect historical events and their present-day implications. The development and increasing value of formal public education occurred throughout the 20th century. At that time, social studies aimed to educate and foster students who would sign up for military service, work for U.S. ingenuity and superiority during the Cold War and participate in civic activism for political and legal change during the civil rights movement. In addition, an increasing need to remain at the forefront of the world’s stage led to the educational era of standardized testing and results-based learning. The catalyst of the era of standardized testing and results-based learning was *A Nation at Risk*, a report by the Reagan administration that focused on the rising tide of mediocrity in U.S. education (Maranto, 2015; Urban & Wagoner, 2014). As a result of the report, scholars, policymakers, and educators wanted to ensure and validate the greatness of the United States with data and sought test scores as proof of America’s superiority (Maranto, 2015; Urban & Wagoner, 2014). The 1990s standards movement resulted in changes in history and social studies teaching. For most of the 20th century, school and district leaders could choose curricula. Social studies curricula could focus on single subjects like history and geography for civics education. Alternately, the curriculum could have a more comprehensive approach with social studies as an all-encompassing topic for exploring civics regarding U.S. history, world history, economics, sociology, geography, and political science (Saxe, 2004). In 1989, leaders at the Education Summit in Charlottesville approved the Goals 2000 Act, in part due to the urgent call of policymakers to improve the effectiveness of public education. The call for effectiveness included a request for definitive content for history and geography. President Clinton signed the Goals 2000 Act into law in 1994, the same year the NCSS published its independent content standards. Congress later authorized $250 million for teaching ‘traditional’ U.S. history, not social studies (Saxe, 2004). At the same time, citizenship curricula began to focus on the independent subjects of history, geography, civics, government, and economics. Such changes resulted in a continued debate in the field. At the end of the 20th century, the U.S. version of social studies inspired an ongoing discussion of the most effective approaches to teaching and learning content. Although the debate had an academic nature, several scholars dubbed the conversation *social studies wars* (Barton & Levstik, 2004; Evans, 2006; Thornton, 2017). Nelson argued that social studies at the end of the 20th century and beginning of the 21st century had the responsibility of ‘liberating students from ignorance on social topics and developing critical thinking for making social decisions,’ (p.30). Currently, no set curriculum or instructional methods are established for social studies across the United States. Districts and states – rather than a federal mandate – guide teachers on how to teach social studies. Such variation has had unintentional but profound effects on students in different states and regions: students across the country learn varied materials, content, and perspectives of history and democratic citizenship. **Purpose of Social Studies Education** The NCSS and social studies scholars have provided the current definition for social studies education. The NCSS bridges social studies scholars to schools, teachers, and students and is the driving force behind most states’ social studies content standards. In addition, the NCSS is the only active specialized professional association for social studies that provides the university requirements for accrediting teacher education programs (Council for the Accreditation of Educator Preparation, n.d.). Therefore, the NCSS determines the current purpose for social studies as far as social studies educators are concerned. The NCSS (n.d.-a) indicates that the purpose of social studies is ‘to help young people develop the ability to make informed and reasoned decisions for the public good as citizens of a culturally diverse, democratic society in an interdependent world’ (para 3). In addition, their College, Career, and Civic Life (C3) Framework indicates that the goal of social studies is to prepare students for their postsecondary futures by developing their abilities to think critically, apply concepts, evaluate sources, communicate conclusions, and take informed action (NCSS, 2016). NCSS further presented powerful teaching and learning as integrative, value-based (i.e., committed to justice, equality, and freedom), challenging, and active. Countless scholars and researchers have informed the NCSS and social studies field. Scholars have varying opinions and research on the purpose and method of teaching and learning social studies. The values of 21\textsuperscript{st}-century social studies education include tolerance, human rights, social justice, and democracy so that students can live and eventually work in a diverse society (Johnson & Morris, 2010). Barton and Levstik (2004) contended that because a goal of social studies is preparation for democracy, social studies teachers must instruct and allow students to engage in the historical inquiry of meaningful content while examining a variety of resources and viewpoints so that the students can make conclusions substantiated with their research, instead of reproducing and imparting a ‘consensual narrative of the national past’ (p.260). Because countless references exist for the scholarly work that has contributed to the purpose of social studies, particularly in the 21\textsuperscript{st} century, the following sections present only a selection of those references. Social studies has three components for guiding students in becoming effective and successful humans: democratic citizenship, global citizenship, and the skills needed to implement both and interact in the 21\textsuperscript{st} century. **Democratic Citizenship.** As with any type of government, living in a democracy has needs that its citizens must fulfill and perpetuate to foster the growth and success of society. Democratic citizenship, often synonymous with civics, presents the principles for what citizens and residents should do and believe for the functioning of the country. Ho and Barton (2020) posited that the purpose of schools is to prepare students for ‘civic action by developing the knowledge, skills, and commitments necessary to participate in the public life of a democracy, particularly as they work toward goals of social justice’ (p. 1). Principles, such as participation (e.g., military, voting, jury duty), having shared values (e.g., justice and freedom), and abiding by the law have benefits for democracy. Effectively teaching these principles requires teachers to embody and model them. Any citizenship includes the embedded fields of national history, economy, society, geography, and government. Democratic citizenship may focus on civics education, yet it does not necessarily result in excluding the aforementioned areas. Instead, democratic citizenship includes these areas to provide students with a full scope of understanding of their country and government. The most prominent and recent feature of social studies is an emphasis on diversity and inclusion. Thornton (2017) called for scholars to advance historical research on previously ignored groups, such as LGBTQ+ and English Language Learners (ELLs). Ladson-Billings (1995, 2001) looked at diversity-focused teaching and culturally relevant pedagogy. Cuenca (2017) worked with the NCSS, updating standards to guide students to be ‘active, informed, and justice-oriented citizens’ (p. 371). Scholars have suggested implementing antiracist curricula at schools for both social studies students and teachers (Darling-Hammond, 2017; Hawkman, 2020; Kendi, 2019). **Global Citizenship.** The technological advancements of the 21st century enable people worldwide to connect and learn about others’ realities and hardships. Therefore, social studies has begun focusing on global citizenship. Global citizenship requires students to examine and appreciate the diverse types of people around the world, acknowledge their multiple perspectives, honor their diversity, and support their right to social justice and fundamental freedoms as if they were members of students own race, ethnicity, or nationality. It indicates that everyone is a member of humanity. Twenty-five percent of children under 18 in the United States have parents who were not born in the United States (Byker, 2019). Therefore, students must become educated on the situations of others around the world. Like democratic citizenship, learning global citizenship includes history, economy, society/culture, geography, and government. Myers (2006) argued that teachers should consider global citizenship education as a layer rather than an alternative for developing democratic citizenship. Myers emphasized that some may see the combination of democratic and global citizenship as controversial, particularly in the United States, where schooling focuses on learning about national identity rather than the world. However, learning about national identity and the world both have value, as the knowledge about one contributes to an understanding and appreciation of the other. **Skills Development.** Building and applying content knowledge requires developing skills. Twenty-first-century social studies skills include an array of proficiencies, such as inquiry, critical thinking, and literacy. Teaching students to think historically, having historical literacy (Wineburg, 2007; Wineburg & Martin, 2004), and learning to think analytically and critically of texts (Ravitch, 2003) are synonymous with social studies education. Research, problem-based learning, and project-based learning are common forms of inquiry within this field and others. Teachers implement social studies skills depending on their knowledge and ability. Therefore, district and state mandates must provide 21st-century educators with the ability to implement open-ended learning environments (Saye, 2002) and use authentic pedagogy. Scholars found that authentic pedagogy correlated with improved student engagement and test scores (Saye & Social Studies Inquiry Research Collaborative [SSIRC], 2014). **Theoretical Framework: Critical whiteness Theory** Critical whiteness theory and its subsets of critical whiteness pedagogy and critical whiteness studies originated from critical race theory (Bell, 1987; Crenshaw et al., 1995; Ladson-Billings & Tate, 1995). Critical race theory (CRT) indicates that every aspect of U.S. society, politics, and economy contains racism.
