CWT is an extension of CRT, as it is a lens by which people see the validation and success of white people that occurs because of the social construct of their whiteness while (intentionally and/or unintentionally) systematically and consistently oppressing BIPOC. *Critical whiteness pedagogy* focuses on the impact of whiteness in education and its implications for both whites (positively impacted by whiteness in education) and BIPOC (negatively impacted by whiteness in education)\(^{10}\). *Critical whiteness studies* examines how whiteness is a privileged and socially dominant construct that has contributed to the intentional or unintentional supremacy of white people (Grayman-Simpson et al., 2019). *Critical whiteness theory* requires a meticulous and candid examination of how white people have benefitted from their whiteness and how it is their responsibility to acknowledge and combat the random privilege they have received due to their skin color. CWT has been a topic of discussion within U.S. society over the last decade, although many are unaware of its meaning. White and BIPOC educators, policymakers, and scholars have indicated the need to reexamine how educators and students analyze and present history. This need for reexamination has been a topic frequently studied and manifested in the national news (Aggarwal-Schifellite, 2020; Duhe, 2018; Forest & Johnson, 2019). Around the world, Americans and non-Americans have protested in favor of reframing history with a critical lens. Reframing advocates have also called for the relocation and destruction of monuments erected to memorialize colonizers’ white superiority and dominance (Rankin & Crary, 2020; Silva, 2020). In contrast, those wanting to maintain the relics in honor of white supremacists have voiced concerns about ‘erasing history’ (Joyner, 2020; Newsome, 2017). However, an increasing number of white people have amplified the voices of the marginalized to stand in solidarity with \(^{10}\) For a description of the goals of critical whiteness pedagogy, see D. Nichols (2010), ‘Teaching Critical Whiteness Theory: What College and University Teachers Need to Know, *Understanding and Dismantling Privilege*, 1(1), 1–12. those whom they acknowledge as having withstood oppression for generations, particularly in higher education and research (Cheung et al., 2019; Gardner & Alanis, 2020; Gibson & Hughes-Hassell, 2017). This type of behavior has been categorized as *white traitor* by Barnor Hesse, a Northwestern University professor of African American Studies who’s *Eight White Identities* went viral, largely for the use of the aforementioned term (Sargent & Rico, 2021a). While *white traitor* is cited to be negative in connotation, Hesse (Sargent & Rico, 2021a, 2021b) contended that to be a white traitor simply means to vocalize and fight for the best interest of non-white people, which essentially is antiracism. For racial harmony to occur between the historically white-dominant race and the people they have categorically marginalized and oppressed, it is essential to reframe the collective worldview to include multiple perspectives and counternarratives (Nganga et al., 2019) of BIPOC, not to mention other marginalized groups (e.g., the queer community, groups in poverty, and individuals with disabilities). Reframing history unavoidably results in CWT and the reexamination of how white people have dominated, colonized, enslaved, and marginalized BIPOC worldwide and over time. CWT requires white people to reflect on their worldviews intentionally and candidly, assessing how they have explicitly or implicitly benefitted from their whiteness while simultaneously intentionally or unintentionally marginalized others (Collins, 2018; McIntosh, 1989). CWT emphasizes the need for a deliberate and thorough examination of white privilege and superiority complex. Thus, CWT is an essential framework for this study on the impact of a teacher’s view of race on curricular decision-making. **Toward a Critical Lens** Inevitably, examining and critiquing whiteness can cause individuals to question how they can overcome their white superiority, white normativity, or white supremacy. Such individuals often question, *Am I inherently racist because I am white?* or, *Can I, as a white person, ever be naturally unbiased?* However, as Applebaum (2016) pointed out in defining critical whiteness, the point is not the goodness or badness of a person; instead, it is to acknowledge one’s privilege and discriminatory practices and work against them. As Kendi (2019) described in *How to Be an Anti-Racist*, ‘The most threatening racist movement is not the alt-rights unlikely drive for a White ethnostate but the regular American’s drive for a ‘race-neutral’ one’ (p. 19). People who acknowledge their privilege and critically examine their perspectives of race often commit to championing the marginalized to raise awareness of their oppression. They also often work to dismantle personal prejudices and point out prejudices around them. As a result, such individuals are often considered allies of oppressed communities. According to Pizzo (n.d.), allyship requires several actions: - to amplify the voices of the oppressed ahead of one’s own - to transfer personal benefits of privilege to those denied privilege - to acknowledge that while they may sympathize, they cannot empathize - to take responsibility for acquired knowledge rather than depending on the marginalized to justify or explain themselves - to listen, validate, and acknowledge the pain and experiences of the oppressed A white person can become an ally by operatively displaying the belief that race is a false social construct that must be dismantled to impart equal and just benefits to all human beings (Smith & Crowley, 2018). Allyship should be a common goal of white people to work against their inherent privilege and bias. However, white people cannot determine their level of allyship for themselves. The increased awareness of allyship and white privilege has resulted in a subsequent increase in white people proclaiming themselves allies on social media, although they may not have done any physical or educational work to protect or elevate People of Color. Consequently, Scholars of Color and journalists have called out to correct white people who claim to be allies but have not shown action (Hilaire, 2020; Owens, 2020), as have other white people (Animus, 2015; Rosen, 2017). Calling out those claiming to be allies aims to prevent a sense of white saviors and pride. Those who have historically experienced racial oppression have the right to acknowledge the people they trust and with whom they feel safe. The members of a dominant majority have no right to claim themselves as helpful to an oppressed minority without acknowledging the members of that oppressed minority. White people on the road to becoming allies must see *ally* as a verb, not a noun. They must follow certain behaviors and mentalities: always focus on the impacted, listen and learn from the oppressed, leverage privilege, and yield the floor to create space for the voices of the marginalized (Rosen, 2017). Saying otherwise contributes to privilege. **Applying Critical whiteness Theory To Racial Perspectives** Grasping the impact of CWT on teachers’ curricular decision-making requires understanding teachers’ racial lens, racial consciousness, racial subconsciousness, and racial unconsciousness, which I will refer to hereafter as racial (sub/un)consciousness. These five categories – racial lens, racial identity, and racial (sub/un)consciousness, I collectively refer to this as racial optics, outlined in Figure 1, and further explored in Chapter 5. However, it is first necessary to understand the various nuances that make up racial optics. Afterward, one can apply those concepts to the data regarding what and how a teacher chooses to present a particular curriculum. **Racial Identity and Racial Lens** One area of social studies teaching that is becoming less ambiguous as the 21\textsuperscript{st} century progresses is the perceptions and implications of race-conscious teaching. Teachers must understand their identities, beliefs, and goals as educators to develop their effectiveness and positively impact student learning (Noonan, 2019). This section focuses on teachers’ understanding of racial identity and their race lens as it pertains to themselves, their students, and society. Because race is widely regarded as a social construct, recognizing racial identity depends on how one perceives themselves and how others perceive them regarding their race (National Museum of African American History & Culture, n.d.). The term *racial lens* can be ambiguous, though it is straightforward. The National Institute of Health (NIH) defines *critical race lens* as how the ‘dominant culture perceives different races and ethnicities, how those perceptions are presented in the media and how those factors affect the way society treats people and their opportunities in life’ (National Institute of Health, n.d.). While *racial equity lens* is a much more common term, *racial lens* differs because it is not necessarily specific to issues of equity. To see with a racial lens means to perceive the world as it is viewed through one’s race. This means that the individual would need to know their racial identity – which race they perceive themselves to be categorized. The history of how one identifies racially – particularly for Black Americans – is extensive (Kendi & Blain, 2021), as there is a history of legal precedence for how one may identify themselves if they are of mixed race. For example, Barack Obama is the offspring of a Black man and a white woman, but he is identified by others and himself as a Black man, not a white man (Citrin et al., 2014). While this distinction is no longer legally required, this difference is significant because one’s identity informs their worldview and vice versa (de Witt, 2016). Because he perceives himself as a Black man and because others relate to him as a Black person, he presumably views the world and others through the experiences of a Black man, not a white man, even though his biological mother was white. It is important to note that identifying one’s race is very personal and subjective and can be complex (National Museum of African American History & Culture, n.d.). For some, a person of one nationality may have a similar skin color to a person of a different nationality, which is why the United States Census Bureau does not list Hispanic or Latine as a race but rather an ethnicity. In addition, it has become more common for people whose race may confuse others to include a caveat in their racial identity. For example, people whose outward appearance may be similar to a race/ethnicity which is not a part of their ancestry may clarify their race to others by using phrasing such as: *as a white-presenting Hispanic male*, or *I am an Asian-presenting Native American*. This distinction is significant because it speaks to the experiences of that particular person, who may have previously been mistaken for the wrong race. It is also important to note that people of mixed-racial background can identify themselves as they choose, based on any number of factors such as the culture that was dominant and/or influential in their upbringing, the racial groups they most resemble, or the groupings with which they most feel comfortable and self-identify (Parker et al., 2015; U.S. Census Bureau, n.d.). It is necessary to point out that racial identity, while personal and subjective, must remain consistent with truth. It is socially acceptable for people of multiple racial ancestries to choose their racial identity (such as Barack Obama). However, it is socially unacceptable for a person of one race to identify as anything other than that race, such as Dr. Rachel Dolezal. She became infamous as a white college professor who was fired from her position because she presented and identified herself as a Black woman, despite having no People of Color in her family line, (Johnson et al., 2015). Racial identity is also political. The United States classifies people into five racial groups: Asian, Black, Indigenous/Native Alaskan, Pacific-Islander/Native Hawaiian, and white. As mentioned, Hispanic/Latine are considered ethnicities since their varying subgroups reflect a wide variety of skin colors ranging from white to Black, and the cultural origins range from European to South American. There is a history of controversy over these five categorizations since the construct of race is considered social rather than biological (Bonham, n.d.; Chou, 2017), but also the wide variety and differences between people who are lumped into these categories. For example, people from Pakistan, while classified as Asian, have considerable physical, cultural, ethnic, and historical differences from others from China or Japan who are also classified as Asian. A 2019 Pew Research poll on how Americans perceive race produced interesting statistics on white people’s awareness of their race and its impact on their worldviews as opposed to People of Color. Seventy-four percent of Black people indicated their race as extremely important or very important to their identity, along with 59% of Hispanics and 56% of Asians. However, only 15% of white people found their race important to their identity (Pew Research Center, 2019). This statistic may or may not be a surprise. However, it shows the difference between how People of Color perceive the world concerning their race versus white people. Racial lens and identity are significant in the classroom. Studies show that Black students’ racial identity will impact their achievement and behavior in school (Carter, 2005, 2008; Sellers, Chavous et al., 1998; Sellers, Smith et al., 1998). Kohli (2008) revealed the importance and impact of teachers’ racial lenses on their classrooms. Kohli wrote about a ‘…Chicana from the Los Angeles area described an experience she had in her high school chemistry course. Her White teacher outwardly made racist comments that revealed low expectations and a lack of respect for his students and Latinas/os generally’ (Kohli, 2008, p. 183). While not many people openly admit to racism, the aforementioned example demonstrates how a teacher’s racial perspective can influence their students. Therefore, this study will focus on teachers’ racial lenses and how they see race to evaluate the decisions they make in their classrooms. **Racial Consciousness** Racial consciousness is comparable to racial lens and identity, yet distinctly acknowledged and studied by scholars (Carter, 2005, 2008; Pitts, 1974). Pitts (1974) defined race consciousness as ‘behavior indicating the actor’s interpretation of the situation as representing invidious relationship, even struggle, between the races’ (p. 669). Carter (2005) expanded upon racial consciousness with a critical lens and defined critical race consciousness as ‘a critical understanding of the asymmetrical power relationships that exist between Blacks and Whites in America’ (p. 102). Teachers with racial consciousness must be grounded in their racial identities and remain cognizant of their racial lenses. They must also acknowledge and accept the relationship between BIPOC and white people as unjust and unequal. **Woke.** Racial consciousness is synonymous with the modern term woke. This term originated in the 1940s in *Negro Digest* and was used throughout the late-20th century and into the 21st when Black singer Erykah Badu included the term in her song *Master Teacher* (Mirzaei, 2021). In addition, Black Lives Matter used the phrase to call attention to racial injustice after the Michael Brown murder. To *be woke* or *stay woke*, as Merriam Dictionary defines it, is to be ‘aware of and actively attentive to important facts and issues (especially issues of racial and social justice)’ (Merriam-Webster, n.d.). **Antiracist.** This study uses the operational definition of antiracism as the active and intentional outright opposition to any ideology, wording, or action in which race is a rationale for intentionally or unintentionally omitting, ignoring, degrading, or devaluing human beings. This definition originated from multiple source experts on the word (Center for the Study of Social Policy, n.d.; Kendi, 2019; National Museum of African American History & Culture, n.d.). Kendi (2019) further articulated that antiracism ‘requires persistent self-awareness, constant self-criticism, and regular self-examination’ (p. 22). While many believe the opposite of *racist* is *not-racist*, is important to note that the opposite of *racism* is actually *antiracism*, meaning the intentional and active rejection of racism (Kendi, 2019) as opposed to the *not-racist* mentality which is often associated with ignorance, compliance, and silence. A 2017 article focused on five urban schools that provided services for predominantly Black students. The longitudinal mixed methods study examined how educators can teach Students of Color to be racially conscious in oppressive societal conditions to achieve academic success. El-Amin et al. (2017) observed that ‘schools seeking to increase black students’ academic achievement must directly address these relevant social forces,’ (para. 5). Ultimately, educators must teach their students to enact a Freirean critical consciousness of race (Freire, 1970) and see, name, and challenge racial oppression to augment student achievement and success (El-Amin et al., 2017). **Racial Unconsciousness** Racial consciousness cannot be examined without positing its antithesis – *racial unconsciousness*. If being racially conscious is choosing to be woke, then I posit that being racially unconscious is to *choose* not to be woke, to be obstinate, or apathetic. Scholars have conducted an abundance of research on racial apathy and framed it as a major focus for education researchers (Banks, 2019; Bell, 2002; Crowley & Smith, 2015; Forman & Lewis, 2015; Hawkman, 2020; Sleeter, 2016; Smith & Crowley, 2014). Based on a cumulative reading of these works, *racial apathy* is a chosen perception and practice of minimizing the value and importance of race as having significance or impact. It is a choice made by people in how they see the race of others to avoid conflict or discomfort. Many people believe that identifying themselves as colorblind or choosing not to ‘see race’ conveys to others that they are ‘not racist’ (Bell, 2002; Collins, 2018; Crowley & Smith, 2015; Haviland, 2008; Hawkman, 2020; McIntosh, 1989; Picower, 2009; Sleeter, 2016; Smith & Crowley, 2014). However, as scholars have pointed out, dismissing or ignoring people’s racial identity causes degradation and harm, as the recipients often perceive such behavior as outright ignorance of their identities, experiences, and worldviews. Regardless of its intentionality, colorblindness does not acknowledge People of Color and the oppression they have experienced at the hands of white people *because of* their skin color (Applebaum, 2010). Many studies have focused on teacher education programs and found that the faculty members of these programs must discuss racial apathy and the impact of whiteness on students (Picower, 2009; Sleeter, 2016). Stereo-Racial Apathy. Stereo-racial apathy is a mannerism even more degrading and harmful to BIPOC. Hagerman (2018) defined stereo-racial apathy as not caring about racism or its perpetuation. She identified President Trump as a leading example of young people legitimizing racism by not actively fighting against it. In an interview, Hagerman (Banks, 2019) observed, ‘not caring about racism is perpetuating racism’ (min. 21:45). Media outlets and civilians nationwide have engaged in stereo-racial apathy by defending and supporting President Trump despite his racist and sexist remarks. Another example of stereo-racial apathy is the continued display of the Confederate flag and monuments despite well-publicized calls for their removal because they indicate support for racism. Stereo-racial apathy consists of acknowledging racism but disregarding it as harmful or an area of concern for humanity. Americans have varied opinions about race, antiracism, and racial apathy, and the various opinions indicate that the topic of race and racism is far from resolution (Forman & Lewis, 2015). However, evidence has shown that effective and productive classroom learning occurs when teachers have learned how to address racial issues in their methods classes (Haviland, 2008). Because of the nature of the content area, secondary social studies educators have many opportunities to discuss historical and contemporary racism with their students. However, it is ‘a task they cannot undertake without an awareness of the implications of their own White racial identities’ (Smith & Crowley, 2014, p. 18). Racial Subconsciousness If being racially conscious means to be aware of the asymmetrical power struggle between races, and being racially unconscious is a willful rejection or ignorance of racial oppression, there indeed exists a mid-point between these two ends of the spectrum. I posit this mid-point is racial subconscious - meaning to be unaware of said power struggles or holding unintentional bias. I contend intentionality is the defining force between evaluating one’s racial subconscious versus knowingly holding racist opinions. Racial subconsciousness or being unaware of racism and/or racial injustice occurs when one’s positionality prevents one from understanding a reality outside their lens. **Not-Racist.** Kendi (2019) bluntly stated that ‘not-racist’ is a ‘mask for racism because there is no in-between’ (p. 9). King (2016) identified non-racism to be the ‘passive rejection, opposition and disassociation from behaviors, discourses, and ideologies that are considered racist’ (p. 63). Conversely, antiracism is a ‘critical awareness of…and…an active process against racism that seeks to understand how racial ideology is manufactured and how it impacts the lived experiences of people daily’ (King, 2016, p. 63). To be not-racist is not enough; fighting racism requires one to become an antiracist (Kendi, 2019). **Colorblind.** The term colorblind has come to take on a negative connotation in recent years - justifiably - partly because Authors of Color are raising awareness (Kendi, 2020; Wingfield, 2015; Williams, 2011) about the damage this mentality has on people. Nevertheless, it is vital to acknowledge that the intent behind the phrase invokes the mentality of treating all people as human beings regardless of color (Perey, 2011) though it misses the mark. It is this same intentionality people still want to express while at the same time not being dismissive of the uniqueness all cultures and races have in humanity. Undeniably, whiteness has been normalized for centuries, partly because of race-based social structures without scientific or realistic grounding (Kendi, 2016; 2019; Kendi & Blain, 2021). Racial consciousness is not an absolute determination because humans are flawed and ever-changing. There are instances where a person may have an opinion reflecting racial consciousness and then make a separate statement revealing their racial subconscious. As Kendi discussed, being an antiracist is partly to acknowledge existing implicit bias. There are also varying degrees within each. For example, one could be racially conscious but not have marched in a BLM protest. One could be aware of their position on removing Confederate monuments but be unaware of critical race theory legislation. The level at which someone is racially conscious is not the aim of this study but instead to determine if there are occurrences for each participant demonstrating a racial (sub/un)consciousness. Table 1 offers a breakdown of the nuances of racial identity, racial lens and racial (sub/un)consciousness, which collectively comprise one’s racial optics. Similarly, Figure 2 gives a visual of how racial subconsciousness, racial unconsciousness and racial consciousness combine to encompass racial (sub/un)consciousness. **Applying Critical whiteness Theory to Social Studies** An innate starting point of this vital work exists within the education system, where all Americans and noncitizen residents can access knowledge and experience unification through teaching. An abundance of evidence has shown the existence of systemic racism in education (Ladson-Billings, 1998; Ladson-Billings & Tate, 1995; Navarro & Howard, 2017; Patterson, 2001; Urban & Wagoner, 2014). Students of Color continue to struggle due to residual and ongoing racism within the educational system (Patterson, 2001; Riddle & Sinclair, 2019). Thus, I posit a need exists to examine how educators in the 21st century still contribute to the educational oppression of People of Color, if even unintentionally or unknowingly. Numerous scholars have researched the impact of teachers’ beliefs and practices on student performance (Behar-Horenstein et al., 1996; Schmid, 2018; Turner et al., 2009). In addition, several researchers have examined how teachers can effectively adopt and implement antiracist curricula to promote critical whiteness and fight against white normativity (Chandler, 2015; Hawkman, 2019; King & Chandler, 2015). Others have explored the impact of race in the classroom, with varying combinations of Teachers of Color and their students or Students of Color and their teachers (Dee, 2005; Egalite et al., 2015; Gladwell, 2021; Shuls, 2017). This research has contributed to a vast body of knowledge on the impact of whiteness on education. Nevertheless, for all the progress, there is more work needed to completely dismantle white dominance from... Table 1 Racial Optics Defined | Racial Identity | Explained as... The racial classification one sees themselves as | Based on... Race as a social construct | Sounds like... | |-----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------| | | | | ‘I’m Latine’ | | | | | ‘My parents were born in Thailand; I identify as Asian American.’ | | | | | ‘My mom is Black, my dad is Hispanic, but I connect more with the Black community – I’m Black.’ | | | | | ‘My family’s heritage is from Italy, France, Germany, and Norway, and my skin is white. So I just say I am American.’ | | Racial Lens | How one perceives the world based on their race | One’s particular racial identity | ‘Because I was raised by a Mexican family and I’m Mexican, I most easily understand how Latin see the world.’ | | | | | ‘Even though I have some Indigenous ancestry as well, I look and identify as Asian, and see the world as an Asian person.’ | | Racially | The extent that one acknowledges asymmetrical power | Varying levels of awareness of race | ‘As a white person, I have spent a lot of time listening to my Friends of Color and I can see how my privilege is random and unearned.’ | | Conscious | relationships between races | | ‘Even though I’m Indian, I know my Cuban friends struggled as immigrants in the United States in different ways that I did.’ | | Racially | The extent that one is unaware of asymmetrical power | Varying levels of unawareness of race | ‘I don’t think Latine people have had it as hard as Black people in the United States.’ | | Subconscious | relationships between races | | ‘I try not to see color.’ | | | | | ‘Slavery ended a long time ago. I had nothing to do with it.’ | | | | | ‘I’m a good person – I’m not racist.’ | | Racially | The extent that one willingly or unknowingly does not | One of two pathways: the refusal to | Examples of a) | | Unconscious | acknowledge the existence of asymmetrical power relationships| accept systemic differences between | ‘White people do not experience privilege or advantages based on race.’ | | | between races | races; or the complete lack of | ‘All races are treated the same in the United States.’ | | | | knowledge of systemic differences | Examples of b) | | | | between race due to being situated in | ‘Everyone in my town is white – I have zero interactions with nonwhite people.’ | | | | a racially homogenous environment | ‘Since I was born and raised in Japan, I’ve only spent time with Asians and have never been around any other race.’ | Note: This table is based on information from The National Museum of African American History & Culture and Pew Research Center (National Museum of African American History and Culture, n.d.; Parker et al., 2015). education and replace it with opportunities and resources to reach and represent all human beings equitably. Thornton (2017), in his chapter on the historiography of the social studies in the *Wiley Handbook of Social Studies Research* (Manfra & Bolick, 2017), indicated the whiteness of the field’s ‘Old Masters’ (p. 12) in the early 20th century and prior. Thornton identified the curriculum foci as created by and for the members of a white, Christian, middle-class heterosexual audience. The civil rights and feminist movements of the 1960s and 1970s resulted in acknowledging more minority scholarship in social studies. However, the 20th century ended with small and limited changes to the field’s advancement and acknowledgment of BIPOC history and inclusion in social studies curricula. Regardless, social studies is a natural area in which students and teachers can learn to examine whiteness critically and its impact on education and society as a whole, historically, presently, and imminently. Effectively achieving meaningful change within society requires teachers of all colors to acknowledge white social studies as a dismal reality of historical teaching. All teachers must work to find methods, curricula, and goals to combat the whitewashing of history actively and intentionally. **White Social Studies** White social studies (WSS), a term initially coined by Chandler and Branscombe (2015), ‘categorizes the ways whiteness determines the perspectives, values, and voices that are present and absent in social studies classrooms’ (Hawkman, 2019, p. 217). WSS is social studies that addresses the racial lens of white people (i.e., their understanding of history and race) and how white people pass on learning and understanding of a topic with a white lens via white normativity and standardization. Chandler and Branscombe (2015) identified WSS’s ten characteristics in their chapter ‘White Social Studies: Protecting the White Racial Code’ (Chandler, 2015) and Colley and Broome (2020) went on to summarize it as seeking ‘to maintain the status quo of the white dominant hegemonic narrative across K-12 history, economics, civics, geography, and other social sciences’ (p. 272). While instructors might not know they are teaching a whitewashed version of history, their lack of awareness causes no less harm to the legacy of BIPOC and contributes to the misplaced framing and default standardization of whiteness. While such unawareness may appear accidental or unintentional, it results from a legacy of systemic racism in education (An, 2020; Chandler, 2009). In addition, critical whiteness scholars have exposed the often-unnamed role of whiteness in producing racial hierarchies (Foste & Irwin, 2020). Therefore, studying the impact of whiteness on People of Color throughout history is a vital step. There is a growing call to reassess how educators frame social studies education (Hawkman, 2020; Navarro & Howard, 2017; Picower, 2009). Social studies educators and scholars had to reframe the approach to content and its implications on citizenship education after September 11 due to societal ideological changes in the United States (Foner & Bender, 2002, as cited in Gerwin, 2015). Similarly, teachers and scholars must attend to the conversation on how to address race in a #BlackLivesMatter versus #AllLivesMatter society with nearly a decade of civil unrest and police brutality to understand the experiences of U.S. students and teachers. Countless examples exist of the whitewashing of U.S. history to present white people as the dominant and superior race. An exhaustive listing of these examples is beyond the scope of this study, as every event studied in U.S. history is a whitewashed version of a partial truth presented to history students. This notion is based simply on the fact that prior to the 21st century, curriculum and content were taught and decided on by the white majority (Kendi, 2019). U.S. History is required in all schools; however, only elective (i.e., optional) courses focus on People of Color (e.g., African American History or Native American History). The very existence and need for such courses and counternarratives indicate the lack of balanced, holistic, and truthful representation of People of Color in U.S. history courses (Navarro & Howard, 2017). Also, not all districts and states have standards and course offerings for classes such as African American History. Therefore, few students receive exposure to a curriculum representing and normalizing the contributions and realities of People of Color. Woodson (1933/1999), in *Mis-Education of the Negro*, implored teachers to instruct Black students on the European ‘mainstream’ as the history, language, and philosophy of their people so that they could be educated enough to serve their communities (as cited in Delpit, 1995). Hooks (1989) wrote about how her white teachers corrected and admonished her when she used her chosen dialect rather than ‘standard English’ (p. 11). Chandler and Branscombe (2015) examined three teachers who taught with a refusal to acknowledge the tensions between their whiteness and its impact on U.S. history education, stating, [I]t can be argued that these teachers are unconsciously engaging in teaching about dominant narratives because this is the way they see their jobs as teachers and as White folk—to reinforce and uphold traditional narratives about U.S. history and the role of race. Or, it can be argued that these teachers, fully aware of their whiteness, have purposefully decided to leave out racial explanations of how and why events in early American history occurred. We believe that both of these are operating, simultaneously, in the thinking of our teachers, but not in their enacted teaching. In this way, they are upholding, through their negotiations of race and history, the idea of White superiority within their history classes. (p. 80) Epstein (2009) found that white students’ experiences in social studies did not cause them to challenge their colorblindness; instead, their experiences contributed to the narrative they saw at home. In contrast, Black students found a disconnect between their personal and academic lessons, resulting in disinterest in school and mistrust of history textbooks. Arguably, such situations are one explanation for Black History Month. Teachers have used Black History Month to implement the history of Black Americans as a curricular focal point (King & Brown, 2014). However, again, such teaching occurs based on teachers’ discretion and awareness, curricular autonomy, and departmental curricular requirements (e.g., preparation for standardized testing). Tosolt (2020), a white educator who teaches white preservice teachers, asserted that white teachers have floundered, thereby perpetuating white supremacy in their attempts at teaching Black History Month. Although Tosolt did not blame the teachers, she cautioned white teachers to examine the intents, processes, and goals of their teaching, writing, ‘Absent a grounding of pedagogy in sociological and historical reality, well-intentioned teachers’ attempts to celebrate Black history through the presentation of decontextualized heroes only reinforce existing anti-Black sentiments’ (p. 773). King and Brown (2014) discussed the numerous transgressive and regressive ways of teaching Black History Month, arguing that scholars and educators must develop more appropriate pedagogies. Scholarly works such as these, as well as news stories of teachers asking students to ‘dress like an African American’ or in ‘animal print’ (Hillbard, 2012), prove that there is a long way to go for white teachers to teach Black History in equitable and humane ways. This is but one example of how the education system has failed in incorporating and normalizing People of Color, especially since this common approach of Black History Month highlights one non-white race, leaving Asian, Latine, Pacific Islander/Native Hawaiian, and Indigenous/Native Alaskan without prominence of acknowledgement on a wide scale. While the intentionality of Black History Month may be inclusion, the outcome is lacking, to say the least and does not to begin the large picture of decentralizing white normativity within curriculum and instruction. Several authors have exposed the continuations of WSS (An, 2020; Chandler, 2009; Thornton, 2017). Prentice Chandler (2009) focused on establishing WSS as a phenomenon as he studied two white high school U.S. history teachers and found that both had a liberal, incremental progressive mentality toward race and racism. Both teachers acknowledged racism in their classrooms and the significance of race but indicated that progress was occurring for a more just society. One of Chandler’s participants admitted to being colorblind and sought to ‘get past’ accountability for the race-based oppression of the ‘past’ (p. 268). Chandler explained, ‘he assumed that his take on race’s impact and the way that it should be handled are in line with the students of color whom he teaches’ (p. 270). More recently, An (2020) observed her daughter’s responses to the WSS taught in her school. Her daughter, an Asian American who was in fourth and fifth grades during the study, engaged in informal conversations with her ‘motherscholar’ (p. 175). Over two years, An collected and analyzed the data and concluded that her daughter, at ten and eleven years of age, sought a sense of belonging in the story of the United States. Her daughter noticed Asian invisibility in her lessons and noted hegemonic whiteness in the curriculum. An’s study sought to disrupt master narratives. While white social studies is a regime of thought (Chandler & Branscombe, 2015) that establishes the field to critically examine how whiteness has impacted the teaching and learning of history, economics, geography and political science, it has yet to be explored further by scholars in order to make it accessible and relatable to not only 6th-12th grade educators but also the general public. Its foundational concepts, though certainly undisputable by field experts and scholars, may not be as palpable to educators, parents or administration. A considerable amount of supporting evidence, discussion, and scaffolding would need to occur in order for it to be a framework that those without expertise in the areas of CRT or critical pedagogy would accept as fact. Still, in understanding why whitewashing and white normativity occur, it is a valuable concept to be examined and discussed with any stakeholder interested in taking social studies education to the next level. **Curriculum** A growing movement exists to implement a more diversified curriculum into the existing presentation (Mercer, 2020). However, without mandated standards for a diversified curriculum, this fruition depends on individual states, districts, or departments. In 2005, Philadelphia was the first district to require a Black history course for graduation (Mercer, 2020). States such as Florida, Virginia, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Colorado were working toward mandates for Black and Latine studies within curricula following the racial unrest in 2020. The introduction of the Black History is American History Act by U.S. Democratic Representative Fudge from Ohio occurred in May 2020 to incentivize schools to include Black history instruction (United States Congress, 2020). However, much of these efforts were thwarted in 2022 with the anti-CRT legislation that swept the country (which will be discussed further in this chapter). Still, several states were successful in passing laws to require increased diversity in social studies curriculum: - Illinois became the first state to require the teaching of Asian American history (Shivaram, 2021) - North Dakota passed a law requiring the teaching of Native American history in K-12 curriculum (Brown, 2022) - Connecticut became the first state to require Black and Latine studies in high school (Kountz, 2021) While all these steps advance the field toward equitable representation in social studies classrooms, the last five years have revealed how passionate some can be in preventing the diversification of American history. Textbooks. Classroom textbooks and resources that present an edited reality of U.S. history have contributed to the WSS curricula. Unfortunately, members of generations still alive today learned from textbooks that presented the oppression of People of Color with a rose-colored, whitewashed mutation of the truth. Several scholars have examined these texts closely (e.g., California State Department of Education, 1964; Goldstein, 2008; King, 2016; Schwartz, 2019; Stoskopf & Bermudez, 2017). However, scholars have not been the only ones aware of whitewashing. Other outlets have provided media exposure of the flaws in U.S. history textbooks. *The New York Times* compared eight textbooks from California and Texas published in 2016 or later and how they depicted similar historical events (Goldstein, 2008). The findings showed historical accounts written with subtle and extensive differences, each aligning to the states’ political and moral climates. On the HBO satirical news show *Last Week Tonight*, John Oliver (2020) presented a 30-minute segment on the whitewashing of U.S. history via textbooks, presenting examples from Alabama resources used within the last 40 years. These more accessible forms of information may be part of the reason for the general population’s heightened awareness of how social studies have caused many to misunderstand U.S. history. Primary Documents. Applying CWT to social studies resources requires critically examining primary documents. Many social studies courses include teaching students to read and decipher primary documents for disciplinary literacy (Barton & Levstik, 2004; Wineburg & Reisman, 2015). However, white male enslavers authored many primary documents on the establishment and government of the country. Therefore, educators must teach students to read these documents for their knowledge as U.S. residents and to critically examine the intentionality and reality of what the nation’s leaders wanted for the country as opposed to today’s society. This conception has caused scholars and members of the general public to reexamine certain foundational documents. Scholars have long scrutinized The U.S. Constitution for the phrase ‘life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness’ while millions had been and continued to experience slavery. However, critics have begun closely examining other primary sources for hypocrisy or exclusion. For example, the National Anthem has undergone critical examination because of the phrase ‘land of the free,’ despite its writer being a slave-owning lawyer who represented other enslavers looking to find their ‘runaway property’ (Lehman, 2020, p. 15). Lehman (2020) suggested switching the National Anthem to ‘America the Beautiful’ because of the all-inclusive nature of the song. Music can be a powerful resource for teaching students how to think deeply about the messages in songs from the past and encouraging them to question truth claims (Howell & Callahan, 2016). Students often read the Emancipation Proclamation issued on January 1, 1863. However, they rarely learn that it did not provide freedom for enslaved people until the ratification of the 13th Amendment on December 6, 1865. Additionally, people in Texas remained enslaved until June 19, 1866, after the Civil War (Garrett-Scott et al., 2013). Teaching students to critically examine resources and primary documents is an increasingly valuable 21st-century skill (Wineburg & Reisman, 2015). **Citizenship Education and National Identity** Nearly every generation of Americans educated in public schools, particularly within the 20th century, heard stories of Columbus’ discovery of America, pilgrims seeking religious freedom, and the founding fathers fighting for and establishing democracy. These accounts’ purpose was to create a national identity (Barton & Levstik, 2004) and historical teaching, regardless of the extent of their omissions. The goal was to create unity to remain strong in the face of the perceived 20th-century political enemies of democracy (Thornton, 2017; Urban & Wagoner, 2014): communism, socialism, fascism, and totalitarianism. Like after 9/11, the national unity taught in social studies was an essential component in the survival of the United States; this likely remained true during times of war and hardship throughout U.S. history (Foner & Bender, 2002, as cited in Gerwin, 2015; Wagner et al., 2018). However, scholars have considered U.S. history curricula patriotic propaganda. Nelson (2001) described this as ‘Americanizing youth through patriotic sloganeering, limiting dissent, producing passive but morally righteous citizens, and convincing the masses of the virtues of work and proper status of the upper class’ (p. 28). These selective and edited stories result in a false narrative that does not include countless facts. These selective stories present a version of history that places white people at the forefront of the creation and success of the United States. Historically, national identity has been coupled with whiteness (Barton & Levstik, 2004). Preparing students for democracy requires redefining national identity to include pluralism and inclusivity. When examining the scope of United States history, a central theme is the mistreatment and exclusion of certain people from the full benefits of citizenship, resulting in skepticism toward defining a national identity. Scholars and educators have begun grappling with the tension that has resulted from this injustice: acknowledgement of the honor, respect, and truth long overdue to People of Color who have experienced generations of oppression and mistreatment and balancing this with teaching students of all colors to become active, participatory, and collaborative citizens and residents of the United States (Gibson, 2020; Ho & Barton, 2020). This tension has been manifested with national controversy over Black Americans who refused to stand for the National Anthem or Pledge of Allegiance\textsuperscript{11} (History.com Editors, 2021). \textbf{Standards} \textbf{Existing Standards}. Teachers look to state standards when deciding on curricula, yet the states have varying learning standards based on their policymakers. Therefore, the NCSS’s National Curriculum Standards for Social Studies (2010) and C3 Framework for Social Studies State Standards (2017) is most often used as the guiding force of most states in creating their standards. All the NCSS National Curriculum Standards for Social Studies provide language for guiding teachers toward diverse, reflective curricula that address changes over time and include various cultures and perspectives so that students can develop their identities, understand the nature and impact of authority and power, and learn about societal advancements (NCSS, 2010). The goal of the C3 Framework is to strengthen social studies courses with critical thinking and problem-solving, developing participatory citizenship, and rigorously responding to the marginalization of social studies (NCSS, 2013). The NCSS standards provide a general guideline while allowing state autonomy for content. These standards are nationally available and utilized but not mandated, allowing state and district leaders to decide what to teach students about history. However, therein lie some new problematic disagreements. \textbf{Development of New Standards}. In light of the recent political and racial strife throughout the United States, state leaders have begun to amend their social studies standards and adapt the language most befitting lawmakers as they see their constituency. For example, in \textsuperscript{11}For more information about the history of this movement and protest, see https://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/colin-kaepernick-kneels-during-national-anthem January 2021, the North Carolina Board of Education presented a draft to the new state superintendent with changes to the language, including systematic racism to omit ‘systematic,’ gender identity to omit the word ‘identity,’ and systemic discrimination to omit the word ‘systemic’ (Granados, 2021). Board members reported feeling that the standards that included those words were ‘leftist dogma’ that provided a feeling of ‘America the oppressor, not America the land of opportunity’ (Granados, 2021, para. 5). Conversely, Michigan provided a revised set of standards in 2019 for implementation in 2023 that include more examples of the roles of women, minority organizations, Muslims, and Black people in U.S. history (Chambers, 2019). In 2020, CBS News aired a report on the social studies standards of all 50 states for a comparison of how educators teach Black history in the United States. The report found many areas of disconnect that could be the reason for national confusion about Black people and the severity of the oppression they experienced at the hands of white people in the U.S. Among the findings were: - Less than half of the states required students to learn about racism. - Sixteen states included states’ rights (e.g., sectionalism, tariffs, and economic disagreement) as causes for the Civil War. - Eight states did not address the civil rights movement. - Seven states did have direct requirements for teaching slavery. - Only two states (Massachusetts and Maryland) required students to learn about white supremacy. - West Virginia presented slavery as an example of supply and demand. - North Carolina presented slavery as immigration. Texas did not require learning about institutional racism in the required U.S. history course; it only provided an elective course. Teachers spent less than 10% of class time teaching Black history (Duncan et al., 2020). NCSS President Heafner commented in the report that while the standards’ lack of specificity does not indicate that teachers do not include the concepts in their lessons, the teachers do ‘turn to the curriculum standards as their guideline. So, the fact that they are not there could give a perception that is not something that is absolutely essential that they have to address’ (Duncan et al., 2020, para. 14). **Curriculum Results of Applying Critical whiteness Theory** **The 1619 Project.** Suppose the ultimate goal of social studies education is to teach young people to operate in a ‘…culturally diverse, democratic society in an interdependent world’ (NCSS, n.d.-a, para. 3). In that case, social studies must have innovative curricula with a unified understanding of the principles of a culturally diverse and democratic society. To this end, there have been profound implications in implementing two opposing perspectives of one innovative curriculum. In August 2019, *The New York Times Magazine* presented *The 1619 Project*, which has since undergone adaptation into a social studies curriculum. *New York Times* writer Nikole Hannah-Jones, who received the Pulitzer Prize for Commentary for her introductory essay, led the project. *The 1619 Project* suggests critically reframing U.S. history to begin in 1619, when the first Enslaved People of African Decent arrived in the United States. The curriculum provides reading guides, activities, lessons, and other resources for social studies teachers (Pulitzer Center, n.d.). It has received praise for providing innovative and powerful concepts to challenge how learners see and perceive the United States with the placement of slavery and the contributions of Black Americans ‘at the very center of the story we tell ourselves about who we are’ (Da Silva, 2020, para. 7). Currently, educators at more than 4,500 schools nationwide have adopted the curriculum (Farrow, 2020). Some historians, conservatives, and politicians have criticized the curriculum (Meyrat, 2020; Schaefer Riley, 2020). President Trump provided some of the most public criticisms of *The 1619 Project* in a national statement in which he denounced the curriculum and threatened to defund the schools where it is implemented. Trump (2020a) professed, ‘Critical race theory, *The 1619 Project* and the crusade against American history is toxic propaganda, ideological poison, that, if not removed, will dissolve the civic bonds that tie us together, will destroy our country’ (para. 20). The President then signed *The 1776 Commission* into executive order to produce a report on the core principles of the founding of the United States to ‘promote patriotic education,’ (Trump, 2020a, para. 25). The Commission, funded by the Department of Education, consisted of members appointed by the president (Trump, 2020b) and provided its report on January 2021, on Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day and just before President Trump’s final day in office (The President’s Advisory 1776 Commission, 2021). The 18-member commission, none of them professional U.S. historians, lacked scholarly citations, downplayed slavery and slave ownership, and omitted Native Americans in its collective impression of America’s narrative. It additionally listed challenges to America’s principles to include slavery, progressivism, racism, and ‘identity politics,’ which they described as valuing ‘people by characteristics like race, sex, and sexual orientation and holds that new times demand new right to replace the old,’ (The President’s Advisory 1776 Commission, 2021, p.15-16). The American Historical Association, American Library Association, American Civil Liberties Union, and American Association of University Professors all condemned *The 1776 Report*. The NCSS also provided a statement before the report’s release that indicated that state leaders and educators decide curricula, not the federal government (NCSS, 2020b). President Biden abolished the executive order for the commission on his first day in office. President Trump’s (2020a) public criticism of critical race theory and teaching history with multiple perspectives spilled into a nationwide conversation after he left office. This resulted in many states passing legislation preventing social studies teachers from discussing difficult history topics that may cause feelings of guilt based on students’ race (Farag, 2021). The difficulty of many secondary social studies teachers in addressing controversial issues and teaching students how to resolve differing opinions (Hawley et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2017; Lintner, 2018) will likely result in continued controversy on which curriculum materials to include. However, the NCSS (2020b) indicated that curriculum resource decisions are not the federal government’s responsibility. Instead, the NCSS expressed support for any school, district, and teacher in the implementation of resources such as *The 1619 Project* ‘to accurately depict the history of slavery in the United States, broaden the horizons of their students, and prepare citizens for a just democratic society’ (para. 4). Despite this statement of support, policymakers in many states still strive to eradicate *The 1619 Project* curriculum from schools in their states based on the statement by Trump’s commission (Rodriguez, 2021). Other Antiracist Curricula. Antiracism can have many forms in social studies education: a topic, an approach, a curriculum, an ideology, and a goal. Many states now provide educators with guidance for implementing antiracist materials in their course content (Arizona Department of Education, 2020; Nebraska Department of Education, 2020; New Jersey Department of Education, n.d.; State of Michigan, n.d.). Additionally, national organizations have provided antiracist resources on their websites (Center for the Study of Social Policy, n.d.; NCSS, n.d.-b). Other antiracist curricula have emerged over the years to reframe the teaching and presentation of U.S. history. For example, LaGarrett King (2019, 2020), founder and director of the Carter Center for K-12 Black History Education and professor at the University of Missouri, released a curriculum used in a Kentucky school district entitled *Developing Black Historical Consciousness*. Another resource is *The Howard Zinn Project* - a database created by Zinn (2008) to ‘introduce students to a more accurate, complex, and engaging understanding of United States history than is found in traditional textbooks and curricula’ (para. 2) and presents lessons and activities for teaching about reparations for Black people, teaching Reconstruction, and abolishing Columbus Day. Finally, many scholars have evaluated and supported antiracist materials and sentiments in secondary social studies classrooms, as well as other content areas and grade levels (Hawkman, 2020; King & Chandler, 2015; Pollock, 2008; Smith, 2014). As such, the construct of antiracism is a practice with increasing popularity and an approach to the social studies experience for teaching students how to work against racism actively and intentionally. **Backlash Against Critical whiteness Theory in Social Studies.** In the eighteen months from the beginning of this study to its completion, the United States watched social studies education move from being a venue in which to educate students about America’s difficult history with racism and make efforts to correct white normativity in social studies with the aforementioned curricula (Chambers, 2019; Duncan et al., 2020) to a politicized reactionary response in which lawmakers have enacted bills preventing such curriculum from being included (Granados, 2021; Greene, 2022). Even further, in some cases, the bans dissuade the inclusion of certain factual history being taught in the classroom (Laats, 2015; Waxman, 2022). Such drastic and swift measure leaves scholars seeking to understand how it is education is being edited to perpetuate white normativity (Feingold, 2022; Pfeifer, 2022; Schocker & De Senso, 2022), and secondary educators are left confused and afraid about what they can and cannot teach about in their classrooms, under penalty of the new laws being passed in their states (NBC12 Newsroom, 2022; Schocker & De Senso, 2022; Waxman, 2022). **Legislation.** By January of 2021, just eight months after the death of George Floyd and the subsequent global unrest that followed which resulted in sweeping pledges from states and districts to begin to explore mandates *for* higher diversity in social studies, several conservative lawmakers and their constituents began proposing legislation that would not only stop this diversification but, in many cases, reverse it. From the time President Trump left office January 2021 to July 2022, 42 states introduced bills or mandates restricting the teaching of critical race theory in K-12 classrooms as well as universities and/or set restrictions about how educators can discuss race (Arrojas, 2022; Schwartz, 2022). Nineteen states have passed legislation regarding how race can be presented and taught in secondary and/or higher education classrooms. Some of the language in these laws are ambiguous and therefore problematic, leaving educators unsure of what the limitations are and why they are now required to edit the truth of America’s difficult history. Forbes described an active index designed by PEN America that is actively tracking the proposed, passed and contested legislation restricting conversations of race and gender in secondary and higher education (Greene, 2022). For example: - Florida HB7/SB 148 bars K-12 schools and public colleges from adopting any instructional materials or engaging in any professional development programs that ‘espouses, promotes, advances, inculcates, or compels’ belief in certain ideas about race, sex, color, or national origin. Classroom instruction related to past racial injustice may not ‘indoctrinate or persuade’ students to believe these ideas. - Idaho, HB 377 bans public schools and institutions of higher education from ‘direct[ing] or otherwise compel[ing] students to personally affirm, adopt, or adhere’ to the outlined ‘critical race theory’ tenets. Bars funding for such prohibited acts. - Texas, HB 3979 bars teachers from being ‘compelled to discuss a particular current event or widely debated and currently controversial issue.’ Teachers who do so should ‘strive to explore the topic from diverse and contending perspectives without giving deference to any one perspective.’ Bars requiring an understanding of the 1619 Project. Bans schools from requiring or making part of a course certain concepts. (Greene, 2022) While these bans will be further discussed in Chapter 6, it is clear from the language in these laws that the ambiguity of what is and is not allowed to be discussed in secondary and higher education classrooms can be easily perceived as censorship and thus contradicts the basic ideals of democratic education. **Applying Critical whiteness Theory to Curricular Decision-Making** If the field of social studies education wants to ensure students of history are receiving a robust and comprehensive examination of America’s narrative and if students are to explore history as critical thinkers of 21st century learning, it is imperative that their teachers first become fluent on their perspectives of race and how it may or may not influence what they teach in their classrooms. Therefore, it is essential for teachers to explore their professional identity, beliefs, pedagogical content knowledge, and their positionality on their roles as gatekeepers of student learning. Professional Identity Scholars have explored teachers’ professional identities to understand how they approach pedagogy, curricular choices, classroom interactions, and even their success as educators across the field of education worldwide (Bosse & Torner, 2015; Siraj-Blatchford, 1993). In this study on how teachers’ racial perceptions could influence curricular choices, teachers’ professional identities will be the means used to frame the connection between their identities and curricular decision-making (Beijaard et al., 2004). Many ways exist to approach, define, and categorize teachers’ professional identities (Beauchamp & Thomas, 2009; Beijaard et al., 2004; Farrell, 2011; Gee, 2000; Hanna et al., 2019; Noonan, 2019; Sachs, 2001). For this research, *professional identity* will be teachers’ perceptions of themselves as educators with beliefs about teaching, learning, and their roles in the exchange contributing to their decision-making and pedagogical processes. Teachers aware of their professional identities can use them to guide educational decisions, make curricular choices, interact with students, collaborate with colleagues, and manage behavior. Beijaard et al. (2004) identified the features of professional identity, including the reinterpretation of experiences, the merging of the person with context, ongoing professional development, and the use of subidentities for different contexts and relationships. *Race* is not synonymous with *identity*; however, it contributes to individuals’ worldviews, how they see themselves in it, and how educators interact within their profession (Brown, 2006; Browne, 2012; Wandix-White, 2020). Teachers’ race, personal identities, and professional identities as educators often intersect to inform their beliefs. In turn, their beliefs often intersect with their pedagogical decision-making (Ho et al., 2017). Secondary social studies teachers must guide students in forming their identities: how they see themselves in the world and where they belong. Educators must ask themselves the same questions (Hubbard, 2019) to inform their pedagogical decisions. Teachers who articulate their goals and rationale within their professional identities can recognize their positionality and, with a further self-reflexive examination, identify the impact of their subjectivity (Madison, 2020) on their curricular choices. **Teacher Beliefs** This literature review has addressed the definition and impact of racial identity on the racial lens. This section of the review addresses the impacts of teachers’ beliefs, regardless of racial identity or racial lens, on their worldview when they know about the systematic injustice experienced by others because of race; this is a concept known as a *racial equity lens*. For a teacher, seeing with a racial equity lens requires looking at pedagogical content knowledge and decision-making for a diverse student body. Using a racial equity lens, educators must look for instances when Students of Color may be disadvantaged against their white counterparts due to the long-standing systemic racism in education. Typically, individuals who utilize a racial equity lens are highly aware of their racial optics. However, individuals with a race lens may not always utilize the racial equity lens. The difference between the two concepts tremendously affects teachers’ decision-making (Ho et al., 2017; Pajares, 1992; Richardson, 1996). Much racial tension occurred in the first 20 years of the 21st century. Secondary social studies teachers have struggled with their perceptions of racial identity, racism, and race history while teaching their students how to navigate these topics, feelings, and experiences at the personal, societal, political, and academic levels. Extensive research has emerged within the last ten years on how social studies teachers should make curricular choices with varying perspectives (NCSS, 2008). Teachers willing to have open and guided discussions with their students on race have better connections and more success (Hawley et al., 2016; Lintner, 2018). **Pedagogical Content Knowledge** Shulman (1986, 1987) indicated that a teacher’s formation of pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) determines the effectiveness in the classroom. Teachers must have the ability to articulate content knowledge and combine it with a pedagogy to successfully reach learners (Gitomer & Zisk, 2015; Shulman, 1987). PCK differs from content knowledge or pedagogy as it ‘goes beyond knowledge of subject matter per se to the dimension of subject matter knowledge for teaching’ (Shulman, 1986, p. 9). PCK is craft knowledge, recognizing what makes a topic easy or challenging to learn and the preconceptions or misconceptions that may exist for students. PCK also means knowing how to transcend those obstacles to create meaningful and powerful learning for students. **Racial Pedagogical Content Knowledge.** Teachers self-aware of their racial lenses who commit to teaching social studies with a racial equity lens utilize a racial pedagogical decision-making process to include the same, if not more, resources for and focus on BIPOC in curriculum topics, resources, and approaches (Chandler, 2015; King & Chandler, 2016). Racial pedagogical decision-making consists of how teachers approach pedagogical decision-making for race content with the awareness of the impact on themselves and their students (Hawkman, 2019). However, students suffer when teachers do not take the time to explore, implement, or subscribe to racial pedagogical content knowledge (RPCK) or claim colorblindness (Buchanan-Rivera, 2019; Tatum, 2019). Consequently, students pass into adulthood without learning how to see the world through others’ perspectives. Such students may subsequently feel less concerned about the common good and remain self-absorbed with their mentalities and views. Gatekeeping In the first chapter of *Teaching Social Studies that Matters*, Thornton (2005) indicated the importance of gatekeeping, which the author defined as the ‘decisions teachers make about curriculum and instruction and the criteria they use to make those decisions’ (p. 1). Thornton argued for the importance of gatekeeping in social studies because they lack a definitive scope and sequence. Therefore, educating gatekeepers ‘requires simultaneous attention to both curriculum and method and their interrelationships’ (p. 5). Teachers are the direct gatekeepers of student learning (Thornton, 1989). Educators guide students in the democratic ideals they use to ground their civic competency. For some, gatekeeping is a natural scaffold and pathway for student learning (Nagy, 2000; Thornton, 1989), while others may find it restrictive and conflicting (Hylton et al., 2018). Secondary social studies teachers who develop their PCK must be able to scaffold student learning of citizenship and civic competency, as the goal of education is to provide for democracy (Barton & Levstik, 2004; Darling-Hammond, 2006; Urban & Wagoner, 2014). Vansledright critiqued Thornton’s (2005) book and the specific consequences of gatekeeping for Students of Color. Thornton (2006) responded to Vansledright’s critique and defended his work, articulating that ‘many teachers do not incorporate research findings into what they do’ (p. 417). Thornton clarified that educators must inform their gatekeeping decisions with research. The back and forth between Thornton and Vansledright showed the additional debate and ambiguities within the field, particularly regarding Students of Color, providing room for further clarifying research with data. Fifteen years of research, literature, and theory on RPCK have focused on racial decision-making and gatekeeping. Scholars have reexamined teaching social studies with a racial lens (Buchanan-Rivera, 2019; Hawkman, 2019, 2020; Hawley et al., 2016; King & Chandler, 2016; Tatum, 2019). Building from Chandler and Branscombe (2015), King and Chandler (2016) synthesized these works and more in the first chapter of Crowe’s and Cuenca’s book, *Rethinking Social Studies Teacher Education in the Twenty-First Century*. They described how race and social studies pedagogy and teacher education are not heavily critiqued and widely discussed topics. Thus, ‘the pedagogical moves employed by these teachers represent an attempt to teach U.S. history without a racial lens…they seek to pass down a version of ‘our story’ that, due to our specialness as a nation, cannot be tainted by the sin of institutional racism’ (King & Chandler, 2016, p. 11). Ultimately, teachers’ strong sense of purpose is the only reliable predictor of effective gatekeeping (Barton & Levstik, 2004; Thornton, 2006). The distinguishing factor is racial identity, which indicates teachers’ professional goals and the why, what, and how they teach social studies. **Curriculum Mandates.** The response to standardized testing as a form of accountability in schools remains divisive in social studies education. Such effects include limiting instruction to the topics indicated by state standards and curriculum mandates and asking students to demonstrate facts rather than critical thinking (Barton & Levstik, 2004). The marginalization of social studies in favor of science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) due to the No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) in 2002 resulted in the absence of social studies in assessments. This absence has reduced the time spent teaching social studies (NCSS, 2008). Mandates for curriculum include predetermined syllabi or resources, state-approved textbooks, or district-required curricula. Many educators have reported not learning about or receiving training for the mandated materials they must utilize (Committee on Youth Education for Citizenship, 1980). President Obama signed the Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) into law in 2015 to replace NCLB as the country’s primary education law. The purpose of ESSA is to hold schools accountable for how students learn and succeed, particularly students who learn and think differently, including those in poverty, special education, and English language learners. The ESSA requires state testing for students in reading, math, and science; however, social studies or civics are not mandated topics. Therefore, state and district leaders must decide what and how to teach. **Teacher Autonomy and Authentic Pedagogy** Teachers’ autonomy in their classrooms influences their curricular decisions for the discussed reasons. A prescribed curriculum correlates with little autonomy. However, studies have shown the benefits of teacher autonomy and authentic pedagogy for student learning (Maddox & Saye, 2014; Saye & SSIRC, 2013, 2014). Teachers trusted and empowered to create and implement robust curricula to facilitate student learning can create powerful social studies courses. However, teacher autonomy and authentic pedagogy can cause problems, such as inconsistencies in student knowledge and ability, minimized time spent with students because of increased time developing curriculum, and feelings of isolation and stress for teachers (Johnson, 2016). On the other hand, teachers receive encouragement in their training programs to become creative facilitators of learning and develop authentic pedagogies (NCSS, 2018). However, when they enter the profession, they find that they have little independence and must adhere to the standards and mandates of an established curriculum (Cochran-Smith et al., 2013; Darling-Hammond, 2006). A need exists for research to balance implementing innovative, authentic curricula and ensuring that students successfully obtain the necessary content knowledge and skillsets. **Controversial Topics** Innovative content reflective of the recent climate and topics of U.S. politics and society has begun to emerge (Callahan & Hubbard, 2019; Moorhead & Jimenez, 2020). Teachers often struggle to facilitate balanced learning on controversial topics (Ho et al., 2017; Lintner, 2018). Thus, the need for students to learn about current events and controversies is more dire than ever. Teachers open to discourse and dialogue with their students are highly effective (National Board for Professional Teaching Standards, 2015). Some scholars contend that educators avoid teaching controversial issues because they fear losing control or getting distracted from accomplishing the lesson (Barton & Levstik, 2004; Pace, 2019). While many teachers avoid teaching controversy (Alongi et al., 2016; Hawley et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2017; Lintner, 2018), Hawkman (2019) followed a teacher who evaluated the impact of race, ethnicity, and gender on how people see themselves and the world around them. Hawkman concluded that these topics are only as controversial as the teacher allows. **Teacher’s Race** Over half of the student population in the United States is nonwhite, and scholars expect the levels of diversity to increase (Geiger, 2018). The growing diversity, along with the civil unrest and polarization of Americans seen throughout 2020, has resulted in a less concrete definition of *democratic citizenship*. Therefore, social studies teachers must apply wise-practice principles (Orrell, 2019; Shulman, 1987) and adapt their curricula and materials to the changing times. However, race is an incredibly controversial topic and controversial issue\(^{12}\) that may cause \(^{12}\) See Ho et al. (2017) for the differences between controversial topics and controversial issues. teachers to shy away from conversations or teachings that present race at the center of controversy (Gindi & Erlich, 2017; Lintner, 2018). Citing research from neuroscientists, King et al. (2018) indicated that brains can shut down during stressful racial encounters. As previously discussed, the very curriculum and truth of race and racial history is a point of contention within the United States. Regardless, discussing race and racism in social studies classrooms is essential should teachers look to prepare their students for participation in democratic citizenship, particularly in a country as racially diverse as the United States. In 2014, 73% of millennials reported a belief in the need for open dialogue on race issues (Bouie, 2014). However, only 20% reported feeling comfortable enough to discuss race issues. If these conversations do not occur collectively in social studies classrooms, then where else can students safely learn to engage in and navigate difficult conversations on race issues and racism history? A teacher’s race does not indicate ability or skill in the classroom. However, the literature has indicated that teachers’ race can affect what they teach and how. There are certainly incredibly powerful and effective antiracist white teachers who strive to make change by presenting their students with a balanced and equitable history. However, there are likely also white teachers whose ‘not-racist’ mentality has unknowingly had a negative impact on their gatekeeping and their students’ perceptions of race history and People of Color. Similarly, some Teachers of Color who have received training and education only from white teachers with white racial lenses may pass on the history they have learned from their whitewashed texts by unknowingly perpetuating WSS. A need exists for more research on teachers’ racial lenses, their experiences, and RPCK to determine if their race impacts their curricular decisions. **White Teachers.** Systemic racism has resulted in a majority of white teachers (Fultz, 2004). Nearly 80% of those in the teaching force identify as white (Walker, 2018), while more than half of their students are nonwhite (U.S. Department of Education, 2019a). Furthermore, scholars have predicted that the number of nonwhite students will grow exponentially in the next 20 years. The disparity of nonwhite teachers indicates the need to guide white teachers in identifying their white privilege and racial lenses. White teachers must also learn more about their students’ difficulties when learning from teachers not of their race and how to work against this phenomenon with RPCK. **Teachers of Color.** Teachers of Color have alarmingly representational statistics in education, which is by no means accidental or coincidental, (Gladwell, 2021; Kendi, 2019). The U.S. Department of Education (2019a) indicated that 7% of teachers are Black, 9% are Hispanic, 2% are Asian, and 3% are Pacific Islander, Native American, or Biracial. As more than half (51%) of the student population is nonwhite, a substantial difference exists. Multiple factors contribute to the likelihood that these teacher ratios will not soon balance out regardless of their proven benefits to students: the ongoing national teacher shortage (Boyce, 2019), the low retention rates of new teachers (McLaughlin, 2018), endemic systemic racism that causes BIPOC to have an academic disadvantage to enter the profession (Gitomer et al., 2010; Shuls, 2017), the complexities of educating during a global pandemic (Anderson, 2020; Walters, 2020) and the subsequent exodus from the field due to burnout and low wages (Gomez, 2022; Walker, 2022) **Conclusion** Many scholars have addressed race, racism, racial identity, antiracism, racial apathy, and white superiority, with much of the research occurring within the social studies field. However, a gap in the literature exists on the influence of secondary social studies teachers’ race or ideologies of white superiority on their curricular choices.
