Because the social studies curriculum was created and taught by mostly white people until the late twentieth century (Gladwell, 2021; Kendi, 2019), I believe educators have likely (unknowingly) conveyed a lens of white superiority to all students of social studies through white social studies and normalizing white people in America’s narrative. Additionally, I posit that most teachers have not instructed students otherwise with a frame of postcolonialism and antiracism because they have likely not been guided or empowered to do so. Therefore, within the United States, every generation and every race of student up to and including the current generation has received a narrative of American history that centers on white people as the dominant, ‘normal,’ and most influential group in U.S. history. What do all children within the United States have in common, regardless of race, class, gender, or citizenship status? Education, whether private, public, virtual or homeschool. Students learn about the history and current events within the United States in social studies. The NCSS (2008) has indicated the need to educate students in all areas of social studies to prepare them to participate in democracy. Thus, it stands to reason that how students learn to see themselves and others within the democracy is the responsibility of social studies educators. Furthermore, students of democracy must understand the context of their realities by learning an unabridged version of U.S. history without the whitewash. The United States needs such education for advancement toward a 21st-century mentality. Ensuring this advancement requires examining social studies teachers and the impact of their racial lenses on how they teach and present U.S. history to future generations. CHAPTER 3 METHODOLOGY In Chapter 1, I presented the comprehensive background and need for a study on secondary social studies teachers’ racial lenses and consciousnesses and their impact on curricular decision-making within their curriculum. In Chapter 2, I provided the key definitions, supporting concepts, and literature of critical whiteness theory (CWT) as the driving framework for this study. Chapter 2 also defined foundational concepts such as racial identity, racial lens, racial consciousness, and pedagogical decision-making of educators. Exploring these components was the means of grounding this study and developing the research questions. This chapter presents the detailed process of the research design, including the methodology and tools for collecting data to answer the four guiding research questions. After the data collection, the data analysis procedure is presented and outlined, along with the study’s ethics, trustworthiness, reliability, validity, and limitations. The study aimed to explore the impact of social studies teachers’ racial lenses and racial consciousness on their curriculum. To fully explore this topic, I developed four research questions to guide the study: 1. How have current secondary U.S. history teachers experienced learning about race? 2. How do current high school U.S. history teachers interpret their racial optics? 3. How have these U.S. history teachers included or omitted their views of race within their curriculum? 4. How have these teachers been guided on decentralizing the white narrative from American history or including diversity in their curriculum, if at all? Qualitative researchers apply the theoretical framework to collect data, draw conclusions, and find results about particular research questions. Maxwell (2013) described the nature of qualitative research design as flexible, inductive, and reflexive. Qualitative research is a standard methodology within social studies education, secondary education, and curriculum and instruction. Quantitative and mixed methods studies also exist within those areas (Iglesias et al., 2017; Samuels, 2019). The goals of qualitative research include understanding the meaning of participants’ experiences in context and developing causal explanations (Maxwell, 2013). My professional opinion is that qualitative research is required to understand the complex development of racial lens and consciousness and the innumerable factors that cause teachers to decide on their content. Qualitative research is the best method to probe the story behind teachers’ racial understandings to capture the complex and nuanced picture of their curricular choices and their implications. Intersecting CwT and pedagogical decision-making is a means of gaining insight into the impact of these elements on how educators present racial history within the United States’ narrative. How educators present history could directly influence the racial perceptions of millions of U.S. students. **Method Overview** This study has a critical phenomenological interview approach. A phenomenological interview is distinctive from other methods as it ‘combines life-history interviewing…and focused, in-depth interviewing informed by assumptions drawn from phenomenology’ (Seidman, 2006, p. 14). This interview method has neither a tight structure nor standard, classic lines of questioning. Instead, researchers conducting phenomenological interviews ask open-ended questions to explore the participants and their responses (Roulston, 2018; Seidman, 2006). The critical phenomenological interview (deMarrais, 2004) procedure was an appropriate approach for this study, as it is considered a standard of qualitative interviewing (Seidman, 2006). I chose it as my primary data collection method because of the study’s focus: the phenomenon of Chandler & Branscombe’s (2015) white social studies (WSS) and its impact on students who grow up to become teachers. Phenomenological interviews provided the opportunity to understand the participants’ educational experiences, personal memories, and professional practices. DeMarrais (2004) emphasized that researchers conducting phenomenological interviews specifically apply their chosen theories to ‘inform, enable, and constrain the qualitative interview studies they conduct’ (p. 56). Additionally, deMarrais discussed the essence of everyday experiences in people’s lives. One of the main goals of the interviews was to capture the quintessence of the participants’ every day experiences making daily curricular decisions and the impact critical whiteness may have had on their decision-making. Further, deMarrais stated that researchers must indicate the theoretical positions they ‘are assuming in their interview studies to set out how these beliefs and assumptions inform the work’ (p. 59). At the end of this chapter, I present the theoretical positionality I used to inform my work in this study. The semi structured interviews in this study had several interview protocol lines of questioning. I constructed the questions preemptively to include all the necessary data, explore the interview questions, and gather information to make conclusions. I created the interview protocols ahead of time. The semi structured approach provided flexibility and dialogue during each interaction (Merriam & Tisdell, 2016). Data Collection In structuring the study, I deferred to Roulston’s (2018) five steps for designing studies with qualitative interviews (see Figure 2). Additionally, to structure the method of this critical phenomenological interview study, I employed Seidman’s (2006) three-interview series wherein interview one establishes the context of the participant’s experiences, the second interview asks participants to apply their experiences to their current context, the third interview guides participants to reflect on the meaning of their experiences (p.21). Figure 3 Roulston’s Five Steps for Designing Studies With Qualitative Interviews - Ensure interview data is well suited to the research questions - Consider criteria for participant selection - Develop interview guides aligning to the interview tradition - Gain consent for research - Recruit participants and schedule interviews Note. Adapted from Roulston, K. (2010). *Reflective interviewing: A guide to theory and practice*. Sage. Knowing that the study’s purpose should shape the content of the interview (Suzuki et al., 2007), I developed interview questions based on the purpose of the study I described at the beginning of this chapter. The interviews took place via Zoom. I anticipated three 60-to-120-minute interviews per person following the three-step interview process (Seidman, 2006). First, I utilized empirical phenomenological research (Rudestam & Newton, 2015) to develop my interview questions to collect detailed descriptions of the participant’s experiences with the phenomenon of white social studies, either in their educations or in their teaching. Second, I used open-ended questions and dialogue during the semi structured interviews which reflectively analyzed and interpreted the participant’s story to ‘describe the structure of the experience,’ (Rudestam & Newton, 2015, p.43). **Developing Interview Guides** In her third step, Roulston (2018) describes that the interview guides must be carefully constructed to gather honest and reliable data from the participants. This goes beyond simply asking open-ended questions in a semi structured format. Instead, an interview tradition or typology must be decided upon so the researcher can ensure the quality of their work. I have subscribed to a neo-positivist typology of conceptions in qualitative interviews (Alvesson, 2003; Roulston, 2010). Methodologically, the neo-positivist interviewer takes on a neutral role and does not express their views on the topic within the interviews (Roulston, 2010), minimizing their influence and allowing the participant to speak openly and honestly. For example, while I disclosed my subjectivity and positionality at the end of this chapter, I did not disclose it to the participants until I completed data collection so I could avoid the participant editing their responses based on my opinions, thus minimizing bias in the data. Roulston (2010) further emphasizes that there should be multiple interviews and data collection methods to ensure the quality of research, and the interview guides should be constructed without leading the participant (p. 205). **Participants** **Recruitment.** After obtaining approval from the Internal Review Board (IRB) to conduct the study initially in June of 2021 (Appendix D), I distributed the advertisement flier for the study via personal contacts, email, social media forums, and pages (e.g., Facebook and Reddit), snowball sampling, and schools wherein I have professional references and contacts. I distributed the flier in late July through the end of August 2021. However, I did not receive many qualified applications – I suspect because the timing of the participant recruitment aligned with the beginning of the school year when educators are often especially overwhelmed. I also determined a need for a general survey element in the data collection and analysis, which I will discuss later in this chapter. Therefore, I withdrew my postings, drafted and finalized the survey, determined how to best attract interview participants, and resubmitted my approval from the IRB. I received approval in October 2021, at which point I reposted the flier with an updated set of requirements and increased participant compensation. I again began advertising on Facebook and Reddit forums and pages in early November and, within weeks, received fifty-two completed applications from secondary social studies teachers across the United States. **Selection.** Purposeful participant selection could have a significant impact on the study’s results. Maxwell (2013) indicated the need for typicality, heterogeneity, uniqueness, and the ability to establish productive relationships when choosing study participants. Because of the proposed topic under study, I carefully considered if I should limit the participants to solely white teachers. The purpose of the study was to understand the impact of social studies teachers’ racial perspective on their curriculum and determine if white social studies is perpetuated in current classrooms. Therefore, the study did not require the participants to be of a certain race in the selection process. Like any students, Teachers of Color have learned from white and BIPOC teachers, and their racial lens is informed by much more than their racial identities. Thus, the study was open to participants from any racial demographic if the participant had two or more white social studies teachers in their education. The desired sample was between five and 10 participants. However, I remained receptive to including more or less depending on the results of the recruitment and selection process. Upon receiving the 52 applications, I first narrowed the pool of applicants based on their completion of the selection process form (Appendix E) as well as the inclusion criteria: - currently teaching sixth- to 12th-grade U.S. History or American history elective - educated within the United States - learned U.S. history from two or more white teachers - certified in social studies - self-described as candid, open, and honest - can provide documentation/samples of their curricula - accessible virtually (e.g., Zoom) After this process eliminated a few applicants, I began sorting the applicants based on the U.S. region, aiming for equal representation from two or more areas. Once arranged, I narrowed the applications based on several other pertinent factors: the applicant’s answers about their willingness to be open and honest, the types of courses they taught, and the extent of autonomy in their curriculum decisions. These eliminated several more applicants, and 28 remained viable. Of the 28 invitations I sent to join the study, 21 accepted and were sent consent forms (see Appendix F) and questionnaires (see Appendix G) that would later provide me with background data on their demographics, teaching experiences, training, and personal social studies education. Of those applicants, 10 completed all the requirements. I eliminated one participant because they were the only remaining applicant from the West Coast; since I wanted equal representation from varying regions and already had three participants each from the Midwest, East Coast, and the Southern United States, I thanked the West Coast applicant, and I dismissed them from the study. This process took me approximately 17 days. Participant Profiles I developed profiles based on their participant application and questionnaire with nine participants for this study. I had three participants from three regions in the United States: the Midwest, the South, and the East Coast. Two participants identified as females, and seven identified as males. All were given the option to choose their pseudonym or would be assigned one. In addition, there were eight white participants and one Person of Color – specifically South Asian. Figure 3 is where I depict the participants in more detail before I present the findings so that readers can consider the context of their identity and upbringing. For these profiles, I also use the participants’ wording to portray themselves to allow the reader further insight into their self-identity and personality. East Coast Participants. Charles. Charles is a white male in his 30s living in an urban city in the eastern United States. He holds a master’s degree, works at a private high school in a lower-middle-class area, and teaches A.P. U.S. history. His responses were often brief and somewhat rushed in pacing – rarely pausing to consider his thoughts before speaking, yet he gave his answers confidently and succinctly. Charles was the participant with whom I had the most difficulty establishing a more personal rapport. Being raised in a large, urban, East Coast environment, his speech patterns and response times were fast-paced and curt, and I got the sense that, unlike the other participants, he held little personal interest in the study itself. This conclusion came from multiple responses that were keenly unlike any of the other participants. For example, he stated at the end of the first interview that he appreciated my work but was “in it for the money the whole time….this is my Christmas gifts right here.” Also, unlike the other participants, when given opportunities to ask me questions at the end of each interview, he declined and was seemingly anxious to move on with his day, which is understandable yet distinct from the other eight participants. While I recognize I based this purely on my interpretation of his tone, he was also the only participant who declined to describe himself on a personal level but instead left any self-identifiers to be professional. As an educator, he interpreted himself as “a very flexible teacher when it comes to the way I deliver material…in that I want my students to be the most successful possible.” **Ellen.** Ellen is a white woman in her 30s who teaches 7th and 8th-grade social studies in a rural public school on the United States East Coast. The school she teaches in consists of 100% white students and teachers and is classified as Title 1. Ellen has worked at the school for her entire career and is currently taking courses for a master’s degree in social studies. She plans her curriculum autonomously and has been educated exclusively by white secondary teachers. Ellen was one of two females in the study and the only white woman. She spent time describing her higher education process and how she came into teaching. Ellen went to school for an agricultural degree. After graduation, she obtained a ‘backdoor certification’ wherein she was hired as a teacher with a conditional certificate but needed to complete specific courses and the PRAXIS within two years of teaching. She was hired three days before the start of school and taught several different content areas. Ellen was completing her M.A. in social studies at the time of our interview and was a current NBCT applicant. She held a valuable point of view that was unlike any of the other participants in that she was born, raised, and still lives in a small, rural East Coast town that has a population of 100% white people. Her students, the faculty, and even daily interactions with people at stores or restaurants are 100% white. Being raised and educated with and by white people with no exposure to People of Color was of great interest to me during this study, mainly because she frequently referenced her online master’s courses that focused on racial consciousness. She often acknowledged her lack of exposure to diversity. She, therefore, was “afraid to say the wrong thing because I’m worried I’m going to offend somebody” concerning her course interactions with others in her master’s program. She also described herself as ‘not racist’ during several parts of the interviews\textsuperscript{13}. \textbf{Kenny}. Kenny is a white male in his 30s living on the United States East Coast. He teaches in an urban high school with a highly diverse student body and a faculty/staff consisting of mostly white people. Kenny earned his master’s degree in Urban Education, so he spent much of his graduate coursework learning to teach various racial and socio-economic groups. Kenny identified himself as a “cis-gender white male.” His language usage indicated to me that he considers himself a progressive and inclusive person. He was raised in a highly ranked school district, and within the first ten minutes of interview one, he showed himself to be well-spoken, fluent with academic terminology, and regarded teaching as more of a vocation than a career. He confidently stated that he is: definitely aware of my privilege. I’m not one hundred percent aware of it; I don’t think I ever can be as a person living with it. I’m not sure that I can ever fully grasp what this privilege has done for me, but I can definitely…appreciate that I have it. Kenny discussed early in the first interview his passion for writing, reading, and storytelling, which drew him to social studies. He attended a major university in a large, diverse, East Coast city for his undergraduate and a “crunchy” ivy league school for his teacher education program and master’s degree. \textbf{Midwest Participants.} \textit{Nate}. Nate is a white male in his 40s who has taught social studies for over twenty years. He teaches U.S. history at a suburban, public high school in the Midwest, and his community is \textsuperscript{13} As discussed in previous chapters, ‘not racist’ has become a coined identifier by many antiracists as one who may be in denial of or ignorant towards their inherent bias or racism. The rationale behind this mentality as being ‘code’ for antiracists to capture the racial lens/consciousness of others is the use of the quantifier ‘but’ as in, ‘I’m not racist, but…’ which negates the former statement and validates the latter. This attempts to excuse the racist behavior/thinking/action/mindset at the end of that sentence. See Hawkman, 2020; Kendi, 2020. generally more rural, conservative, and predominantly white. Nate holds a master’s degree and collaborates with his department to design common assessments and pacing guides, yet works autonomously to develop his content methods and resources. As the oldest and the longest-practicing participant, Nate is an experienced practitioner with an ongoing passion for his education and that of his students. He was born and raised on a farm in the Midwest and lives near where he grew up - a small town, two hours from the closest metropolitan city. He chronicled his childhood as “unique,” with grandparents who actively befriended a Black family, “unheard of” in the 1950s rural Midwest. Having been raised with Friends of Color, his mother raised Nate to “look at people’s hearts rather than skin color.” As a result, he is an educator that tries to make his students “feel welcome. Make them feel loved.” He described himself as “very apolitical,” loves “studying other people’s culture,” and sees “people are people, but they’re different.” Steve. Steve is a white male in his 30s who teaches at a public, suburban high school in the Midwest. He has a master’s degree and has taught social studies for 12-15 years. Steve is the only teacher in the study who teaches a U.S. history elective course – *1960s America*—which has 10th through 12th graders. As an elective, he has complete autonomy over his curriculum. He also teaches world history and geography. He has taught and will again soon teach standard American history courses. He articulated that he is interested in non-white populations who are reflective of the students he teaches: I’m interested, like, because my…youth was spent around all white people….I’m very interested in what makes up who they are. And that doesn’t have to do about race, but like, I can see similarities between my youth and white kids. But I can see that there’s a difference….I want to connect with them in whatever way that I can….I’m open to…learning things that, that they’re interested [in]. Thomas. Thomas is a white male high school U.S. history teacher in his 30s from the Midwest. He teaches in a rural, public, middle-class high school with a 95% white student population and 100% white faculty and administration. He has a master’s degree, has been teaching for 8-11 years, and designs his curriculum autonomously. Thomas was raised in a midwestern, middle-class home in an area that has historically voted ‘blue’ but now identifies as ‘Trump country” where farming is prevalent and diversity is not. Politically, he says he is “a moderate” who has “moved farther away from the Republican Party as a result of…nominating Trump and electing him and that platform.” He discussed the extent of his introspection and further education, which exposed him to innovative ideas, particularly regarding class. He labeled himself a “nerd” because he takes an interest in reading about the 1940s and 1950s American housing policies, which he said educated him about his white and economic privilege. Southern Participants. Amina. Amina identifies herself as a South-Asian female in her 20s. She has a bachelor’s degree and teaches at a charter school in a suburban Southern state with a high level of diversity. Amina also is Muslim and wears a headscarf, which she discusses during her interviews. She grew up economically privileged with immigrant parents and lived on the East Coast and in several Southern states. Amina was the youngest participant and the only Person of Color in the study. I was grateful for her involvement, participation, and willingness to share her unique perspective and stories. Amina was an energetic and engaging interviewee, and her excitement was almost contagious. She grew up in the southeastern region of the United States in “white suburbia,” where she attended a charter school in an “affluent part” of a major Southern city. Amina moved to another Southern state a few years ago and has since resided in a suburban area near a major metropolis. She characterized herself as “not very timely,” “not a planner,” and “very visibly different” because she is a darker-skinned Muslim woman who wears a headscarf. While her parents immigrated to the United States, she was born here and raised with values from both cultures. **Nolan.** Nolan is a white male in his 30s teaching at a suburban school near a major city in the Southern region of the United States. His school is highly diverse, with no majority of one racial grouping in the student body, and he teaches 11th grade A.P. U.S. history. Nolan spent time describing experiences in high school when he outwitted his teachers. He relayed several stories about times he either corrected them or came away from their classes to discover that what he had learned from them was inaccurate. He also described his love of learning and quest for knowledge. Nolan expressed having more interest in economic divides than racial/cultural divisions, although he was aware they overlapped. His “love of history more than the kids at the time” brought him into social studies education. He admittedly was confused about race and expressed that his perception of racial identity was more along cultural lines. While Nolan lives in a highly diverse suburb of a major Southern city and has a multi-racial student population, he described himself as “unaware of race as a concept.” He stopped short of identifying himself as colorblind but acknowledged that he is unobservant in noting race when encountering people. **Wyatt.** Wyatt is a white male in his 20s and teaches U.S. history in a Southern state. He is the only participant to hold a doctorate (in Curriculum & Instruction) and is also the most novice educator, with about two years of teaching experience. Wyatt was born and raised in the South, and lives in a highly diverse major Southern city, Wyatt described himself as a “patriot.” He began teaching in January of 2020, just a few weeks before the COVID-19 pandemic would change education’s structure, focus, and method. Having completed research studies for his education, Wyatt was eager and helpful to participate in the study. He recounted his negative experiences as a white male attending a predominantly Black and Latine school during his childhood. Yet, he outlined highly diverse friendships with People of Color in his neighborhood and church, namely Filipino, Vietnamese, and Nigerians. He shared with me that he was still struggling to reconcile his past difficulties with Black and Latino people with his current positive relationships with People of Color. **Data Point 1: Interviews** Since the main datapoint of this study was phenomenological interviews centering around the highly personal and potentially uncomfortable topic of race, I sought previously studies to guide my approach. I primarily used Hawkman’s dissertation (2017) and subsequent work (2020), which evaluated pre-service social studies teachers’ positionality and the lens of their whiteness. In her article *Swimming In and Through Whiteness: Antiracism in Social Studies Teacher Education* (2020), Hawkman describes her findings to be in three distinct modes: participants in the *deep end of the water* who can critique race/sim and whiteness in social studies education; participants who *tread water* – that is people who are wrestling with their evolving racial awareness and cognitive dissonance that came with it (p.16); and those who are *splashing around* – participants who kept an emotional, cautious distance from engaging with and in antiracism. This is one example of how a researcher can assess the racial lens of a participant while at the same time not quantifying that evaluation. To come to these themes and conclusions, she employed interviews, journaling, and data collection methods for her study. When establishing the process for interviews and journaling, Hawkman identified various themes and sample questions to which her pre-service students would respond. I followed Hawkman’s model in my approach to the three-interview process. **Interview One: Background and Experiences.** This interview was needed so that I could specifically collect data about research question one - *how have current high school U.S. history teachers experienced learning about race?* During this interview, I explored the participants’ educational backgrounds and how they learned about race within their educations and upbringing. To prepare for this interview, I utilized data collected from their questionnaire and followed the interview protocol I created (see Appendix H) to focus specifically on their educational background and learning of race within social studies. Because the interview was semi structured, I utilized the interview protocol as a guide rather than a rigid set of questions. In addition, the interview was phenomenological in nature; therefore, I kept my focus on gathering information on if/how the phenomenon of white social studies may or may not have been passed down to the participant. The protocol for the first interview focused on the participants’ 6th through 12th grade social studies educational experiences. I selected this topic strategically so the participant would become comfortable and familiar enough with talking with me about themselves to feel relaxed speaking truthfully, mainly since the interview content was largely biographical. After asking teachers to review their memories of courses, teachers, and their impressions of their classes from sixth grade through college, I laid the foundation for asking more personal questions by asking teachers to reflect critically on their educational experiences with race. To do this, I developed questions such as: • Do you feel your race was appropriately represented, overrepresented, or underrepresented when you learned social studies in the sixth through 12th grades? • Did you sense that any of your educators had racial biases or gravitated towards one race over others? I also focused on how the in/exclusion of race in social studies education may have impacted their perceptions. **Interview Two: Racial Lens.** This interview focused on collecting data to answer research question two: How do current high school U.S. history teachers interpret their racial lens? While the phenomenological interview was the method of data collection, this interview was preempted by a set of journaling entries to allow the participant to self-reflect and examine their outlook on race. For many, mainly white teachers, racial reflection and self-examination is a personal process they have generally not undergone (Hawkman, 2019). Conversely, People of Color are often forced to reflect on their racial identity from an early age and throughout their lives in part to understand the varying inequities and injustices that have been/may be thrust on them based on their skin color. Since white people collectively have not had the same injustices systematically forced upon them, they may not have spent as much time reflecting on their racial identity. The second interview protocol scaffolded questioning to become more personal, focusing on how participants view their race within society and their views of other racial demographics (Appendix I). At the beginning of the interview, I asked the participants about their experiences living within the United States as a Person of Color or white person, stereotypes they may have heard or experienced, and the extent of their daily interaction with people outside their race. I also asked questions about their opinions of current events that brought race to the forefront of national conversations, such as critical race theory, Confederate monuments, and the concept of white privilege. Asking teachers how they felt about these topics, amongst others, was vital to understanding how each participant viewed race relations within the United States. It would also shed light on how aware they were of these types of diverging topics and their sensitivity to the division’s existence. For example, I asked, *What have been your reactions to the racial polarization in the last five years within the United States? Please describe your reactions from your perspective as a social studies teacher, American, and Person of Color/white person.* I anticipated that hearing answers to this question and others like it would give me significant insight into how the participant understood their racial positionality and also offer clarity on their perception of positionailities of people outside their race. In the interview, I also included a section wherein we would discuss their responses to various tools and documents I asked them to review before the interview. Amongst these instruments was the Racial Bias Implicit Association Test (IAT) developed by Harvard’s Project Implicit. The IAT “will measure attitudes and beliefs that people may be unwilling or unable to report” (Project Implicit, 1998, Overview, para. 2). The Race IAT measures race preferences (see Appendix J). While the validity of these tests has been questioned (Lopez, 2017), the results gave me a general idea of the participants’ racial bias based on their feedback. **Interview Three: Curricular Decision-Making.** The first half of interview three explored research question three - how have current secondary American history teachers included or omitted their racial lens from their curriculum? To explore this question as objectively as possible and set a precedent for the interview to be founded on truth and honesty, I began interview three with questions about the participant’s general pedagogical decisionmaking Appendix K). I asked participants first to describe their process when they sat down to plan a unit and how they, as history educators made choices about what aspects of history would be included or excluded from their lessons, given the expanse of the subject. This would encourage the teachers to approach the interview with a systematic and reflective mentality. I also asked more probing questions such as What roadblocks exist in your situation that may prevent you from teaching what or how you want, if any? and to what degree, if at all, do you disclose your political, personal, and social ideologies to your students? As the interview progressed and the level of personal questions intensified, I remained cautious about asking questions as objectively as possible so as not to cast a feeling of judgment on the participants’ answers but instead encourage them to think reflexively about the extent and depth in which they include multiple racial groups in their lessons and units. The second half of interview three focused on research question four - How have these teachers been trained or supported to decentralize the white narrative from American history and include diversity in their curriculum, if at all? Including this research question within the study would allow me to interpret if teachers have ever had formal education addressing white normativity in social studies and if they have even been interested in intentionally and systematically embedding multiple non-white groups in their lessons and units, particularly in non-traditional race-focused topics. I was also curious to know if any of their awareness of antiracism or white privilege came from their exploration or if their administration supported it. Finally, I asked questions about the types of media and books centering on racial topics they had read and whether they have ever had professional development on racism/diversity awareness. I anticipated that these answers would allow me to determine the level of investment participants had in moving towards a curriculum wherein the majority of the racial categorizations are included in every unit. **Data Point 2: Journals** Because exploring one’s racial consciousness is a very personal and reflective process, I wanted the participants to have time to reflect on their positionality privately. Therefore, I designed the journals to supplement and verify the data I would draw from the interviews. My interest in the participants’ responses was not necessarily in their self-classification but rather if their analysis of their racial lens and the level of diversity in their curriculum was consistent with what they shared with me during the interviews. Researchers use journals instead of diaries to examine emotion, introspection, and self-reflection, particularly with sensitive or taboo topics (Smith-Sullivan, 2008). I asked participants to engage with specific texts and respond to predetermined prompts so they could reflect expressly on their racial lens. Because determining and acknowledging the racial lens is a very personal process, I did not ask participants in the journals or interviews to identify their findings but rather discuss their thought processes and responses. This activity was primarily meant to prompt the participant to consider their worldview regarding race and allow them time to reflect on their opinions. Edwards (2019) described the value of reflective journaling as a contemplative practice in her chapter *Contemplative Pedagogies for Transformative Teaching, Learning, and Being*. In addition, she describes her experiences with journaling throughout her career and the social-emotional development within a social justice frame, allowing her to explore her thoughts and feelings on complex topics. …I would frequently find myself flustered with feelings of anger, resentment, or frustration when I encountered prejudice, ignorance, or even critique. However, because of my reflective journaling practice, I find myself becoming more grounded and self-confident, less reactionary, better able to articulate my perspectives, and, as a whole, more equipped to pursue this work (p.333). Certainly, journaling is a reflective process for any person, let alone a teacher examining their racial lens and its impact on decisions in their classroom. I expected it would offer insight into their innermost thoughts and processes in dealing with complex realities. To guide participants on their path of self-reflection, I offered them a series of journal prompts (see Appendix L) that required them to interact and react to racial consciousness documents (see Appendices A, B, and C). While many racial lens spectrums have been created to help explain the continuum of racism and racial consciousness (see Chapter 1), there is no empirically tested and widely used method for evaluating other people’s racial lenses, nor, arguably, should there be one. Evaluating and determining racial lens and the levels to which one may hold racial bias is a personal, introspective process. It is not for others to assess on behalf of those with whom they interact. This is not to say that participants’ racial lens cannot be examined or even evaluated or corroborated, as in the case of this study. However, a tool to do so has not yet been scientifically validated. Some of these prompts centered on their experience taking the discussed Racial Bias IAT. The prompts asked for their responses to the assessment process and its outcomes. It is essential to understand that for this study, I was not interested in the result of the test but rather the participants’ *reaction* to their result – looking for attitudes of defensiveness, confidence, surprise, comfort, relief, confusion, or distress. Other prompts asked participants about their responses to Peggy McIntosh’s (1989) essay *White Privilege: Unpacking the Invisible Knapsack*, the *Race Reporting Guide* from Race Forward (2015) (see Appendix N for both documents) and Hesse’s *The 8 White Identities* (Sargent & Rico, 2021a, 2021b). I had 10 journal prompts (see Appendix L) that guided and revealed the participants’ thinking about their positionality and racial lens with how they see themselves. These prompts were sent and received via email throughout the 2 months I collected interview data. **Data Point 3: Curriculum Analysis** I conducted a curricula analysis to gauge the scope by which participants include/omit their racial lens from their curricula and verify that the answers they gave me during the interviews were not inaccurate or misrepresented. Document analysis is a widely used qualitative research design method and is often used in conjunction with interviews further to substantiate the data (Kahlke, 2014). I was examining the conduit between the teacher and their students. Because documents serve as receptacles of instruction and agents of purpose (Prior, 2008), they were the best resource for me to examine the intention authentically and potential outcomes of if/how teachers impart their racial lens within their curricula. While a complete curriculum analysis is beyond this study’s scope, I requested the best samples of each participant’s curriculum they used over the semester, according to specific criteria (see Appendix O). These samples ranged from lesson plans, primary/secondary resources, assignments, assessments, or other materials that may reflect the teacher’s curriculum for the semester. These samples were collected either via email or uploaded to Google Classroom. I designed a rubric (see Appendix P) that evaluated the curriculum. I based my design and criteria of the rubric on several existing models of racially-inclusive curriculum building: The Metropolitan Center for Research on Equity and the Transformation of Schools (2019), The Equity Literacy Institute (2017), and Hanover Research (2020). I also drew from several scholarly works that defined multi-cultural content (Banks, 2019; Gorski, 2009) and its best implementation practices (Chandler & Hawley, 2017; Darling-Hammond, 2017; Gorski, 2017, Shelton & Melchior, 2018). My rubric notes specific instances where examples from multiple race groups from history are included or excluded, allowing me to determine if the curriculum focuses specifically on white people, non-white people, or some of each. The rubric has six criteria: Content: Variety; Content: Perspective; Content: Equity; Normalization; Critical Lens; and Impact on Diverse Perspectives. Assessing impact is also necessary when evaluating curriculum (Jansen & Reddy, 1994). I also specifically chose to offer ‘bonus points’ for authorship of resources that included BIPOC. This last section was offered as bonus points because I am aware that some white scholars are intentional about critically examining whiteness and unburdening People of Color who often carry the arduous task of decentralizing the white narrative in social studies (Chandler & Branscombe, 2015; Hawkman, 2017; Zinn, 2008; 2020). That is an essential aspect of critical whiteness and moving towards antiracism. Ideally, social studies educators learn from these scholars how to work towards dismantling white normativity in secondary social studies. At the same time, I wanted to encourage and emphasize the importance of Authors of Color, particularly when it comes to the writing and teaching of their own racial group’s history. Therefore, in my curriculum evaluation, if a participant’s curriculum reflected authorship by People of Color, they were allotted extra points in determining if their curriculum reflected a multiple race narrative. This system also ensured teachers were not penalized if I could not determine the race of the documents/curriculum provided to me in the sampling. **Data Point 4: Survey** Because of this study’s qualitative nature and the subjectivity expected to occur when examining how teachers’ racial lens may impact their curricular choices, I wanted to find another data point that would validate and ensure consistency amongst the interview answers given by my participants. The survey was a summation of the entire three-interview process. It asked teachers to reflect on their learning of race, their perspectives on contemporary racial topics, and their inclusion/omission of racial perspectives within their content. I also wanted to compare the data I would be collecting from my participants with that of other secondary social studies teachers across the United States. Therefore, I created a brief survey that was not only taken by the nine participants upon concluding their final interview but also distributed over social media. The survey (see Appendix Q) consisted of 30 questions: 12 regarding demographics and personal information, three regarding their own educational experiences in learning social studies, seven regarding their personal beliefs on issues surrounding race, and eight regarding their pedagogical beliefs and practices. The survey was available over Qualtrics XM and took an average of ten minutes to be completed. **Data Analysis** I chose phenomenological interview as the primary data point to capture the essence of the participants’ firsthand experiences, curricular decision-making, and interpretations of their perceptions of race. Because of the richness of my data collection, I took extreme care during the data collection and analysis to ensure I was as thorough and open as possible. Consequently, the data analysis phase lasted from January through April 2022. Due to the depth of the results, I have opted to include my process for data analysis in this chapter. **Interviews** Participants were informed before each interview that I would assign them a pseudonym. Their location and other identifying factors would be kept confidential to protect their identity and encourage them to be open and honest about their experiences, beliefs, and racial acumen. I used Zoom and Otter.ai to conduct all interviews with audio and visual recording, with the participants’ consent and knowledge. During the interviews, I wrote memos (Miles et al., 1994; Saldaña, 2021) to record my initial findings, noting poignant quotes from the participants and taking note of prevailing emotions, language, and sentiments relayed during the interviews. Immediately after each interview, I continued my memo-writing to note my initial impressions and critical moments that stood out to me as I reflected on the conversation. I also took notes about each participant’s overall impression in my memos. I developed descriptive initial codes from this practice that would later initiate the entire coding cycle. I then was able to code according to Hahn’s (2008) levels of coding (see Figure 4) with multiple cycles per level (see Tables 2, 3 and 4). Once I completed all the interviews, I employed an exploratory method—provisional coding—which allowed me to begin the coding cycles (Miles et al., 1994). First, I applied structured coding with the list of predetermined, a priori codes I anticipated using based on my research questions and what I had already heard repeated throughout the interviews. I read through the interview transcripts during the first level of coding with this list of codes collected on NVivo 12 data analysis software. Then, as I read the transcripts line-by-line, seeking the predetermined codes, I applied open coding by remaining watchful for emergent codes and subcodes that arose from the data. Figure 5 Hahn’s Level of Coding Note. Reprinted figure from Hahn, C. (2008). *Doing qualitative research using your computer*. SAGE Publications, Ltd. Table 2 Process for Interview Data Collection and Analysis | Analysis Method | First Cycle → | Identified Codes | Second Cycle → | Identified Codes | |-----------------|---------------|-----------------|----------------|-----------------| | **Level 1 Coding** | Provisional coding → | Attitude Physical Location RQ1 – Learn about Race Documents RQ2 – Racial Lens RQ3 – Practice RQ4 - Training | Structured coding → | Attitude – Positive/Negative RQ1 – Diversity Exposure RQ1 – Teacher Ed. program RQ2 – Awareness RQ2 – Mentality RQ2 – Positionality | | | Descriptive initial codes → | Effects of Study Journals Race-Based Terms Pedagogy | Open coding → | Effects of Study: - Effects of Journaling - Future Choices - No Change - Positive Race-Based Terms: - Ally - Antiracist - Not-Racist - Ignorance - Lack of Exposure | | Level 2 Coding | In vivo coding → “I’m not racist but…” “People are people” | Values Coding → Bias Apathy Antiracist thinking Ally thinking Racial Lens Toward Others Diversity Privilege Whiteness | |----------------|----------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Level 3 Coding | Narrowed codes, subcodes, and simultaneous codes → Secondary Ed Experience Higher Ed Experience TEP Professional Experience Racial Lens – aware Racial Lens – unaware Racial Lens - apathy | Developed themes and subthemes → Support Roadblock Levels of Exposure to Race Personal Interest Racial Optics - Consciousness - Subconsciousness - Apathy | | Level 4 Coding | Evaluate all underlining themes to determine the common theoretical concepts: white normalization; racial optics; learning about race; hinderances; pedagogy + practice | Note: The above codes are not an exhaustive list of the codes I utilized and discovered, as the full listing of codes, subcodes and simultaneous codes was 179. However, I provide a general overview of the codes I most frequently utilized to offer the reader an example of the process I used. Table 3 Process for Journals Data Collection and Data Analysis | Level 1 Coding | Data | Identified Codes | |----------------|------|-----------------| | | Open coding for descriptive codes | 8 white identities IAT McIntosh Race Forward Racism Scale Spectrum | | Level 2 Coding | Applied a priori codes from interviews | “I’m not racist but…” “People are people” | | Level 3 Coding | Narrowed codes, subcodes, and simultaneous codes | Whiteness, awareness, whitewash, white identity, | | Level 4 Coding | Evaluated all underlining themes to determine common theoretical concepts | Racial identity, racial lens, perspective, awareness, | Note: The above codes are not an exhaustive list of the codes I utilized and discovered, as the full listing of codes, subcodes and simultaneous codes was 179. However, I provided a general overview of the codes I most frequently utilized to offer the reader an example of the process I used. Table 4 Process for Curriculum Analysis Data | Prior to Data Collection | Data Collection | Data Analysis | |--------------------------|-----------------|---------------| | Level 1 | Conducted prior to curriculum analysis | Developed curriculum profile for each participant | | Level 2 | Conducted during curriculum analysis | Analyzed using rubric | | Level 3 | Post curriculum analysis | Determined the level of non-white perspectives and history present in the curriculum using the rubrics scoring guide | I moved into the second level of coding, using in vivo coding to identify significant phrases and quotations, referring to my memos for guidance, and remaining open to new quotes and words that emerged. I continued to cycle through the data, each time searching for new codes that may have been overlooked. Toward the end of the second level, I applied a method to find specific data that concentrated on values. Values coding allows the researcher to explore cultural values, identity, and intra- and interpersonal participant experiences and actions (Miles et al., 1994). After cycling through the data several times, I moved on to level three coding, in which I narrowed and condensed my codes, subcodes, and simultaneous codes to develop themes and subthemes for the interview data set. Finally, at level four, I narrowed my theme to the main findings informed by all the data points. During the study, the participants engaged with three sets of journal prompts (see Appendix L). After completing the interviews and collecting all three journal entries via Google Classroom, I spent the first coding cycle utilizing open coding to develop descriptive codes and noted compelling quotations from the text. During the second coding cycle, I used the *a priori* codes I had listed in NVivo for the interviews and drew out text that applied to those codes. I then condensed and narrowed my codes and subcodes and engaged with the text using discourse analysis, examining how the participants expressed themselves in the journals versus what they said during the interviews. **Curriculum Analysis** The curriculum samples were submitted via Google Classroom or emailed directly to me. I approached the data analysis process for the curricula as I would for any curriculum analysis based on Fitzpatrick et al.’s (2011) program evaluation. I created an assessment rubric and corresponding scoring guide (Appendix P) that would allow me to view the complete set of documents I collected from each participant. I would also use it to determine how the curriculum reflected a multiple-race approach toward U.S. history, if at all. I created the rubric based on several established sources that have been used to analyze curricula for multiple racial groups to teach students history (Lawrence & Tatum, n.d.; Parker & Wilsea, 2019; Tatum, 1992.). Additionally, I consulted resources from existing curriculum designs and evaluations from the NYU Metropolitan Center for Research on Equity and the Transformation of Schools (2019), the Interaction Institute for Social Change (2019), the Education Justice Research and Organizing Collaborative (2020), and the New York Collective of Radical Educators and Inquiry Action Groups (2009). Using the rubric as the final assessment of the curriculum analysis, I took a backward-design approach (Wiggins & McTigue, 1998) and then created a curriculum profile for each participant (see Figure 5), which included each document (or entire units for those that offered their comprehensive course curriculum). After sorting through all the documents given to me by the participant and after creating their curriculum profile, I utilized the rubric based on criteria from several established sources. I reviewed criteria from the aforementioned organizations and resources and merged overlapping ideas to reflect the best of these tools in assessing if and how racial diversity is present in the curriculum. These sources based their curricular analyses on criteria such as a variety of BIPOC voices being conveyed in the content, the authorship of classroom resources to include People of Color, and the perspective from which the content is seemingly presented, among others. After completing the rubric based on the curriculum profile, I tallied each participant’s scores using the guide I designed and concluded the levels of non-white perspectives and history within each participant’s curriculum. I then compared how the participants described their curriculum and its racially diverse perspectives in their journals and interviews with what their curriculum reflected. I developed this curriculum profile based on resources from the following: NYU Metropolitan Center for Research on Equity and the Transformation of Schools (2019), the Interaction Institute for Social Change (2019), the Education Justice Research and Organizing Collaborative (2020), and the New York Collective of Radical Educators and Inquiry Action Groups (2009). Survey The survey was available from December 2021 through the end of March 2022. I posted to various social media forums and groups whose participants were social studies educators. While I posted in four Reddit groups, most of my focus was on Facebook private and public social studies educator groups and pages. I requested permission to post the survey to 28 various groups and pages, and 17 of those approved the posting in their groups.\(^{14}\) Several groups have \(^{14}\) I only posted in groups with a neutral or open target audience to ensure the data was not biased. For example, I purposefully posted in groups with titles like ‘History Teachers,’ ‘NBCT Social Studies Support Group,’ or ‘Social Studies Critical Thinking’ but intentionally did not post to groups with names such as ‘Antiracist Educators Forum’ or ‘Black Educators Rock Social Studies’ because I did not want to target my audience based on their race or ideals. over 1000 members, bringing the total exposure of the survey to over 10,000 social studies teachers. I received 425 survey submissions, 349 of which I accepted based on the completion rate of over 50%. The participants partaking in the survey were from various locations and racial groups (Appendix T). Similar to the study’s interview participants, the only requirements to participate in the survey were that they are current secondary social studies teachers, sign the consent form, and were educated within the United States during their sixth- through 12th-grade experiences. **Quality of the Study** **Ethics** Transparency is an essential link between ethical research and reflexivity (Etherington, 2007). Transparency is a vital component of the integrity of the research process. I withheld my antiracist positionality and passion to avoid swaying the participants or causing them to alter their responses to appease or deflect my emotional responses. Maintaining objectivity as a researcher is essential for this research to have authenticity. Gilligan (1982) noted, ‘Reflexive relational ethics pays attention to the balance required between our own needs as researchers and our obligations toward, care for, and connection with those who participate in our research’ (as cited in Etherington, 2007, p. 614). I acknowledge that, as a researcher, I have an existing bias for antiracist curricula and an awareness of my white privilege. Therefore, I must recognize how to regard these biases for ethical consideration (Suzuki et al., 2007). I already knew about my biases; consequently, I reviewed the interview questions and document analyses with third-party individuals aware of the study whose personal racial lenses are unknown to me. In addition, five committee members reviewed my interview questions and protocols for data analysis and pointed out potential conflicts or unintentional bias. Reflexivity is a skill and a tool by which I can include myself within the context of my theory, research, or interpretations. Reflexivity allows for remaining transparency about personal values and beliefs and their influence on the research process (Etherington, 2007; Pillow, 2003). I worked to stay sensitive to the cultural differences in my participants’ histories or values. I am also aware of my positionality as a white female researcher; as such, I have considered that those descriptors, mainly *white* and *female*, could have influenced the comfort and candor of the interview participants. I first established myself as a social studies educator with the participants, so they felt comfortable with our commonality. **Trustworthiness** Trustworthiness is one-way qualitative researchers show themselves and their work worthy of attention from the field (Lincoln & Guba, 1985; Nowell et al., 2017). This study’s credibility required several realities. Primarily, prolonged engagement occurred over a year, from start to finish, with a data collection phase over several months and the data analysis lasting twice as long. Second, the data collection underwent triangulation with several data points, overlapping their insight to address the research questions. Finally, the study underwent a peer review with five committee scholars. The committee scholars are individuals professionally and ethically bound to ensure that the research process, methods, tools, findings, and interpretations are to the highest standards of scholarly work. **Reliability and Validity** Reliability and validity have distinct roles in qualitative and quantitative research. Reliability in qualitative research is the quality of the study to produce understanding Validity in qualitative research consists of gaining knowledge and experience of the phenomenon’s meaning, attributes, and characteristics (Agar, 1986; Krefting, 1991). The similarities of the terms have caused researchers to couple them in qualitative research (Golafashani, 2003). However, Maxwell (2013) contended that clarity and the value of each require the inclusion of validity in a separate section. Therefore, I approached these two concepts separately to substantiate my declarations of the study’s reliability and validity. **Reliability.** Merriam’s (1998) definition of reliability is ‘the extent to which research findings can be replicated’ (p. 205). While this study occurred in a unique place and time, other scholars can certainly repeat its procedures, methods, and tools. Like all human-based research, the findings will vary from person to person, and a level of generalizability should be considered. All people have some level of insight or ability to analyze their racial lens and consciousness or can do so. Similarly, while teachers’ curricular decision-making is a process unique to each individual, the process itself generally stems from the teachers’ training programs, departmental allowances, pedagogical goals, and state standards that all teachers undergo. Lincoln and Guba (1985) contended that considering the reliability of a study requires examining the consistency of the data results. They noted that it is less pertinent for other researchers to produce the same results because studies with human participants result in unique data. Instead, others will validate the study’s results as consistent and dependable. **Validity.** Merriam (1998) described many researchers’ faulty logic that reliability and validity are exchangeable. As such, they could assume that a study ‘is more valid if repeated observations in the same study or replications of the entire study have produced the same results’ (p. 205). Scholars can, of course, repeat being wrong. Internal validity for this study occurred via triangulation, peer examination, detailed, thick descriptions, and researcher biases (Merriam, 1998). External validity, or generalizability, does not require scholars to replicate the findings from single cases or specific situations with other people. Instead, it requires researchers to replicate the methods and procedures in studying the effects of the shared phenomenon on various people within the inclusion criteria. That said, scholars will have the ability to and should replicate this study for further research on the phenomenon of WSS. At the same time, this study and its findings have their basis in solid foundations and methods. The results are meaningful and insightful for teachers, researchers, policymakers, and teacher educators. **Triangulation** Multiple types of data collection strategies occurred in this study. The approaches overlapped in terms of thematic analysis. Therefore, data triangulation reduced the risk that the conclusions would reflect the bias of one specific method. Triangulation also provided the opportunity to gain a deeper understanding of the phenomenon of WSS (Maxwell, 2013). Triangulation does not always result in reduced bias (Suzuki et al., 2007). However, this study’s extensive and varied methods produced unbiased conclusions on the impact of a racial lens on educators’ curricular choices. **Limitations** A beneficial data point of this study would be to evaluate the participants’ students to discover the impact of their teachers’ racial lenses and curricular decisions. After all, learning about the effects on student behavior and perceptions of race could contribute to the broader purpose and indicate the influence of a teacher’s racial lens and curricular choices on students’ views and understanding of race. However, such a study would be a significant undertaking. Although this is an important topic to explore, examining students’ learned perceptions first requires evaluating their social studies knowledge source: their teachers. **Positionality and Subjectivity Statements** Traditional methodological approaches require the role of the researcher to remain distant from the participants. However, Sherif (2001) noted that scholars have begun to recognize the effects of a researcher’s self on ‘every aspect of the research process, from conception to final interpretation’ (p. 437). Thus, there is a blurred boundary between my role as the researcher and my positionality in this study. A vital need exists to establish researchers’ positionality for themselves and the reader regarding insider/outsider status (Sherif, 2001; Suzuki et al., 2007). I must also identify the potential effects of firsthand experiences, beliefs, and professional predispositions on the research so readers can remain aware and critical of this study’s credibility (Preissle, 2008). Therefore, I have identified and will now articulate my personal, professional, and researcher positionality and acknowledge any potential subjectivity. **Professional** I decided to become a secondary social studies teacher for two main reasons. The first was to ensure that U.S. high school students did not learn a false narrative of U.S. history as I had. The U.S. history I learned contained the whitewashing of the nation’s development, the over-sensationalism of U.S. patriotism, and the omission of various multi-cultural perspectives. Second, I wanted to teach and inspire high schoolers to be participatory, informed citizens. Fifteen years after my initial certification, these motives remain just as pressing. I intentionally spent all my teaching career in low socio-economic schools with highly diverse populations to develop my identity and a continued commitment to social justice, tolerance, and a global perspective. I am a middle-class white woman who believes in activism. I’ve made concerted efforts to ensure I work at schools with at-risk populations to inspire and guide students to learn a fully informed history. Students must understand the context of today’s societal problems, many of which Students of Color confront daily, to work toward a remedy. **Educational** I must discuss my background with learning about and experiencing the phenomenon of white social studies, which was my motivation to become a social studies teacher and conceptualize my white privilege. As a first-year college student, I first read Zinn’s (1980) *A People’s History of the United States*. I felt disgusted that I had 12 years of education and had even taken A.P. U.S. History but had never learned about the atrocities of Columbus and his false glorification for discovering a country upon which he never actually set foot. My exposure to this book, given to me by my first Black educator, caused me to feel disturbed and enraged by Columbus’s depiction as a U.S. hero and his award of a national holiday. At that time, I decided to major in history to learn about the other myths I had been taught throughout my education to develop a national identity (Barton & Levstik, 2004). Sadly, I learned that many myths were the means of either glorifying white people or wholly ignoring People of Color (Loewen, 1995; Zinn, 1980). Through self-reflexive examination (Etherington, 2007; Pillow, 2003), my understanding of U.S. soldiers (from the Revolution through World War II), technological innovations in communications or travel, and even cowboys of the Wild West caused me to maintain imagery of white people because of how I was taught. I argue that I have been educated with a lens of white superiority; as such, my educators did not pass down BIPOC stories and counternarratives as a normative frame of understanding. I do not believe that my educators did this intentionally, as I have (unintentionally) taught in the same manner. When embarking on an action research project amidst my doctoral coursework (Muhlenfeld-Johnson, 2020), I experienced the situated mentality that I needed to examine my choices and practices, positionality, and white superiority. As a result, I actively chose to take on an antiracist process. Therefore, I fully understand how personal this process of self-reflection could be. However, I also recognize its significance for transforming how teachers perceive themselves and the present culture and informing future generations of the whole truths of history. **Personal** My relationship with racism in the United States is not the result of personal experience, which may or may not be a limiting factor. However, I have spent much time listening to friends and Students of Color, which has developed my empathy and awareness of how they have reconciled and combatted the prejudices they encounter. As a social studies teacher, lifelong student of history, and military wife of a combat veteran, I have a deeply rooted and personal commitment to activism and action. I take great interest in the United States’ victories, as well as its mistakes and intentional atrocities; I believe that this is an essential aspect of genuine and authentic patriotism. I also strive to help improve its future by teaching students to approach their civic participation proactively and intelligently via volunteerism, service, voting, engagement, or schooling. I have naturally merged my three passions: education, social justice, and active citizenship. Another area of identity that could have influenced this study was my pedagogy of place (Gruenewald, 2003). As a Chicago native raised in highly diverse and multi-cultural areas with little exposure to racism against my person, diverse and integrated populations feel most natural. In addition, the people who surrounded me throughout my life - my parents, friends, and husband - all grew up in similar environments with integrated mentalities and natural expectations of diversity, tolerance, and inclusion for race, sexual orientation, class, or religion. A recent relocation to Alabama caused me to open my eyes to the uniqueness of such an upbringing. I have gained a heightened awareness of the polarization felt in many parts of the country where individuals still engage in and tolerate racism. Seeing Confederate flags and monuments daily has been a new and highly uncomfortable experience, providing me with a firsthand awareness that racial divides still exist. Additionally, some individuals still celebrate racial divides as personal and cultural history with much pride. For the first time, I now grasp how it is that racism has persisted more than 150 years after emancipation. As a social studies educator taught and trained in the North by many Professors of Color, I look to my teacher counterparts in the South for their part in perpetuating and immobilizing this phenomenon. **Researcher** I am a highly reflective educator and have received formal training in research. I have often reflected on my effectiveness and methods as a National Board-Certified Teacher and a doctoral student. Therefore, I am aware of my vital role as an instrument of inquiry in this area. I know that discussing such a personal topic and asking colleagues to examine their potential biases would first require me to explore my practices, choices, and perspectives to model and determine the unintentional impact of my prejudices on my practice and students. Exploring these complex topics and learning the transformative nature of such a process requires me to examine the repercussions of white normativity on social studies education as a researcher, scholar, educator, American, and white person. For this reason, I have directed much of my academic research to explore a better understanding of the intersectionality between racial lens and pedagogical and curricular decision-making. My intention and passion are to continue this research beyond this study’s scope to define, understand, and explore the occurrences of WSS, teachers’ potential impact on WSS, and its lasting impact on the impressionable minds of the youth in the United States. **Summary** Chapter 1 presented the background and existing problematic phenomenon of WSS. Chapter 2 indicated the study’s theoretical framework of CWT, which will frame how teachers develop their racial lenses and impact on curricular choices. This chapter included the study’s methodology, participant selection, data collection and analysis, and the various aspects that will show the quality of the study. Chapters 4, 5, and 6 present the data analysis process findings, and Chapter 7 will complete the study with a discussion of the results and their meanings and impact. CHAPTER 4 FINDINGS 1: TEACHERS’ EXPERIENCES LEARNING ABOUT RACE This study focuses on the educational experiences that contributed to social studies teachers’ racial lens, consciousness development, and how said understanding may impact their curricular decisions. Learning about and questioning the racial perspectives of others and oneself is a highly personal endeavor. Nevertheless, critical phenomenological interviews and supporting data in journals, curriculum samples, and a survey allowed me to draw out valuable themes with the participants’ commonalities and variations. In this chapter, I address the first of three significant findings of my data collection and analysis: social studies teachers’ experiences in learning about race in their education. While this chapter will address research questions one and four, the next chapter will address questions two and three. Chapter 6 will also contain findings from the study, namely the intersection of Chapters 4 and 5. Finally, in Chapter 7, I conclude the study and discuss possible implications it may have on the field. To begin this chapter, I will first describe the nine participants more thoroughly, dividing them by region and describing their general experiences in secondary and higher education. I will then discuss the participants’ experiences in learning about race in several contexts: a) secondary social studies, b) higher education, c) professional development, and d) individual experiences. Then I will conclude by answering questions two and three of these four research questions: 1. How have current secondary US history teachers experienced learning about race? 2. How do current high school US history teachers interpret their racial lenses and consciousnesses? 3. How have these US history teachers included or omitted their views of race within their curriculum? 4. How have these teachers been guided on decentralizing the white narrative from American history or including diversity in their curriculum, if at all? This chapter describes the participants’ memories regarding their education specifically related to topics involving or excluding race, including notable differences between their secondary levels and higher education experiences. I will then discuss the participants’ professional experiences in learning about race, including antiracism, diversity awareness, and other support (or lack thereof) offered by colleagues and administrators. Finally, I will present the findings on how the participants experienced learning about race on a personal level, identifying common themes and outliers. As explained in Chapter 3, these participants were selected solely on their fulfillment of the participant criterion and their successful completion of the initial components of the application and participation process. I did not include race, gender, religion, marital status, or sexual identity/orientation in my selection process because I did not want those criteria to influence it. I intentionally designed interview one to collect information about the participants’ educational experiences and the impact diversity inclusion (or exclusion) may have had on the long-term memories of each person. I began the interviews by asking teachers about their education to hear their answers and establish an essential professional yet friendly rapport with each participant. I wanted to disarm potential anxieties and ensure they were comfortable speaking with me candidly about their views and experiences with race, as would be the subject of interviews two and three. To that end, I spent intentional time becoming familiar with their biographical background and chose to discuss their social studies educations because it would be a topic I expected they would easily relate to, feel comfortable and familiar with, and be able to connect with me on because I informed them that I was also a social studies educator. Interview one allowed me to understand the context and experiences that would serve as a foundation for their racial lens and consciousness. After completing the interviews, I sorted through the data with several coding levels and methods rounds described in the previous chapter, and several major themes emerged. Ultimately, one of the significant findings of this study is regarding the various experiences and impacts these secondary social studies teachers had when they were in a student-learning situation, be it secondary level, higher level, professional level, or personal level. To maintain the integrity of the study, I have centered the participants’ voices intentionally throughout this and the following two chapters to allow them to speak for themselves and minimize any researcher bias. The impact of secondary social studies teachers on their students’ awareness and perceptions of freedom, justice, citizenship, and equality cannot be understated (Alongi et al., 2016; Ho & Barton, 2020; Knowles, 2019). It stands to reason that those students may similarly be influenced regarding sociological areas such as gender or race. Because social studies teachers are gatekeepers of students’ learning of America’s history (Hawley & Whitman, 2020; Thornton, 1989), it is necessary to examine teachers’ perceptions of race to determine if they influence what and how they teach. In this study, the nine teachers that participated were all educated within the United States by primarily white teachers. This chapter will present how their social studies experiences may have influenced their perceptions of race. Educational Experiences as a Student Like any social studies teacher in the United States, the standard curriculum for learning social studies includes some variations of taking a course in world history and a course in U.S. history at both the middle-school level (sixth through eighth grade) as well as at the high-school level (ninth- through 12th grade). However, the amount, timeframe of historical context, specific content covered, and the assessment are all based on state standards, district guidelines, and departmental practices. That is to say, there are no national-level curriculum or content standards mandated for social studies at the secondary level. While the NCSS first published its national curriculum standards in 1994, these serve as guidelines and are not federally enforced.
