Similarly, while the NCSS has teaching standards used by various TEPs and state-level teacher requirements, they are not mandated and, therefore, not uniform across all 50 states. Secondary Education Experiences The Impact of Nondiversity in Content. I fully acknowledge that each participant recalled their experiences of the impact of diversity within their secondary education in a highly selective manner due to the amount of time that had passed since they were secondary students. While I had informed the participants that we would discuss their experiences in secondary social studies during the first interview, they were not prepared to recall how much and in what specific ways race was taught. I purposefully planned the interview this way because I wanted to capture the lasting impressions their educations had left them with rather than allow them to explore their recollections more deeply. The nine participants all recalled learning about race in one of two very distinct ways. The first is that several participants were left with a vivid impression that American history was positioned to centralize the white narrative while simultaneously avoiding the equitable inclusion of the stories of People of Color. For example, Nolan did not hesitate to describe how his secondary classes left him with the impression that Black people had already won equality with the Civil Rights Movement, indicating that there had been no further racial issues after that: There was really a lack of any kind of discussion about it. It wasn’t really something that I was consciously aware of…not that like, I didn’t know….different colors, and race and everything. But it was never really discussed or brought up. Like geography, it’s different cultures, obviously. Things like slavery were brought up, and maybe a little discrimination against freed Africans. But it was always something that was very in the past, like, Oh, that’s not today. Today, everything’s fine. And we’re all equal [emphasis added]. That was certainly the impression that I got. Though inaccurate, his experience in a Southern, suburban environment was much more advanced than Nate, a participant raised in a small rural midwestern town. Nate is the eldest participant and the newest to antiracism ideals. We never got past the Civil War….in all my classes in school. So that was the only time that it [race] was brought up. In slavery and plantations in the antebellum South, maybe Crispus Attucks in the American Revolution…and Native Americans, of course, during colonial times and westward expansion. So, I guess if we did talk about it, it was because it was in the book, or we read about it. Kenny is an East Coast teacher who grew up in one of the highest-ranking suburban districts in the country at the time and, therefore, he reported, was very “academically-oriented.” Of all the participants, Kenny was perhaps the most adamant about the absence of diversity in his learning of history, with multiple references in answering several different questions: I would say there wasn’t any real conscious effort by my teachers at that time to teach race or to teach you about racialized issues in any real sense of the word. … Even in Civil Rights—it was presented as, you know, these people back then did racist things. And then we fixed it, and it’s done. … So I remember not being exposed to talk about race, or issues between races at all, or like this idea of…race consciousness. It was not really a thing. It was just everything was taught and taken from a white perspective and presented as fact—this is the way it was. While Kenny reported not being familiar with Chandler and Branscombe’s (2015) white social studies framework, he described how he was still left with the impression that his secondary social studies experiences were precisely that: It was painted; it was so whitewashed that it was hard to see it as biased. Or it’s hard to pick up its bias because it just was presented as the way it was, right? And there was no counterpoint or counterviews… It was all through a white middle-class perspective. There was no sense of class consciousness either. No racial consciousness or class consciousness. It was just kind of like this happened. While participants’ recollections and takeaways from their secondary social studies courses may have varied, none reported remembering the inclusion of multiple races in non-traditionally taught historical topics. Some participants attributed this partially to the textbook: Amina also faults the curriculum: “it was kind of the standard. … Follow your textbook, and your textbook does not highlight different races. I feel like we just followed the one storyline.” **Impact of Educators.** For other participants, their teacher significantly influenced the curricular presentation (or lack thereof) of race. It was evident throughout the interviews that the participants had sympathy for their former educators, themselves now realizing the amount of stress and workload on teachers’ shoulders. A few participants outright defended their teachers for not being more racially inclusive. Charles reflected on his learning about the segregation of the Army during World War I: “I think… they tried their best. It was 2003. They were teaching from a textbook, you know? I think they probably made like $32,000.” Ellen attributed her teachers’ lack of diversity in the curriculum to the curriculum itself, not stating if she was aware of who developed the curriculum: “I wouldn’t say it was that my teachers were intentionally trying to, you know, push an agenda or anything, I feel like their curriculum, probably, at the time, presented it that way.” While Charles was explicitly reflecting on a beloved white teacher prior, he went on to expand his reflection to his overall learning experience in secondary social studies and the impact it had on him. He stated that he felt he’d “lucked out” to have had a smaller portion of his learning focused on the history of marginalized people, despite his education taking place in a large, diverse, East Coast city: I would say I probably wasn’t taught enough about the privilege of the white people at the time. It was more the positive stories and the glorified stuff. And then I lucked out to some 20% to 35% knowledge of smaller marginalized groups in history and things like that. And really, if I look back at it now, I’m a little upset that they hid those things from me; I would have rather got the whole picture. His white teacher’s negligence also impacted Nolan in presenting varied racial perspectives in American history and historically accurate perceptions of truth. Instead, he noted that his distrust of education resulted directly from the learning he received in his suburban Southern school: I remember eighth grade; she was my US History teacher for kind of the first half of American history. And I still remember going home and telling my parents she’s like, ‘Oh, the Civil War was not about slavery, it was about states’ rights,’ which I believed for a while, until it’s like, oh, wait, the state’s right to what? And I read the Cornerstone Speech in college, and I’m like, ‘wow, I don’t know what else from that [course] I can actually trust now.’ **Outliers.** While most participants recalled secondary social studies emphasizing white normativity, there were two outliers in two distinct yet relatable aspects. First, Charles detailed how he began to understand the inequality between racial groups in secondary US history: He would kind of point out different racial disparities and certain things throughout history as we discussed, and he did it in just a really awesome informational way…And I remember the one that really stuck with me and led me more was that People of Color didn’t get any of the New Deal programs during the Great Depression, and I was like, damn, well, that’s generational wealth right there. I just remember that always stuck with me. I was like, wow, that makes a lot of sense. So that kind of just led me to a book to just kind of dive in a little deeper history of just trying to understand all the aspects of all people in history. Though Charles did not indicate if there was anything other than the teacher that seemed to influence this curricular perspective, Wyatt interpreted his experiences with learning about race in his AP US History course: they were more geared towards college-level content, as well as teaching…the skills. They’d tell us about the missing voice. Well, what is the missing voice? You know, predominantly, women, People of Color, people that, you know, like you said, they typically might be left out whenever we’re, you know, we’ve been studying history. And so, I feel like that missing voice component for the AP curriculum really kind of touched on that. Ultimately, the secondary social studies experiences of the participants unanimously reflected the phenomenon of white social studies, indicating that regardless of urban versus rural, East Coast versus Southern, or the participant being white or a Person of Color, their white social studies teachers’ (be it intentional or not) centralized the white narrative throughout secondary levels. Steve succinctly articulated this point from his own experience, unaware it would reflect similar experiences of the other eight participants: I think it’s one of those institutionalized racism things that I only know my perspectives as a kid. And I feel like most of kids in suburban America, rural America, got the same kind of experience in how history and social studies were taught. So, I can’t speak to…the way African Americans and in the city would have been taught, but I still…that statistic that what, like 80%…. of [teachers] are white…even in areas that are predominantly a different ethnic group, they’re still being kind of taught…because that’s what the standards are. So, I feel like…my whiteness has greatly influenced the way that I look at history and the way that I teach history because it’s, it’s the prevailing view, or it has been the prevailing view…that I do see shifting. **Higher Education Experiences** While learning about race in secondary social studies generally varied for teachers based on various factors, teachers commonly reported uniformity in their undergraduate history courses and learning about race. Unanimously but unsurprisingly, all nine participants said that their undergraduate experiences in learning about race surpassed their secondary educations in range and detail. In addition, all participants reported that their undergraduate history majors required non-white history courses (i.e., Asian history, African American history) and that the requirement exposed them to a level of learning about racial history they had not realized existed in their secondary experiences. Kenny described his experience further: I really didn’t start to think about race in a serious way until I got to college. I went to a very, very, very racially diverse college, and that was…a shock. I remember being like, wow, like this actually exists as like, a real thing. . .I didn’t put any thought into it until that moment. Nate, who had described his secondary experience as never learning history past the Civil War, noticeably became more enthused when describing his college courses, using language such as “got to,” indicating a sense of excitement rather than learning by force or requirement: I got to learn history. I got to cover parts of history I’d never covered before. Some classes I took, you know…general studies and Western Civ…but then I got to take The Holocaust from a Jewish professor, and I got to take a Civil War course, from a…a published author. Got to learn…Asian history, Latin American history. It expanded. That’s just my undergrad. My…graduate work…the master’s project was to develop a class, and the class was Civil War to Civil Rights: The History of African Americans. And so, I got to…I did a reading class on that. I took a graduate class called The Native Americans. I took a class on the South, in which…I had the same professor for all my graduate classes, but one…she brought in the perceptions of the South. We talked about slavery, talked about how all that- It was the way history should be taught. I mean, I got to see, learn so much more about race. Nate also noted that when he learned history in his college courses, the material often presented varied non-white perspectives of the historical event: “we read Native essays about Native Americans through their eyes. . .We didn’t get past World War II because we focused on the Holocaust. But he did present other views, immigrants’ views, and African Americans’ views. . .” Similarly, Steve described the impact that learning about varying perspectives had on his development of love and understanding of history: there was an African American professor who was very…open and wanted us to discuss race relations, not only during that time but also now and…We specifically read a book about the African American women’s experience in Petersburg, Virginia, during the Civil War, that I just thought was, was fascinating. And I just was like - I’ve never learned perspective like this before. And I’m like, I want to know more. So, I absolutely loved that class. Due to the standard requirements of social studies education majors to take a variety of coursework spanning time and location, all nine participants reported taking coursework that required them to learn about non-white history. The commonly studied groupings are Asian history courses, African history courses, and Latine history courses. While TEPs often require equal or more coursework in American history, it is common for American universities to diversify these course offerings to include options like *African American History*, *Native American History*, or *Latine in the United States*. **Graduate School/Teacher Education.** Several participants focused explicitly on their graduate or teacher education programs when recalling their exposure to racial diversity. While methods courses often train pre-service teachers on incorporating diversity, social studies courses increasingly focus on incorporating racial diversity and analyzing one’s racial lens. For example, the most recent graduate and newest teacher Wyatt recalls: Obviously, you know, like, I was talking about that class that I took in the doctoral program that I went through…she had us, you know, unpack our little white privilege backpack, she had us do these surveys - survey someone that was from a group outside of our own, whether that be a Person of Color, or you know, opposite sex, whatever that might be. In his undergraduate program, Kenny was a history major, focusing on Catholic Church history, which is very Euro-centric. When he enrolled in graduate school for teacher education, he recalled his adviser telling him to diversify his coursework: When I got to grad school, my advisor for education was like, ‘Hey, you have never learned about anything besides European history; you should probably change that if you want to get a job,’ and I was like, okay. So then…I had to take a semester, a full semester and a half of history courses. So that ended up being six or seven history courses I had to take….and all of it was non-western….I was also in an urban ed program, as well. And so, I got some pretty explicit instruction, both in pedagogy as well as in just like general knowledge about antiracism and the movement. **Outliers.** However, Kenny also recalled a professor in his undergraduate program: He would like to talk about how great the Confederacy was….like he was…old. He was like, 85…at the end of his career. And…you could tell he was just like, not 100% there anymore, but…he would literally say stuff like, General Lee was a great general and…talk about the Confederacy and talk and talk about the Confederacy in this class about classics, so he definitely had some racial bias there. While this experience is an outlier in my data set regarding the blatancy of the educator’s candor and interests, it points to the potential implications a teacher’s racial perceptions may have on their students, particularly students who are more cognitively impressionable than college students. Thomas, Wyatt, Amina, and Ellen all said they could not recall having an educator who reflected antiracist ideals in their educations. **Professional Development Experience** Within the United States, many school districts require teachers to complete a certain number of professional development hours (PD) to maintain their certification. Schools and districts often provide PD, including seminars, training, workshops, or enrichment activity. Additionally, teachers may attend conferences or participate in continuing education coursework that pertains to their content area or instructional level. Many organizations and companies have developed PD courses focusing on content and practice and addressing more significant education concepts, such as inclusion, discipline, or differentiation. Articles and studies have reported responses from teachers on their experiences of professional development opportunities (Pillow, 2021). Historically, PDs have gotten a bad reputation from teachers and are often viewed as ineffective or a waste of time (Gulamhussein, 2013; Strauss, 2014). However, with the professional development industry growing, teachers are quick to give positive feedback when they find the experience highly effective and valuable (Fullan, 2015). Seven of the nine participants distinctly remember professional development on racial consciousness, racism, antiracism, or diversity at the school or district level. Of those seven, two struggled to describe the experience and therefore found it ineffective, while five were able to describe the training vividly because it was effective. Effective Professional Development While five of the participants experienced professional development that was effective enough for them to discuss it in detail without preparing their answers ahead of time, there are two of those participants whose experiences were highly significant. As described previously, Charles had sounded aloof and hurried throughout the interviews. As a result, I struggled to make a personal connection with him. However, there were two points throughout the three interviews that I could sense his excitement. In both instances was his discussion of being awarded a fellowship for educators to deepen their content and practices to develop equitable learning for all students. Charles described it as “culturally responsive teaching in the classroom. So pretty much those principles and trying to make sure your students feel that they can talk about race in the classroom with you and stuff like that.” For an educator to choose to attend a professional development opportunity or commit to one that takes place over a period of time, such as Charles’ fellowship, one certainly indicates their interest and passion for the subject – in this case, culturally responsive and equitable teaching practices. It is, of course, the intention that compelling PD experiences will be translated to the educator’s classroom, and that result for Charles and others will be further discussed in Chapter 6. Similarly, Kenny described his elective experiences and commitments regarding diversity and equity in his professional development. Where he lives on the East Coast, teachers must participate in yearly professional development and growth, including college course work or established curriculum enrichment through the school’s database. Kenny spent time as a coordinator for his school’s diversity committee, which also existed at two other schools where he worked. He said he wrote a curriculum for teachers about race and developed learning opportunities for his colleagues. “We came up with PD [professional development], or we at least discussed PD, and the administrator decided what we did about issues of race and diversity.” At Kenny’s current school, the committee is student-led, which he describes as much more ‘powerful’ than his previous experiences. “They teach teachers about race and how students feel about race. …Teachers also engage with…a reading series. …they read a series of books, and then they talk with students who have also read the same books about issues of race.” While the other three educators reported their respective training experiences on racial consciousness or antiracism as effective, they were also required or forced, unlike Kenny and Charles, who volunteered for their development sessions. It is not uncommon for those required/forced to attend PD to already have a negative approach to it, despite its potential value and effectiveness. Wyatt, having only been teaching for two years at the time of this interview, vocalized the common frustration many teachers express amongst each other (Pillow, 2021; Strauss, 2014) regarding mandatory professional development: Racism awareness - yes. So, a lot of that has been present in my education, as well as professional development and everything else, not to the point where it’s been antiracism training, but definitely at that level. We have had professional development on that…Obviously, it’s always good to kind of have a refresh. But with my district, a lot of it is, you know, they’ll give you these wonderful resources in these trainings. But they don’t really tell you or show you what to do with that knowledge with these resources. It’s just like, ‘here we go; we got this great resource.’ Okay….then, you know, take it a step further: how do we really implement this? How do we really use these? They don’t really do that, and I feel like that’s where they fall short at my district. There are instances, however, of required professional development sessions that are well-received by educators (Ferlazzo, 2022; Schwartz, 2019). Steve vividly remembered one session he attended that he found particularly helpful: We had a guy, an antiracism…speaker through Zoom, talk to us about antiracism strategies. [He] really talked about, like, ‘Don’t say that you’re ‘colourblind to race,’ don’t say that you’re ‘not racist.’ Just be honest with yourself, and be honest that you do have biases, and you might not eradicate them, but you are willing to see them for what they are, and then see how that influences your teaching, but also realize that the cultural differences in your students are very important. Interestingly, it was when I asked him if he feels being white impacts what he teaches and which resources he uses that Steve made an astute observation about one of his PD sessions: Most of the professional development that I do…specifically in social studies…it’s mostly white males talking to other white males. And if they’re not white males, they’re white females. I have very limited experience with African American women, like social studies teachers. …I’ve had professors who were from all kinds of different ethnic groups, but I think that most of the curriculum that is out there for me to sample from was built by white males or white females. But I have noticed - and maybe it’s just because I’m more aware and I want to think about it - more perspectives on it was on US history. We just teach about 13 colonies. And here’s the basic story. But let’s dig into…a free African American in Rhode Island…those are more stories that I’m seeing available because especially white people are saying, hey, there’s more than just this one perspective. Let’s make things complicated. Let’s make things more realistic than…the myths and things that we’ve talked about. Another well-known and highly effective professional development session is organized by a group that uses lessons of history to challenge teachers and their students to stand up to bigotry and hate (Facinghistory.org, n.d.). Facing History & Ourselves is an organization that holds professional development seminars and training from California to Massachusetts and the United Kingdom. To access a broader range, many of their seminars are online. Nate attended one of their seminars that focused on race. He described it as “antiracist” in nature. He noted this professional development opportunity because his district “focuses on poverty, but not race.” He found the experience impactful and described it as approaching an “antiracist” mindset. **Ineffective Professional Development** Professional development has gained a reputation amongst educators because of time wasted on ineffective or inapplicable information (Gulamhussein, 2013). Nolan and Amina both remembered having PDs regarding race, but neither found the session effective. Nolan said, “a lot of it was like, *Hey, what are your biases that you have?* and recognizing it, I found it to be, like… it seemed very simplistic to me.” Amina also struggled to think of the session she was required to attend: “We had one…about racism in the classroom…But I truly just don’t remember.” Amina did recall another professional development experience that she found to be ineffective as well: I thought it was a little weird that they had us read as a staff just because our schools aren’t famous for the issues that they’re going through, but we were also the largest school in the district. So I’m pretty sure that’s why they had us be the guinea pigs. But we were reading *Why Do All the Black Kids Sit Together in The Cafeteria?* …it’s a forced book club. I don’t think many people would be reading, but you are supposed to bring something to share at every meeting. And for some of our staff meetings, they literally were like, *You’re just meeting with your circle, and you’re just gonna go through your book*…like [literature] circles…and we had time…Yeah, I don’t think everyone actually read the book…I read most of my book – maybe…There were other great things in this book, but I don’t remember all of them. That was only two years ago. That’s unfortunate. **Absence of Professional Development** Two participants reported no memory of receiving professional development, specifically regarding race, antiracism, or racism awareness. When I asked Ellen if she had ever received such training in a professional setting, her reply was “Nope,” and she didn’t expand any further. When I asked Thomas if he had ever had professional development or training on racism awareness or something similar, his response was, “No. If I did, I don’t remember it. Typical…” While this data is not indicative of teachers’ experiences nationwide, it is alarming that two of the nine educator participants (22%) had no memory of receiving impactful training on handling race issues within their classroom – especially since one of them has been teaching for between eight to eleven years. While Ellen’s school is 100% white and her community is also racially homogenous, it is concerning that the teachers receive no enrichment in instructing their students about diversity to prepare them to enter into and participate in American society. **Personal Experiences with Race** The data revealed another type of experience social studies teachers had when learning about race and the development of their racial lenses aside from their formal education and professional development experiences. Participants reported having a personal interest when learning about race throughout their lives. These individual experiences have often occurred during their adolescence, making a long-term impact on their racial lens, their interactions with their students or in their social lives, and their pursuit of knowledge about race through reading and media. **As a Child** To further understand the development of the racial lens, it is crucial to examine the impact racial groups had on people growing up in the United States. I have divided the childhood exposures of varying racial groups into two sections: high exposure amounts and low exposure amounts. These subgroups would dissect if the participant reported memories of significant and impactful interactions with two or more groups outside of their race during adolescence. I further divided the exposures into the level of impact – meaning that the participant reported their experiences (or lack thereof) to have positive or adverse effects on their understanding of race (Table 5). In the next chapter, I will explore the influence of these exposures on the participants’ racial lenses/consciousnesses. However, first, I will report my findings on how the participants personally interacted with people outside of their race as they developed. **Table 5** *Participants’ Personal Experiences With Racial Diversity in Their Adolescence* | | High Exposure | Low Exposure | |--------------------------------|---------------|--------------| | **Positive Impact** | Nate; Nolan | Kenny; Steve | | **Negative or Neutral Impact** | Amina; Wyatt | Ellen; Thomas| *Note.* The only participant not included in the table was Charles, who only shared his positive learning experiences from secondary social studies regarding race. While he did not expand on his levels of exposure to People of Color during his childhood, he is the only white participant who reported current personal relationships with People of Color, including his current romantic relationship and their child. **High Exposure.** *Negative Impact.* Two participants reported high exposure to multiple racial groups as they grew. However, they both experienced adverse effects. I don’t remember a single thing from the government [class] because I didn’t actually like her teaching style. I didn’t like the way she talked. I did not. I just didn’t like how she talked. I didn’t like how she taught; I didn’t like her class. I didn’t like the kids in that class, even though we all saw each other in other classrooms. …She was Black. Growing up, my parents were low-key racist, okay, like I know that now. But I did not realize that like, it’s kind of just in their language, it’s in their jokes that they make that like, they’re very colorist, and I feel like that’s kind of just South Asian culture in general. And I…like that, I know that it’s just a remnant of colonialism, but it’s just…they prefer being lighter. While that portion of the conversation seemed to flow easily from Amina, she would later discuss how she spent the last few years examining her position on race and how she has been engaging with her parents regarding their issues. I will further discuss that in the next chapter. Wyatt also discussed the negative impacts of his high exposure to multiple racial groups. While in middle school, he attended a predominantly Black and Latine school. I am compelled to share our entire exchange so the reader can understand my role as the researcher with the sensitive information he shared with me. Researcher: Can you describe any experiences with discrimination you’ve had because of your race? Wyatt: Yes. So that’s actually why ….sixth grade…I’ve kind of, you know, kind of repressed that. And I just kind of revisited that a little bit more in-depth and specifics with my therapist a couple of weeks ago. Um…[pause] Researcher: You don’t have to broach that if you’re not comfortable. Wyatt: I won’t, I won’t…break it down like that, you know, I’m not gonna turn you into my therapist for this. But it was something…I was perceiving myself to be the victim of racism from Black and Brown kids. And, you know, they would target me for being the small white kid. And I perceived that as ‘well they’re picking on me because of my race.’ So that in turn, you know, I kind of thought I’d fight fire with fire. I’d heard that before. So that I kind of became racist myself. Well, not kind of- I was, I was racist in middle school. And yeah…That’s…I’ve gotten past that- I’m working past it with my therapist. But um, that was one time whenever I really, really thought that I was kind of getting targeted for being white at a predominantly Black and Latino school. Researcher: You attended a predominantly Black and Latino school? Wyatt: Yeah, that was in middle school. Researcher: Gotcha. Um, were there any other white students with you? Wyatt: There were, there were…(pause) Researcher: Okay, thank you for sharing that. Let me move on to the next one. At that point in the interview, I sensed that because of Wyatt’s slowed and hesitant speech pattern, he had shared what he wanted regarding that aspect of his life, and I wanted to respect his boundaries, so I didn’t press further or revisit that particular topic. However, I found it highly significant that he was willing to be so open and candid with me - a stranger recording the conversation for a study that was to be published. Wyatt is likely not the only person within his demographic to have issues with racism. But he is seeking therapy and working through those issues while simultaneously teaching at a school with a highly diverse population.\(^{15}\) This is unusual, in my opinion, and offers the field important insight into how the racial lens of white social studies teachers with self-acknowledged racism issues has not only been developed but also relates to his racial consciousness and progression towards an antiracist mentality. I will explore this in the next chapter on participants’ racial lens and consciousness. \(^{15}\) Wyatt estimated that the student population at the Southern, suburban, Title 1 school where he works is 37% Latinx, 28% Black, 18% white, 14% Asian, and 1% Indigenous./Pacific Islander. These are his rough estimations. Conversely, Nate grew up in a largely homogenous community during the 1970s and 80s, but his exposure to People of Color was intentional and consistent. Moreover, these experiences were distinct enough for him to remember. We had some Native Americans in my class. My dad and my brother were best buddies with one [non-white] family. I mean, that was an extension of our race [diversity]. We did have one Hispanic custodian, and her two daughters went to school with us. And she filled in for Spanish…which my Spanish [class] was the superintendent coming in, turning on the TV, and letting [the university] teach us rather than him teaching because he was a shop teacher. He also discussed the Black family his mother had grown up with: “One of my mom’s best friends is ‘Mama Jean.’\textsuperscript{16} She loves me like a son. And so, I mean, I grew up loving. You know, the color skin doesn’t matter.” Therefore, while his community was predominantly white, his parents made a concerted effort to ensure Nate was exposed to and in relationships with People of Color. **Low Exposure.** On the other end of the spectrum, the other half of the participants had minimal exposure to People of Color during their childhoods. While none of them were raised in urban environments that usually beget diverse populations, they were not necessarily in isolated regions. Again, the impact of their exposure had varied outcomes, depending on the situation and the person. Thomas, Ellen, Steve, and Kenny all were raised around mostly (if not wholly) white people; their neighbors, classmates, restaurant workers, etc., were generally all white. **Negative/Neutral Impact.** For Thomas, the impact of living in a primarily white area was fairly neutral: he had neither positive nor negative memories of diverse groups. For the most part, he likely was not aware of how racially homogenous his area was until he attended \textsuperscript{16}Nate said her full name, but I changed it to an unrelated name for confidentiality, keeping the title ‘Mama’ to show his filial affection towards her. university. He described how this area was so void of diversity that he never witnessed racism because there was no one to direct it towards: My most self-aware part is that, like growing up in the Midwest, there’s not a lot of diversity. …So, a lot of…racial animosity has never been given an opportunity to present itself. So, if there were, say, growing up, someone that I knew that was racist, it would very rarely be observed, simply because there’s not opportunity to demonstrate that. Similarly, Ellen was born and raised in a community where she was always around white people. She still resides in that town and spoke earlier about the lack of exposure to diversity in school. However, she knows she is missing out on the experience and benefits of being around various racial groups. I would say that just living where I live, I haven’t had a lot of experience with people of other races. …We have a few Native Americans around, but there’s really not. I mean, I don’t think there’s any African American people that live in our town. …a lot of people forget that this is a nation of immigrants. And I think it’s wonderful to have a diverse nation. I honestly wish that my area was more diverse because I think people would be more understanding. Well, maybe not, because I don’t know if people in areas that are more diverse are. Like Thomas, Ellen’s experience was neutral as opposed to negative. However, the benefits of being immersed in a multicultural environment and surrounded by various racial groups have shown to be helpful to all people (Chavez & Guido-DiBrito, 2011; Leung et al., 2008). Therefore, Thomas and Ellen may be unaware of how their homogenous upbringing may have negatively affected their perspectives. The only comparison one may have to make such a judgment is to look at people who were similarly raised in a racially homogenous area but had highly positive benefits from those experiences, such as Steve and Kenny. **Positive Impact.** Steve and Kenny were also raised in racially homogenous areas with little-to-no exposure to People of Color. Kenny described, “my teachers were 100% white through college. …the first time I had a Teacher of Color was in college. My town was 95% white, and then 4%, Asian, and 1% everything else, and that’s being generous with ‘everything else.’” However, Kenny identified his social studies curriculum as whitewashed, and Kenny has been highly involved in diversity committees at every school he has worked. He now acknowledges how far removed he was from non-white people: “I remember being grossly unaware of race…” In a sense, Kenny’s lack of exposure to non-white people throughout his youth inspired him to be highly involved and active with diversity and racial consciousness – so much so that he has spent time writing curriculum and teaching adults how to be more racially sensitive and inclusive. Steve also had minimal exposure to non-white people throughout his childhood: Very, very limited. Again, the community that I grew up in, I can only think of – in my seven years in middle and high school – three African American students. And so, it was kind of a big deal. Like…I never felt like there was any like, racism in the groups that I went with. It’d be like, oh, I can’t believe that one kid’s here. I mean, I don’t think there was any other ethnic group besides the African American. However, this lack of exposure throughout his childhood may have caused him to realize that he was limited when trying to connect with his non-white students as an adult. I’m interested because…my youth was spent around all white people; I’ve kind of said, okay, I know what they’re like. Whereas, especially my African American students or Asian American students, I’m very interested in what makes up who they are. And that doesn’t have to do about race, but like, I can see similarities between my youth and white kids. But I can see that there’s a difference [with my non-white students]. And so…I just, I want to make sure that I can build that relationship with them. And so…I want to connect with them in whatever way that I can. To understand his students better, he would need to search for diversity exposure. This would need to spark an interest in learning about racial consciousness and trying to understand complex current events from a non-white perspective. For Steve and Kenny, their lack of exposure made them acutely aware of how much richness and varied perspectives they had been missing and drove them to search for increased racial awareness in their personal lives. Present-Day Having analyzed the data for how participants experienced racial diversity in their youth, it was then necessary to look at the data for their exposures to and interactions with diversity in their adult lives. I specifically asked about their daily interactions and personal relationships with people outside their school environment. High Amount. Personal Relationships. Three of the nine participants reported having close relationships with people outside of their race. For example, Wyatt, the teacher who previously discussed his struggle in overcoming personal racism, described the variety of POC he has in his life: My two neighbors to the right are Black. I have Brazilians to the left of me… it’s a very diverse neighborhood. And that’s where I, you know, I kind of say yes and no to the colorblind question. And really, whenever I do catch myself, it’s more with socioeconomic status. Because this is a pretty good neighborhood, it’s a very safe neighborhood. I really like it. Very nice houses. And so, [my neighbors] next door to me… they’re real nice. We kind of share the same beliefs, kind of ideologies there. But yeah, it’s a very diverse neighborhood. So, I interact with them. Outside of school. At church, I do interact with a very diverse blend of people at church. Friends: Yeah, I would say I do. Not very many Black friends, not because - you know - the racism or anything, but just because they were more Filipino. …I made friends at church. So, there are more Filipino, Vietnamese, a few Nigerians. But yeah, that’s where most of my friends were made [church]. Similarly, Kenny’s social circle has a wide variety of people: I’ve worked in restaurants. I’ve worked with primarily Brazilian people. So, from the age of 14, all the way through, when I stopped working…So from the age of like, 15…all the way through 22, I worked predominantly with Brazilian people. Because that’s who makes up most of the restaurant workers [here], and that’s what I did in high school in early college. So, I had a lot of exposure through work. I lived with Richard.\(^{17}\) He was Black…Nigerian. I lived with a Man of Color, and then my current best friend…is a Man of Color as well. And I hang out with him and his friend group, and they’re very racially diverse. So yeah, I have a lot of exposure, both personal and professional. \(^{17}\) This is a pseudonym. Charles is the only white participant who reported familial relationships with non-white people: “My girlfriend is a non-white person. I have a child who’s a non-white person. So daily [exposure]....” However, he did describe an establishment he frequents that is racially homogenous: I go to a bar down the street all the time to get food; I’ve never seen a Person of Color in that bar. And I know multiple friends that I hang out with who are People of Color feel uncomfortable going there because they feel People of Color do not go there. And I keep hoping I’m going to go in there one day, and maybe I’ll be proved wrong. So yeah…like I said, it’s an older, white demographic. **Low Amount.** Though three participants reported having considerable amounts of regular interactions with people outside of their race (not including their students), six reported the opposite. For example, Thomas, Steve, Nolan, and Nate all reported seeing non-white people frequently in their community; however, they do not necessarily interact with said people other than the exchanges at stores or restaurants. They also reported not having any current personal relationships with non-white people, though they had People of Color in their lives in the past. Since Amina was the only Person of Color in the study and lives in a highly diverse Southern metropolis, I was interested in her experiences with people outside her race. She described her current relationships/exposures outside of work, “I used to run a youth group; those really do count as students, if I was going to be honest. And they were also pretty, pretty, similar as far as race goes. Um, let’s see anyone else? Nah, man.” I was also interested in how she was treated by those people outside her race when they interacted with her as a Person of Color: It’s in the stares that you get, especially at the grocery store, especially because we were one of the very few - when we first moved there - truly one of the very few People of Color that lived in that, like, neighborhood, like, we were the only ones in my neighborhood in particular. …I never used to be afraid of encountering a white person. I really wasn’t. Until my cousin…they were playing with water guns at the park, and a local white lady called the cops on them. And until that happened, I’ve never been afraid of any white person just because that’s where I grew up. That’s what I’m familiar with. Like, honestly, if I could change schools [place of employment], I would probably go to a white suburb just because I’m so familiar with that. The full results from this chapter can be seen succinctly in Table 6. In the next chapter, I will further explain these instances about findings for racial lens and consciousness. **Personal Interest** Whether it was the realization of their lack of learning about various racial groups, a peaked interest from college coursework or professional development, or their interactions with friends, many participants reported a personal interest in expanding their understanding of people outside their race. Six of the nine participants reported having read books, and articles, listened to podcasts, or watched documentaries that would allow them more depth of understanding regarding diversity and inclusion (see Tables 6 and 7). Each of the six participants reported two types of sources they have sought out to expand their interest in antiracism and racial consciousness. Specifically, two participants cited Kendi’s (2016) *Stamped from the Beginning*. Amina explicitly described watching the documentary *13th*, “I remember watching the documentary *13th* on Netflix, and my eyes were wide open after that.” While she said only this in her interview, her journal expanded further: “This year, starting in January, I made more of an active effort to educate myself on antiracism. I took a hard look at my own biases- I’m very aware of them.” ### Table 6 **Participant Summary of Experience in Learning About Race** | Name | 6-8 Grade Teachers’ Race | 9-12 Grade Teachers’ Race | Undergraduate History Professors’ Race | Graduate/TEP/NBCT* | Professional Learning About Race | Personal Learning About Race | |--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------| | Charles| All | 3 white | U.S.: 2 white | MAT | 2-yr Fellowship: Diversity in the Classroom 4-5 PD sessions | Personal relationships Books Podcasts | | Ellen | All | All | All | MA in Progress | None Recalled | None specified | | Kenny | All | All | All | MA/NBCT | Schoolwide diversity committees | Personal friendships Books Podcasts | | Nate | All | All | U.S.: All white (1 Jewish) World: 2 white (4 Jewish) | MA | Facing History and Ourselves | None specified | | Steve | All | All | U.S.: 4 white; 2 Black World: 5 white; 1 Asian | MA | Zoom speaker – Antiracism | Books Podcasts | | Thomas | All | All | All | MA | None recalled | Articles Podcasts | | Amina | 2 white | 3 white | U.S.: All white World: 1 Asian, 1 Middle Eastern, the rest white | BA | One session vaguely recalled | Books Film | | Nolan | All | 4 white | U.S.: All white World: All white | MA | One session vaguely recalled | None specified | | Wyatt | All | All | U.S.: 3 white 1 Latine World: All white | Ed.D. | | Personal friendships Articles Podcasts | *Note.* See Appendix R for a description of the pathways to teacher certification in the United States. Table 7 Participants’ Resource Types for Personal Learning | | Books | Articles | Podcasts | Film or Media | |----------------|--------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------| | Charles | Yes, but titles unspecified | | Yes, but titles unspecified | | | Kenny | • *Stamped From the Beginning* Kendi (2016) | • *The Hate you Give* Thomas (2017) | • *Throughline* (NPR) | • *Louder Than a Riot* | | Amina | • *Stamped From the Beginning* Kendi (2016) | • *Why are all the Black Kids Sitting Together in the Cafeteria?* Tatum (2017) | | Film: 13th | | Wyatt | Yes, but titles unspecified | Yes, but titles unspecified | Yes, but titles unspecified | | | Steve Thomas | Yes, but titles unspecified | Yes, but titles unspecified | Nice White Parents | | | Ellen Nate Nolan | No reported supplementary material | | Yes, but titles unspecified | | Summary of Findings 1 Ultimately, the nine participants in this study had varied experiences learning about race in their educational, professional, and personal lives. However, all nine have the common denominator of being educated by primarily white teachers, and most have had various experiences in professional development. In addition, some of them have gone further to explore the topics of racism and inclusion on their own; books and podcasts were reported as the most widely used resources. In the next chapter, I will explore the second set of findings from the data, which analyzes the participant’s racial lens and racial consciousness and determine the correlation between that and their learning experiences. CHAPTER 5 FINDINGS 2 – RACIAL OPTICS This chapter reports the findings of 27 phenomenological interviews with nine different participants. Additionally, I collected data from these participants in curriculum samples and journals. I also distributed a supplementary survey to additional participants nationwide between November 2021 and March 2022. The survey results intended to give further insight into the experiences of secondary social studies teachers in learning and teaching about race. All this data was collected and analyzed to address the research questions guiding this study: 1. How have current secondary U.S. history teachers experienced learning about race? 2. How do current high school U.S. history teachers interpret their racial lenses and consciousnesses? 3. How have these U.S. history teachers included or omitted their views of race within their curriculum? 4. How have these teachers been guided on decentralizing the white narrative from American history or including diversity in their curriculum, if at all? In this study, the phenomenological interviews were the main focal point of the data collection. The other data sets served as supplemental information to corroborate and compare with the findings of the discussions. The last chapter revealed how secondary social studies teachers learned about race. I found that nearly all the participants experienced white social studies (Chandler & Branscombe, 2015) in their adolescence. Their learning about racial diversity expanded through their college education, into their jobs through professional development, and to some extent, their personal lives and interest. This chapter will address research questions two and three - *how secondary social studies teachers have interpreted their racial lenses and consciousnesses* and *how that has been included or omitted in their curriculum.* **Racial Optics** Chapter 2 examined racial identity, lens, consciousness, subconsciousness, and unconsciousness.\(^{18}\) When combining all of the following modes: a) how people see their race, b) view the world based on their race, and c) understand and acknowledge the asymmetrical power relationships between races, I establish the overarching term that best encompasses them is *racial optics*. I first analyzed the participants’ data from each realm to determine how social studies teachers’ racial optics impact their curricular choices. **Racial Identity** All eight white participants identified themselves as white, and the Participant of Color identified herself as “Southeast Asian” or “South Asian.” When discussing a former social studies teacher, she further specified, “She was the same race as I. She’s also South Asian. She was Pakistani just like I was.” However, in Amina’s interview, she talked about her thought process concerning her identity: Because I’m a Pakistani, I’m Muslim, I think. Yeah, I think they tie in really closely… and so, they like kind of intertwine…I’ve always worn a scarf. Like, I’ve always identified with that culture and that religion, but I grew up in white suburbia. So, I was just like, if it wasn’t for the color of my skin or my religion like I was just like everyone else. Ultimately, Amina identified herself as Southeast Asian or South Asian, although she felt she could easily relate to and understand white people because she was raised amongst them. \(^{18}\) I refer to Table 1 for the definitions, examples, and differentiation between these terms. The participants’ understanding of their racial lens varies from person to person. Using their interviews and journals, I determined if and how they apply their identity to their racial lens and if they are even aware. To do this and answer research question two, *how do current secondary U.S. history teachers interpret their racial lenses and consciousnesses?* I evaluated the data through a racial lens in three realms: how the participants view themselves, others from within their race, and how they view people outside of their race. **Participants’ Views of Themselves.** *Confident.* Several participants clearly articulated how their race influences their perception of the world around them and could communicate how they view themselves regarding their race. They conveyed this confidence with phrasing such as *definitely, I know,* and *I am.* For example, Kenny expressed high confidence in how he perceives himself: “I’ve definitely been privileged to be born a white straight man, and I’ve definitely have experienced that privilege multiple times. It’s not something that you see every day, but it’s definitely something that I know exists....” Nate also speaks confidently about his knowledge of his race and how it influences what he sees around him: “I’m aware of the whiteness that I am. We were talking, like I said, we started social stratification last week, and I said, I’m a white male. I have never been discriminated against.” While Ellen has minimum exposure to People of Color, she demonstrated confidence in how she perceives herself as a white female: I’m pretty comfortable with who I am, and I know who I am, and I know I’m not hateful, but I do know that I need to be aware of myself and just - my lack of experience could sometimes be an obstacle. And just I don’t have the perspective...of somebody who’s lived it when I teach it, so I need to be mindful of making sure that I’m presenting in a way that’s just...show open-mindedness and kind of share that with my students so that they do the same thing. She demonstrated in her statement that her whiteness was something she had spent some time thinking about, which was true of the other participants who appeared to describe themselves confidently. For example, Steve explained how he seemingly is still considering how he views his whiteness: It’s taking me quite a while to figure out like…*what is my whiteness? And what is maybe white privilege*, but just *where’s my station in society? And…why am I there?* Because I’ve always felt like I’ve worked for the things that I’ve achieved. But I’m also aware that there are certain…challenges I didn’t have to face that other people would because they weren’t a white male. Similarly, Thomas expressed himself succinctly and confidently, as though he had been thinking about this answer before the interview: So, there’s a lot more understanding of myself of just like, oh, I was growing up in a very, like conservative, white, you know, lack of diversity kind of situation. And so, it’s more awareness of like, there are a lot of things that I would have maybe not questioned, just been okay, and then moved on that now. Like, it’s time to go back and say, *okay, I gotta question what this thought is, this idea, am I a product of any sort of, like, narrative that was trying to have been pushed upon me, my parents or their grandparents throughout… racial discourse, right.* And then I’m also very aware like, economically, especially. So, growing up in a middle-class home, able to go to college. I had debt, but I mean, I was able to pay that off. I’m living a middle-class lifestyle now. So just kind of like that sense of continuity between, you know, my grandparents, my parents, and myself… Yeah, I would say, now I’m very aware of what a lot of people label *privilege* that was extended to my heritage, like land ownership, and that kind of stuff that would have been denied to Black people, specifically….but yeah…I would say, like, I’m aware of some of that kind of how my just being born into this situation has impacted the success of myself. Probably as I grew older, moving away from a more individualistic viewpoint of like, *you earn what you get*, and understanding that there’s a lot of inherited wealth, and then I am on the winning side of that. **Less Than Confident.** While clear on their racial identity, some participants appeared less confident when discussing how they view themselves racially. For example, when I asked Wyatt how he understands his whiteness, his immediate response was, “I know that I have kind of gotten away with some things… So, I do acknowledge that….in some instances, it can provide me with an advantage.” However, he stopped short of exuding the confidence in how his whiteness impacts him that Steve, Ellen, and Kenny demonstrated. The participant who seemingly struggled the most with their racial lens was Amina. Her struggle could be attributed to her upbringing in a white community, her largely South Asian education, and perhaps her youth or education level. However, Amina did describe how she grew up thinking of her skin color: I remember my own family being like, cuz I’m darker than most of my family, [they were] like, ‘Oh, don’t go out in the sun; make sure we’re wearing sunscreen.’ Whatever, and [I’m] just kind of like, *just let me run*. And honestly, I stopped going out in the sun because of it. Amina described her mentality as she grew and became more ingrained with varying cultures and skin colors: “I also looked at race differently. I didn’t think of myself as very different from anyone else. I felt like if it wasn’t for the color of my skin and the scarf on my head, I’m just like everyone else.” Amina’s experience demonstrates the paradoxical self-image many People of Color experience – particularly when it comes to how much their race is seen as being central to their identity (Horowitz et al., 2019\(^{19}\)). While often people have trouble identifying their racial lens regarding themselves, they can speak to their perceptions of their race in general. For Amina, this is true as well. **Participants’ Views of Their Racial Demographics.** When asking for any generalization, one must always be prepared to frame the answer they receive in the specific opinions and views of the person they are asking. While I asked highly subjective broad questions about how the participants viewed people within their race, their answers can offer insight into understanding their racial lens and how they perceive their race within the world. I --- \(^{19}\) A 2019 survey of U.S. adults conducted January 22 – February 5, 2019, revealed that 52% of Black Americans see their race as extremely important and central to their identity, as opposed to 31% of Hispanic Americans and 27% of Asian Americans. In this same poll, 5% of white Americans reported their race was central to their identity. was curious if the participants strongly associated with their race because of the racial polarization and strife within the United States in the last ten years. **Distinction From.** Three of the participants very clearly separated themselves from other white people when I asked them about their understanding of white people within society in a general sense. Kenny described one aspect with which he is not like other white people: I think that we all have implicit bias somewhere. I mean, that’s just a given fact. And I think that a lot of white people especially like, I think- that it doesn’t exist, and that’s problematic. That’s very problematic. I mean… I would argue that’s almost worse than someone being explicitly racist… not being able to… reflect on your own racial attitudes. Nate and Ellen also distinguished themselves from others within their race. In his journal, Nate described himself and his view of white people: “I think my race has been oppressive. I am not colorblind because there is a beautiful diversity, and to ignore it would be harmful. I am antiracist. I treat people as people, loving them as anyone else.” Ellen also described in her journal how she sees white people and places herself amongst that group, “I think in history the white race as a whole has been oppressive, but I myself have not.” **White Normalization.** Three other participants specifically responded about how they view white people in society; they described how whiteness had been normalized in the United States. Steve explained how he believes society sees him and other white people – that white normalization is perpetuated: I think that, especially in college, I just kind of realize that, like, just white males, in general, were kind of… I don’t want to say it like this: like the standard everybody else should follow. And I just felt like that was strange to me that like that my life or the way that I’m, I’m living my life should be, what media and the news are all kind of focused on. ….. I don’t know how well I’ve done to kind of adapt or do things because of my, my place in society as a white male, but just be aware of, of the benefits of, of I’m able to walk in certain places or just act in a certain way. And nobody judges me. I feel like most white people will look at ethnic groups as kind of *live and let live*…. like we don’t want to know about your uniqueness because it doesn’t make you any better or worse than anybody else. But by saying that, it does. Nolan noted the same type of mentality – that white normalization is a societal occurrence – if even only perpetuated by white people: They’ve been kind of the default, privileged groups in American history. You look in media; it’s mostly white people. So even though the demographics of the country are changing, that’s still kind of very much the prototypical American, and I guess…people get mad about it, or they say like, ‘white privilege.’ It’s not white privilege. It’s more, ‘Wow, you’ve been assholes to everybody else. Like you would just expect to be treated fairly.’ So, it’s almost like, *if this is white privilege, oh, man, our baseline is really low on being just a decent human being.* Being white in America made me not notice race for a long time. It just never came up. I never really thought about it because I never experienced anything that would make me think about it. It’s not like I didn’t know people in other races, but *okay, their skin’s different.* I remember, in 2008, when Obama became President, we’re all in college, and we were a bunch of white people. And we’re like, *you’re making fun of the fact that like, he’s Black! …Are we like, the first generation that really doesn’t care if he’s Black and actually cares about his policies?* Or it’s like, *wow, there’s more here than I realize* when that’s like every newspaper. Thomas similarly described his mentality while at the same time drawing generalizations about other Midwesterners: But on the whole, you know, most [Midwesterners], they have no animosity towards Black Americans, and they grew up in the racial blindness discourse and so trying to be good neighbors, they will just ‘okay, there’s no…difference between you and me,’ they’re gonna just treat them like neighbors. Thomas referenced his perception that other Midwesterners do not see a difference between themselves and Black Americans. However, because he wrongly assumed all Midwesterners are white and “people outside your race” means “Black Americans,” he revealed part of his racial lens, not only by these generalizations but also by his mindset that the white people in his area are friendly and open to People of Color. His comments also reflect that his racial lens includes the default notion that white is standard and assumed. **Dual Mentality.** Amina described her upbringing in-depth and unprompted, indicating to me that it is an integral part of her identity. “If it wasn’t for the color of my skin or my religion…I was just like everyone else…I never really felt singled out for my race…and I know I’m lucky for that. …Maybe it’s the bubble my parents raised me in.” However, she also has strong identity ties with her South Asian heritage even though she was born in America; she talked through her struggle between reconciling the differences between the two identities: South Asians, in general, really do value education; they value family, they value like, loyalty, and those are all great things. But I think as I got older, I kind of saw the negative side of those same things. Yes, you value education…but it’s at the expense of what your kid wants, or yes, you value…family loyalty, but again…what if they’re not doing the right thing? She went on to describe stereotypes of her culture and religion and their applicability to her situation: I don’t think anyone looks at the full picture. Like for example – the arranged marriages? Yes, absolutely…lots of people I know got arranged marriages. But nobody I know is forced into a marriage. …My own marriage wasn’t technically arranged. Like, the meeting was arranged, but…we chose to go forward with it. …So yeah, there’s truth in everything. Amina’s point that there is truth in everything is a phrasing often repeated when people discuss stereotypes, and stereotypes are often accurate (Jussim, 2016; Shpancer, 2018). Just as she pointed out specific stereotypes of South Asians that maybe be applicable, all eight white participants also pointed out varying stereotypes they have heard about white people. Furthermore, all eight also identified which of those applied to them personally. Though stereotyping is an overgeneralization and often unfair, it is notable that all participants in this study articulated that some of their named stereotypes for their race applied to them. **Participants’ Views of Other Racial Demographics.** People can name and identify with stereotypes regarding their race. Still, it is socially and morally unacceptable for them to determine which stereotypes apply to people outside of their race. Therefore, rather than asking about stereotypes for people outside of their race, I asked a more straightforward question to determine how participants viewed people outside of their race, “how do you view people outside of your race when you first encounter them?” Empathetic. Most participants responded to the question with language that indicated sentiments of empathy. Kenny, Nate, Steve, and Thomas expressed that they appreciate all people and do their best to empathize with their perspectives. Kenny had the most detailed response: I don’t see all people as the same because that’s just not how it works. You know, people’s lived experiences have largely been dictated, especially in America, by the color of their skin… I try to be empathetic and try to understand where they’re coming from and what may be their experience due to their race has led them to believe, or how it’s led them to act in the situation, I guess. Again, I don’t want to say that I don’t see race, but it’s like when I’m dealing with a Person of Color. I don’t – that’s not the forefront of my thought when I’m dealing with them. I’m trying to deal with them as a person, and then if the issue of race gets brought up, then I try to see, you know, how their race- how their racial identity have affected this and then how can I be an ally for them to deal with this issue? Nate had a phrase he repeated over and over throughout his interview – ‘people are people.’ At first, when I noted this phrase as an in vivo code, I perceived it as his way of saying that he maintains an attitude of colorblindness, which is damaging to People of Color (Eberhardt, 2019; Williams, 2011). However, as I employed value coding with further analysis, I recognized that he appreciates the variations amongst racial groups and honestly does look for the quality of a person’s character: I want to learn…how they live, just because that’s how I am. I see people are people, but they’re different….I am comfortable around people outside of my race, and I interact with people daily. People are people. Their race and culture should be appreciated, and I learn from them. Similarly, Steve appreciates other races and looks to learn from them, mainly because his childhood exposure to various races was limited. He described in his journal: Again, due to my limited experiences as a youth, I will admit to not wanting to embarrass myself when interacting with people outside of my race. I strive to get them to understand that I want to just treat them as a real person. My own social anxieties and personal proclivities probably hinder those interactions, especially when they are brief in duration. At the same time, Thomas described how when he encounters People of Color, he is immediately aware that they have likely had a very different experience from him. If I see a Person of Color, like in my school district, or just in my area, I’m aware of, *you are a minority here* in the sense of like, I’m aware that, that they probably have experienced some sort of racism toward them. While empathy may not be overly assertive or explicitly worded as such, the overall attitude conveyed by these four participants was openness, acceptance, and appreciation throughout the interviews. **Normalization.** This study’s essence is to evaluate the history of normalizing whiteness in American history courses. In that same sense, it is essential to understand the mentality of American history teachers and how they are working to decentralize white people and reframe the narrative to normalize BIPOC. This understanding begins with the mindset of the teachers and how they perceive BIPOC in their everyday perspective. Ellen, Nolan, and Charles stood out with their responses to my question about what goes through their minds when encountering a Person of Color. While Nolan and Charles made their points very briefly, Ellen had to take some time to explain her mindset, likely because it was not a question she had ever considered before. I personally don’t care what somebody else’s race is. I tend to care more about who somebody is as a person; what you look like or what color you are doesn’t reflect what kind of person you are at all. So, it really doesn’t matter to me….If somebody’s a bad person, they’re a bad person - I don’t care what color their skin is. If somebody is going to be weird and creepy, they’re gonna be weird and creepy, no matter what color they are, or if they’re mean, they’re gonna be mean, no matter what color they are, that’s not going to affect my interaction with them. In her journal, Ellen was able to articulate her mentality more: I definitely try to be colorblind and do not consider myself racist at all. I have very infrequent interactions with people of other races because I live in an area that is virtually 100% white. However, when I do have them, I feel comfortable; people are just people. Despite Ellen’s use of the word “colorblind,” given its negative connotation discussed in Chapter 2, it is necessary to examine her other statements to determine if her use of the word indicates unconsciousness/racial apathy or if it is being used subconsciously to convey a sentiment, she is unsure of how to articulate. Based on the context of her other remarks about working toward including diversity in her curriculum to present People of Color to her 100% white students, I deduce that Ellen’s intentionality is to normalize People of Color in America’s narrative, even if her vocabulary lacks the appropriate antiracist terminology. This same intentionality is reflected in Nolan when I asked him about his thought process when he encounters BIPOC, “I don’t consciously think about it. They’re just another person.” Charles also described his thought process: When I go up to everyone, I think the same thing. *Hey, how are you*…not much goes to my head. I try to think the best of people. …You know, *what’s going on with you in your life?* is my first thought. Charles did go on to describe his desire for all races to be normalized and society can move towards a post-racial mindset: Well, I mean, just think of this whole interview, right? We’re talking about the way different races or colors are interacting in America, right? We’re still living in, you know, a world that sees race, right? So that’s the conversation we’re having. But you know, you need to find somewhere where you acknowledge this race world but also strive for a world right where we are communicating with each other enough that we can overcome these constructs of race within the next couple what 100 years, 200 years. Charles is a white father of a Person of Color and has a close romantic relationship with a Person of Color. While he is likely fully appreciative of his child and girlfriend’s non-white race, Charles, along with Nolan and Ellen, expressed intentionality to normalize non-white people in white people’s perceptions. They indicated they would do this by setting a precedent to encounter BIPOC as they would a white person. While this may appear to be a fundamental act, this is not always the case. Admittedly Bias. Amina and Wyatt communicated their respective biases regarding people outside their racial group. Amina mentioned several times throughout the interviews that she had an affinity for white people: Growing up, I preferred white people, like I had a very strong preference, just because that’s kind of the culture that we grew up in.’ In her journal, she described the bias she felt toward African Americans: ‘I’m more comfortable now more than ever, but when I was younger, I was, I think, a little of afraid of Black people. Similarly, Wyatt discussed his struggle with bias with me: “sometimes in my past, I’ve caught myself looking at people of other races. And it’s like, well, you know, kind of letting that … like those biases be known … kind of like that societal conditioning.” However, there was a notable difference between the two in their descriptions. As previously stated, Amina’s preference for white people was pretty clear to her. However, when she began to discuss other People of Color, she seemed instead taken off guard by her wording: That same semester is when I took [history of] China, that same semester was when I took [the history of] North Africa, but I didn’t like the teachers in either of those. And you know what? Both of them have an accent……Now that I think about it. Man, people in my own family have accents, and here I am…[I’m] totally dumbfounded. Whereas Wyatt described how he was working on his bias in therapy and was open to sharing more about that experience from when he felt he was experiencing discrimination. While I already included this passage in the last chapter, it is worth repeating because I analyzed it with the scope of the findings of this chapter as well: I’m not gonna turn you into my therapist for this. But it was something, you know, I was perceiving myself to be the victim of racism from Black and Brown kids. And, you know, they would target me for being the small white kid. And I perceived that as ‘well, they’re picking on me because of my race.’ So that in turn, you know, I kind of thought I’d fight fire with fire. I’d heard that before. So…I kind of became racist myself. Well, not ‘kind of.’ I was. I was racist in middle school. And yeah…That’s- you know- I’ve gotten past that - I’m working past it with my therapist. But um, that was one time whenever I really, really thought that I was kind of getting targeted for being white at a predominantly Black and Latino school. Wyatt described the progress he had made in his journal: “I am very comfortable interacting with members outside of my own race and racial identity. I interact with people of varying races every day in a professional setting, and nothing outside of that.” Wyatt’s realization of his bias versus Amina feeling “dumbfounded” are two examples that represent many Americans regarding how they view people of a race different from their own. Namely, their level of consciousness or awareness is a defining factor in determining one’s racial lens. **Comparison of Participants’ Racial Lens to Nationwide Survey.** To triangulate the data, I included a nationwide survey that explored the racial lens of 349 secondary social studies teachers. Following a similar line of questioning that I used in the participant interviews and journals, the survey results paint a particular picture of the racial lenses of secondary social studies teachers within the United States. The sample size was fractional of the population because I distributed the survey on politically and racially neutral social media pages for secondary social studies teachers. Therefore, I have every reason to believe the sample is representative of secondary social studies teachers within the United States. While I collected over 425 surveys, I counted in the results only those whose completion rate was 50% or greater; this totaled 349. Figures 7 and 8 show two sets of data – the first is from the participants, and the second is from the national survey. The first set is responses to a question that asked social studies teachers to identify where they consider themselves to fall within these various definitions. These results reflect how the participants see themselves regarding race, their racial outlook, and their approach to racism issues. Racial (Sub/Un)Consciousness Regardless of how participants viewed their racial identity or others outside of their race, a person’s racial lens is half of the equation when considering the scope of their racial optics. The other piece that must be analyzed is how they are aware of the experiences different racial groupings have. In Figure 6 and Figure 7, I compared how the nine participants self-identified their racial optics and how the 349 nationwide survey participants self-identified theirs. Most participants in both groups self-identified as an ally, with antiracist and not-racist following behind, indicating not a definitive label since this self-identification status is highly subjective and not validated, but rather that the majority of social studies teachers perceive themselves to be allies. Therefore, I look to the evidence for their demonstrations of racial (sub/un)consciousness.
