Figure 7 Participant Self-Identified Racial Optics ($n = 9$) Teachers’ Self-Identification of Racial Optics - Antiracist: 22% - Ally: 56% - Not Racist: 22% - I’ve got some racist thoughts: 0% - I’ve spoken/acted on my racist thoughts: 0% - I fully acknowledge I am a racist: 0% - Other: 0% Demonstrations of Racial Consciousness. All participants expressed some degree of racial consciousness in their interviews and journals. They all are aware of racial issues in the United States. They are all familiar with the terminology popularized in the last five years. They all recognize that the white narrative has been centralized in history. Based on their responses and rhetoric, Nolan, Kenny, and Charles stood out as more aware and passionate about race issues than the others. Kenny spoke about awareness of his privilege and working towards being an antiracist; he articulated his views on both in his journal. As a tall, white cis man, I’ve benefitted in all senses from my identity. This country was made by and for people like me, and that needs to change. I feel very strongly that it is my duty to create a safe space for students to investigate issues surrounding race, both in history and within themselves, and I am proud to provide that space to my students. I am proud to be an antiracist ally, and that aspect of my identity is one of the few ‘political’ things I reveal to my students about myself. I am very comfortable with People of Color and have worked and lived with them for most of my adult life. Kenny’s awareness of the benefit his whiteness (as well as sexual orientation and gender) has had on his life illustrated the level of his racial consciousness. Charles described the influence an educator had on him because of their classroom discussions on being an activist: And so, as we moved more into talk about race in my life, I’ve thought about how you know…my Friends of Color, People I know of Color, being affected. Why shouldn’t I listen to them and actively try to make it so they don’t have to deal with these things? Charles also discussed in his journal his appreciation for all races: No, I am not oppressed, and I do not try to be colorblind; rather appreciate all races for what they bring to the table from their culture. I am always aspiring to be antiracist and frequent my interactions with people of different races daily. Finally, Nolan garnered the most passion and emotion when discussing his mentality on racial issues. I asked him his perspective of the country’s racial strife from an American citizen’s point of view: Our country’s pretty shit, isn’t it? …yes, hugely, hugely depressing. Like, this is not who I ever thought America was as a kid. It’s just incredibly, incredibly distressing. It makes me honestly, I mean, I shouldn’t- but it kind of pretty much makes me, like, feel ashamed. He went on in his journal to empathize with his Students of Color: When it comes to Police, there is no respect, particularly among Black students (understandably). That is the biggest area I see a direct and vehement reaction from the students. I have to admit that I largely share their views (qualified immunity is dangerous). Despite his passion for the topic, Nolan did acknowledge that while his mind is antiracist, his actions are not. I include this perspective as one of racial consciousness because intentionality and awareness are crucial factors when considering how racially conscious one is. The only thing that’s changed [by participating in this study] is just kind of a recognition that I’m not really- so my beliefs would be very antiracist. I’m not actually taking any action. So, I can’t say anything about it. Not because I don’t care but because now there’s way…there’s so many other things in life going on. After all the Trump years and all, I think it’s also about political exhaustion, and I almost I don’t want to engage anymore because I get so depressed…so politically exhausted with the seeming regression we took in, how so many elements that I thought weren’t there came out with Trump, and that’s been exhausting. One’s inaction is not an excuse – and actions are essential in revealing the extent to which one is racially conscious. Ultimately, I found that this journal entry can summarize Nolan’s general racially conscious mentality: I’m not colorblind since to be colorblind would be itself a form of oppression by ignoring the systemic oppression prevalent in our society. I try to be antiracist as much as possible. When it comes to bias, there are admittedly a few in there, but I work to make sure that never impacts my interactions with people, and I’m working to get better. For the most part, I’ve found that any bias is much more the result of a person’s cultural and economic background as opposed to anything with the fabricated concept of race. **Demonstrations of Both Racial (Sub)Consciousness.** Three participants overlapped racial consciousness with racially subconscious rhetoric in their journals and interviews. Because of the complexity of the human mind and the limits that can be internalized at one time, people’s level of racial (sub)consciousness is scaffolded. While Nate, Steve, and Thomas did not express overtly racist statements, there is a level of ambiguity in their responses to various questions about racial issues. While Nate expressed a heightened awareness of and empathy towards the plight BIPOC has, he is new to understanding the terminology and mentality of 21st century antiracist ideas. As a result, he appears somewhat unaware of the internal work needed to become more racially conscious, as shown in one of his journal entries: I do not have racial bias. I am not racist; I grew up in a home where racism would not be tolerated with family-friends who meant more than family. Although being in a racist region, my family has treated people as people. Steve is another participant that moved between revealing a racially conscious mentality and racial subconsciousness. He struggled with the awareness of knowing what he wanted to and should do versus what was best for himself and his family. Unfortunately, this is often the case with many white people. One of the things that I talked about [in the journal] was, you know, was the extreme abolitionists, *would you give your life for this?* And like, I remember mentally thinking like, *well, I’d like to say yes, but would I really do that, but would I really risk my life for somebody based off the color of their skin?* I can’t say that I— I probably would. Because of just the things around me and my family and things like that, it would just be like; it would depend more on just, I like to say, the safety of the individual. But maybe if the situation was going to, like a good chance, it would cause my death, I wouldn’t have done anything. But if I could do something…..but I mean, again, I’d like to say that I would, but I still question whether I really would if presented with that situation. Steve also described the Midwest area in which he lives with his family, with his sister and her family living nearby, Now that I have a little boy, um, I mean, I’m thinking about that stuff. But like, I mean, I’m just a firm believer in public schools. So, I think that, like, my son should go to the elementary school that is literally two miles away. But my, my sister specifically put her two kids in private school. So, it’s, you know, these are questions that I’ve been asking myself about this - about racism - when [my sisters’ kids are] 10 and 11. So for the last decade, I’m like, *why did they do that?* And it’s because they’re from [a small midwestern college town with a highly diverse population], and most of the public schools are predominantly African American. And so…I haven’t asked her that outright, but certainly- I’m reading a book about redlining right now. So, I’m fascinated by that subject. And especially redlining in [Midwestern capital city], like in most cities, they just built the interstate right next to the poorest neighborhood that they could find. Thomas is another example of a person whose level of awareness swings between consciousness and subconsciousness. He has spent much time educating himself on racial issues and described in his interviews his Christian faith. Nevertheless, as he reflected on the racial lens scales (Appendix N), he expressed their lack of impact on his mindset: I’m even someone who like, acknowledges, and understands privilege. And that I have it and that I’m biased and that kind of stuff. And so I don’t know what the target audience for those things are. And I don’t know what the utility is intended to be. But it comes across a lot like preaching to the choir. And then if not, if it’s not preaching to the choir, it’s certainly not affecting people like me, who would be open to what they have to say, in actual real resources to use to get People of Color voices into my curriculum, and how I can do that. If you’re going to push stuff like that out, especially you’re going to push that stuff out into public education, you’re going to get backlash, just… it’s going to be wild; it’s going to be more harmful to your cause than it is beneficial. And I think I understand the desire to classify people to help move people towards activism, but I just think it’s all short-sighted. And it’s just gonna lead people, especially people like me who kind of roll your eyes at the end and say, ‘Oh, my goodness, here we go again, you know, here, here I am. Yeah, you know, the privilege talk.’ While Thomas’ sarcasm and frustration in his last sentence are palpable and intentional, what seems to be coming from within his subconscious is that he has grown tired of the race conversation and does not see racism scale tools as effective. **Demonstrations of Racial Subconsciousness.** As mentioned, all participants described some level of being racially conscious or aware of issues and did express some level of empathy. However, there were two participants whose commentary in the interviews and the journal showed repeated signs of ambiguity regarding racial issues and subconscious bias that I found surprising. Amina and Wyatt both had a racial lens that reflected admitted bias towards people outside their race. Therefore, it was likely that they also indicated signs of racial subconsciousness, though it is surprising given Amina is a Woman of Color and Wyatt is the most highly educated. **Amina.** Amina, being the only Person of Color in the study, revealed various levels of racial subconsciousness, which was in itself a subconscious action. I previously shared how she discovered her bias against foreign or non-American accents, which she realized only as she spoke them out loud to me during her interview. She also described how she was aware of how little she does to be active in racial issues: “I could be doing more than what I do. Like, I feel like I’m at the point where I could be more actively antiracist, not just at the education phase.” This was despite having written in her journal entry prior to the interview, “This year, starting in January, I made more of an active effort to educate myself on antiracism. I took a hard look at my own biases- I’m very aware of them.” While she claimed to be “very aware” of her own biases, her bias against non-American accents came as a surprise to her wherein she said she was ‘dumbfounded.’ **Wyatt.** Wyatt also admitted his racial bias when interviewed and seemed highly familiar with modern terminology and concepts because of his recent doctoral-level schooling. However, he revealed subconsciousness as he described himself in his journal entry: I would most likely describe myself as not racist; however, when there is an opportunity to do away with any sort of racism, discrimination, or biases, then I will take the opportunity; I just do not actively go out of my way to seek them out. . . . I believe that I may have racial bias, as I believe everyone does, no matter how antiracist one may claim to be. I do not think that I hold any sort of racist ideals; however, I prefer to shy away from the phrase ‘I’m not racist,’ as this runs you the risk of being branded a racist anyway. He also discussed in an interview how he doesn’t feel any responsibility to change how others view race: You’re gravitating towards what you’re familiar with. I can check that- I can kind of check on my own biases. But I cannot, you know, my whole initial reaction was I can’t snap my fingers and change the hearts and minds of every white cop. I can’t automatically just end white privilege. Wyatt’s subconsciousness continued to emerge as he went on to describe his approach to understanding the high death rate of People of Color when they encounter law enforcement: Not all of the victims, I will say that- I feel like I can say that safely - Not all of the victims, some of them might have had a different outcome had they behaved differently. But most of them - Yeah, I do. I do believe that justice wasn’t served for *a lot* of them. *A lot* of Black men and women *didn’t really deserve to die in many* [emphases added] of those instances. The conditionality of his statements and opinions reveals his level of racial subconsciousness, as he failed to empathize with the outrage many People of Color and antiracist advocates hold regarding law enforcement bias being correlated to their killing of unarmed Black men and women. Outlier: Demonstration of Racial (Sub/un)consciousness. While being unaware of race and racial issues in modern America is likely impossible given the high levels of diversity and exposure to media, humans can be racially unconscious. Charles recalled his experience in China, wherein he encountered racially unconscious people: “I have blonde hair. So, people would grab my hair a lot in public try to, like, I don’t know, take a strand of my hair because they’ve never seen a blonde person before.” If someone has only lived in America, it is unlikely for them to realize many countries are entirely one race and ethnicity and do not have the exposure to multiple racial groups to which Americans are accustomed. Ellen is the only participant who was born, raised, educated, and currently lives in a completely white homogenous community. Although she has encountered People of Color on rare occasions, she only interacts with white people daily, socially, professionally, and communally. Therefore, it was prudent for me to analyze her data for instances where racial unconsciousness may exist. I found that she also demonstrated instances of racial consciousness and some racial subconscious comments. Therefore, I have listed several of her comments back-to-back in Figure 8 for comparison. Synopsis of Racial (Sub/Un)Consciousness. As part of the interview process, I asked the participants to respond to various prompts briefly and give their initial reaction, opinion, or anything they wanted to say about the phrase. I also articulated that it was acceptable to tell if they were unaware of the term. I decided to include this as part of the interview to help determine how they were familiar with current racial issues and terms and if they had an opinion on them. Figures 9 and 10 reflects their answers concerning if they were aware of the issue or phrase, and if so, was their response reflective of racial consciousness, subconsciousness, or unconsciousness. ### Ellen’s Commentary Reflecting Racial (Sub/Un)Consciousness | Examples of Ellen’s Racial Consciousness | ‘If all lives mattered, then we wouldn’t be where we are.’ | ‘When Trump was the President, he kind of said some things that made people feel like it was okay, really, like horrible, racist things. And then once people felt like, *well, if the President says it, why can I say it?* And then people start saying these things and letting out their true feelings, that before was socially polite to keep those terrible things to yourself.’ | |-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Examples of Ellen’s Racial Sub-consciousness | ‘I feel like my, my professor now is almost over-compensating a little bit. Like, I don’t disagree…There’s racism, and things like that, that we need to deal with. But just some of that, he doesn’t allow you to have an opinion that’s different.’ | ‘I think the African American and Indigenous people probably get more [of my focus in the curriculum] just because I think if my kids were to encounter somebody of another race, that’s who they would encounter. And I also this probably sounds stupid, um, because it’s not entirely true because the U.S. has some terrible things to all those races, but I feel like the atrocities that have been overlooked towards those races need to be reconciled and need to be brought to light.’ | | Examples of Ellen’s Racial Unconsciousness | ‘But I was kind of surprised at my results, but not really, because I figured my lack of experience, just interacting with people of other races, would probably give me some inherent bias. Like I said, I know I’m not a racist person, but just my lack of experience being around people of other races, I figured would have some impact and did on the quiz results.’ | ‘We have a few Native Americans around, but there’s really not- I mean, I don’t think there’s any African American people that live in our town. And I mean, yeah, I don’t really run into any out and about either. Um, my sister lives in Washington, DC. So, she has a few friends that are Black and Hispanic. A few of them come up with her, you know, when she comes home once in a while, and she stays with us. But other than that, not really. There just hasn’t really been an opportunity.’ | ### Figure 10 **Reactions to Racially Controversial Issues** | Race Issues Prompt | Reflects Racial Consciousness | Reflects Racial Subconsciousness | Reflects Racial Unconsciousness | No or Simple Response | |-----------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|-----------------------| | **1619 Project** | Charles; Kenny; Nate; Steve | Amina; Ellen; Nolan; Thomas; Wyatt | | | | | 44% | 56% | | | | **1776 Project** | Nate | Charles; Steve; Wyatt | Amina; Ellen, Kenny; Nolan; | Thomas | | | 11% | 33% | 44% | 11% | | **All Lives Matter** | Amina; Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Wyatt | | | Thomas | | | 89% | | | 11% | | **Confederate Monuments** | Amina; Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve | Wyatt | | Thomas | | | 78% | 11% | | 11% | | **Critical Race Theory** | Amina; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Steve | Charles; Nolan; Wyatt | | Thomas | | | 56% | 33% | | 11% | | **Kyle Rittenhouse** | Amina; Ellen; Kenny; Steve; Wyatt | Nolan; Thomas | | Charles; Nate | | | 56% | 22% | | 22% | *Note.* The percentages indicate the percentage of the nine total participants whose responses fell into the various categories. The peach shading indicates the category which held most participants. Figure 11 Reactions to Race Terminology | Terminology Prompt | Reflects Racial Consciousness | Reflects Racial Subconsciousness | Reflects Racial Unconsciousness | No or Simple Response | |--------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------------|---------------------------------|-----------------------| | Antiracist | Amina; Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Wyatt 89% | Ellen 11% | Ellen, Nate 22% | Thomas 11% | | Gatekeeping | Charles; Kenny; Steve; Wyatt 44% | Amina; Nolan 22% | Ellen, Nate 22% | Thomas 11% | | Racial Apathy | Kenny; Nate; Steve 33% | Ellen 11% | Amina; Charles; Nolan; Wyatt 44% | Thomas 11% | | Racial Consciousness | Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; 44% | Amina; Nolan; Steve; Thomas; Wyatt 56% | | | | Subconscious racism | Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Wyatt 78% | Amina 11% | | Thomas 11% | | Whitewash | Amina; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Thomas; Wyatt 89% | | | Charles 11% | | White Fragility | Amina; Charles; Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Wyatt 89% | | | Thomas 11% | | White social studies | Steve 11% | Ellen; Kenny; Nate; Wyatt 44% | Amina; Charles; Nolan; Thomas 44% | Ellen; Thomas 22% | | White privilege | Amina; Charles; Kenny; Nate; Nolan; Steve; Wyatt 78% | | | | Note. The percentages indicate the percentage of the nine total participants whose responses fell into the various categories. The peach shading indicates the category which held most participants. Specifically, I categorized the participants in Reflects Racial Consciousness not based on their opinion of the topic/term but on whether they had enough awareness of the topic to have an opinion. I categorized participants under Reflects Racial Subconsciousness for any of the following reasons: a) if their awareness of the topic/term was too minimal for them to form an opinion; b) if the opinion they formed was based on misinformation (according to the fact, not my perception); c) if they articulated that they were indifferent, or d) if they gave an accurate definition as a guess based on subconscious knowledge. I categorized participants under *Reflects Racial Unconsciousness* only if they were completely unaware of the term/topic. Any omission of participants from a specific topic/term is because their answer did not indicate the depth or extent of their knowledge and most likely simply responded with an answer similar to *yes, I’m familiar with that.* Finally, as with the findings on participants’ racial lenses, I have included the responses to a question that asked teachers to select any/all thoughts and opinions they maintain regarding race. Figure 11 shows the results from the participants, and Figure 12 comparatively shows the results from the nationwide survey of 349 secondary social studies teachers. **Figure 12** *Study Participants’ Opinions on Race/ism* ![Graph showing study participants' opinions on race/ism] Comparing the participants to the nationwide results is necessary to determine how consistent the data between the two groups is. With higher consistency comes higher reliability (Chiang et al., 2015). Though the extent and depth of the interviews far surpassed that of the survey, the results show consistency between the two data sets regarding their awareness of their opinions on race/ism. Both groups cited the same views in their top five choices, meaning that most surveys (both the interview participants and the nationwide ones) claimed to hold the stated opinions. Both groups also shared the same least-selected views. The peach coloring in Tables 8 and 9 indicate where both groups shared the belief in the same ranking, indicating consistency between the racial consciousness of the interview participants is mainly in line with that of the nationwide survey participants. Total of 36 of 349 selected this answer | | Slavery ended long ago; I had nothing to do with it. | #3 least selected answer (tied) 11% 1 of 9 people selected this answer | #6 least selected answer 10% Total of 36 of 349 selected this answer | **Racial Optics and Social Studies Curriculum** At this point in the findings section, it is tempting to combine the participants’ racial lenses and racial (sub/un) consciousness levels and determine the clarity and awareness of their racial optics. However, as discussed in previous chapters, this is a very personal process, not one that I or anyone other than the individual should determine for themselves. Therefore, because the basis of this study is to determine the extent a teacher’s racial optics may have on their curriculum, the final piece of the findings must be to evaluate their curriculum to determine if the participants’ practice is consistent with their pedagogy. **Pedagogy** Pedagogy is commonly defined as the art and science of teaching, which includes a teacher’s methods, how students learn, and the delivered content. As part of the inclusion criteria for the study, each teacher needed some level of autonomy in their classroom. This was so I could ensure they chose and approved their curriculum rather than a curriculum they were required to present without personal input. Based on their interviews and journals, every participant reported that teaching facts and the complete history of the United States are part of their professional and patriotic duty: Do I think that there are things wrong with that history? Sure. Do I think that we don’t talk about everything that we could be talking about? For sure… And I’m sure that they [students] probably realize that. But, like, how are we supposed to actively teach that? Unless you’re cutting out ugly parts of the history? But you can be patriotic and still recognize that your country did wrong. (Amina) It’s interesting that I should be teaching patriotism. What does that mean? As I go through American history, I’m not supposed to talk about the bad stuff? Is that teaching patriotism? I feel that that’s just a very strange thing for a) government to mandate, a government to say, and b) I didn’t know, as a country, we had to find patriotism, for me to find a way to teach it. (Charles) I think part of being a patriot is acknowledging the terrible things that your country has done and taking ownership of it and doing better – not trying to sweep it under the rug or saying, ‘Well, that’s in the past, and it’s history’… That’s something that’s really important to me is giving the whole story, the whole story from all sides and kind of giving my students a chance to really think about how people felt. I think it makes them better people. (Ellen) And so, from my viewpoint, the history of United States is one of racial history, right? You have to teach- you have to talk about racism in order to understand the history of the United States of America. I mean, it plays such an incredibly important role in- so many of these big decisions. (Kenny) I teach facts. I’m going to talk about the Tulsa Riots because it’s a historical event. I’m going to talk about The Civil Rights Movement- it’s a historical…I teach history. And that’s what it is. (Nate) I think we should say like, ‘Hey, guys, we (America) have actually been pretty horrible in the past [and] We’ve done some great things like, you know, World War II - Okay, we’re definitely the good guys. [But] we’ve done some terrible things. How can we not do that again?’ versus this view that seems to think that, like, kids are going to be ashamed if they know their country’s done bad things. And I’m like, well, they [the students] didn’t do it. The only way to make people passionate is to give them something to actually fight for versus just like, ‘Oh, yes, America’s great,’ which doesn’t seem to hold any candle to my kids. (Nolan) But also, to some negative parts of history, that it doesn’t necessarily mean that you have to, like, be completely patriotic the whole time or sometimes being patriotic means claiming negative aspects of the history or negative events that happen. But I want to make sure that it’s both sides that we talk about chall- challenges and successes of the country. (Steve) It’s probably best just to deliver the content and allow the student to decide for themselves if they think this is positive or negative. Maybe that’s been the mistake is trying to get them to believe one thing. Just teach the content, and then they can- they’re going to decide what they want to decide. (Thomas) I try to be as unbiased and as objective as I can whenever I’m really introducing these topics… I do try to be, you know, just give them the facts. Tell them what happened. ‘Here’s what happened. Here’s who did it. Here’s what motivated them. Take it as you will.’ (Wyatt) I listed the traditional units for a U.S. history course during the interviews. Then, I asked the participants to respond with *yes* or *no* if they could, off the top of their heads, think of non-white examples or resources they include when they teach those units. While this was in no way a scientific or conclusive approach to take – the purpose of the exercise was to determine if the teachers’ inclusion of non-white history was included in their teaching of that topic to be recalled instantly. I also asked them to self-assess which of the five races the United States Census Bureau identified most frequently appeared in their overall curriculum and which they included least frequently. I also included Latine as a grouping because that ethnic group is often included as an entire group taught throughout U.S. history. Many participants reported including them as a non-white population often throughout their curriculum. |-------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------------| | **Note:** "XXX" indicates units the participant reported they did not teach or include in their curriculum either due to the course requirements or because they chose not to include it. Figure 15 Participant Top and Least Included Racial Groups in Their Curriculum | Name | 3 Races Most Included | 3 Races Least Included | Racial Group | Percentage of Participants Reporting Racial Group Inclusion in Top 3 in Their Curriculum | |--------|---------------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Ellen | White; Black; Indigenous | Not reported | Asian | 22% | | Charles| White; Black; Indigenous; Asian | Latino; Pac Islander | Black | 100% | | Kenny | White; Black; Indigenous | Pac Island; Latino; Asian | Indigenous/Native Alaskan | 56% | | Amina | White; Indigenous; Black | Asian | Latinx | 56% | | Nolan | White; Black | Pac Islander; Indigenous | Pac Islander/Hawaiian white | 0% | | Wyatt | White; Latinx; Black | Pac Island/Hawaii | | 100% | | Nate | Black; Latinx; Asian “white is a given” | Pac Island | | | | Steve | Black; Latinx | Hawaiian; Indigenous | | | | Thomas | White; Indigenous; Black | Pac Islander; Alaskan; Asian | | | Practice While participants may believe they include non-white people in their teaching of America’s history, and it may be their intention to do so, the actual reality of the situation is often different. Therefore, I requested access to the participants’ currently used curriculum so I could evaluate it for the inclusion or omission of their racial optics. I required a minimum of six documents from each participant and requested them to be their best representations of their curriculum. The full criteria for the curriculum analysis are found in Appendix O. When I completed the interviews and collected all of the curricula from the participants, I analyzed them using the Curriculum Analysis Rubric (Appendix P) I described in Chapter 3. Once I finished analyzing the participant’s curriculum, I compiled my findings into a Curriculum Profile (see Figure 5). These completed charts are located in Appendix S, though I present an example of the Wyatt curriculum as a sample within the text in Figure 15. This shows my analysis of the six curriculum documents Wyatt submitted to me: four quizzes, one PowerPoint presentation, and class lecture notes. When analyzing these documents, I listed the resource type, its title or unit, and the source. I then went through the following steps and repeated this process for each participant: 1. I identified if non-white racial groupings were present and, if so, which race was presented as a primary narrative of the curriculum. 2. I analyzed if the depiction of non-white racial groupings was being portrayed based on fact and, if so, determined if it was a positive(heroic), neutral, or negative(victim) depiction. 3. I did the same analysis of any white people in the document. 4. I examined the document to determine if it was presented from a particular non-white racial group’s perspective or if it was generically based in a neutrally historical manner. 5. I dissected the curriculum to see if it offered students any critical lens when learning about the racial groups (i.e., were students presented with/asked to look for information that caused them to consider a perspective critically and analytically). 6. I evaluated the documents to see if the curriculum might present multiple racial perspectives. I repeated this process for each document until the profile was completed. Curriculum Analysis Rubric measures level of inclusion of non-white perspectives and history in social studies content | Scoring Guide | Participant: Wyatt | Score: 12 | |---------------|--------------------|-----------| | **Unsatisfactory** | **Unclear** | **Attempted** | **Emerging** | **Excelling** | | **0 – 4 pts.** | This curriculum appears to whitewash history. Anyone who receives the information taught with this content will likely not learn the valuable contributions or rich history People of Color have within the United States. The curriculum – regardless of intentionality - will likely lead its students into falsely believing the white narrative is the only one important enough to be taught | **5 - 9 pts.** | This curriculum presents American history from a primarily white perspective, as it has traditionally been taught. It doesn’t allow sufficient availability to and teaching of the value and contributions People of Color have made to the country. There is little intentionality – if any – to include People of Color in America’s history beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included | **10 - 14 pts.** | This curriculum includes People of Color alongside the traditionally taught white narrative. However the white narrative remains the featured story, thereby marginalizing People of Color. Further intentionality needs to be contributed to offer equity to People of Color in America’s history. | **15 - 19 pts.** | This curriculum includes People of Color to a similar extent as white people in the teaching of American history. This curriculum reflects equality in that it offers a balanced narrative that allows People of Color to be normalized in the telling of American history. Further intentionality is needed to truly offer an equitable perspective of the impact People of Color have had on American history. | **20 - 24 pts.** | This curriculum elevates People of Color above the white narrative in order to give equitable perceptions of the value BIPOC have had in the telling of America’s history. Utilizing this curriculum would guide learners to see People of Color as valuable stakeholders in the progress and success of the country and presents history from their perspective, thus positively impacting how the learner understands various racial groups. | *Note:* The shaded blue area denotes the over score of the participant’s Curriculum Rubric, in this case, Wyatt’s curriculum fell into the ‘Attempted’ category for level of inclusion of non-white perspectives and history in social studies content. **Inclusion or Omission of Teachers’ Racial Optics in Their Curriculum** Research question three asked, *how have these teachers included or omitted their views of race within their curriculum?* To address this question, I needed to compare the participants’ racial optics, *perceived* pedagogy, and the curriculum evidence they supplied. To present these findings most succinctly, I have taken the results from the following data sets, which were then compiled into Table 10 for an overarching comparative chart: I then took Table 10 and compared its findings to each participant’s Curriculum Rubric and Scoring to answer the research question. My findings show that of the nine interview participants, 78% (seven out of nine) have created a curriculum and chosen resources that reflect their racial optics, intentionally or unintentionally. I did not see Nolan’s and Ellen’s racial optics reflected in their curriculum. They both described interest and passion for including multiple races in their curriculum; however, they have not done so – at least regarding the curriculum, they submitted for this study. This is shown in Table 11. ### Table 10 **Participants’ Self-Identified Racial Optics Versus Data Analysis of Participant Racial Optics** | Name | Participants’ Self-Reported Racial Optics | Participants’ Awareness of Racial Issues and Terms | Participants’ Perceived Inclusion of Multiple Races | Study’s Analyzed Data of Participants | |------|------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | | Figure 6 | Figures 9 & 10 | Figure 13 | Appendix Q | | Amina| Not Racist | Racially Conscious | Reports 100% inclusion of BIPOC | Ally | | Nolan| Ally | Racially Conscious | Reports 25% inclusion of BIPOC | Ally | | Wyatt| Not Racist | Racially Conscious | Reports 70% inclusion of BIPOC | Privilege | | Nate | Antiracist | Racially Conscious | Reports 60% inclusion of BIPOC | Ally | | Steve| Ally | Racially Conscious | Reports 100% inclusion of BIPOC | Undetermined | | Thomas| Ally | undetermined | Reports 78% inclusion of BIPOC | Ally | | Charles| Antiracist | Racially Conscious | Reports 100% inclusion of BIPOC | Antiracist | | Ellen | Ally | Racially Conscious | Reports 100% inclusion of BIPOC | Ally/Privileged | | Kenny| Ally | Racially Conscious | Reports 50% inclusion of BIPOC | Antiracist | **Note:** ‘pts.’ = points.
