Level: | undetermined | | | | | Figures 10&11 | | | | | Charles | Survey | Antiracist | Emerging | Yes – teachers’ racial optics are reflected in their curriculum. | | | Self- Report | Antiracist | | | | | Racial Con. Level: | Racially Conscious | | | | | Figures 10&11 | | | | | Ellen | Survey | Privileged and ally | Unsatisfactory | No – teachers’ racial optics are not reflected in their curriculum.| | | Self- Report | Ally | | | | | Racial Con. Level: | Racially Conscious | | | | | Figures 10&11 | | | | | Kenny | Survey | Antiracist | Emerging | Yes – teachers’ racial optics are reflected in their curriculum. | | | Self- Report | Ally | | | | | Racial Con. Level: | Racially Conscious | | | | | Figures 10&11 | | | | Teachers’ Racial Optics Included in Their Curriculum. *Amina.* Amina showed evidence of being racially conscious and is still learning and admitting personal bias. Her curriculum reflects some inclusions of BIPOC equitably and appropriately, yet not as consistently as she believes. Regardless, her racial optics are present in her curriculum content and resources. *Charles.* Charles is a self-reported antiracist with a personal investment in the equity of BIPOC. He has contributed personal time towards learning how to be an antiracist ally professionally. His curriculum primarily reflects his racial optics in that BIPOC are presented at times as the main narrative and requires students to think critically and from varying perspectives. *Kenny.* Kenny is a self-reported antiracist ally and is racially conscious. His curriculum highly reflects his racial optics, as he had chosen resources and books specifically to present varying perspectives and draw attention to the whitewashing of history. *Nate.* Nate is in the beginning stages of learning about antiracism. While he classifies himself as an antiracist, he reports that his actions do not reflect that. He is racially conscious, sensitive to BIPOC’s history of oppression, and includes equity and equality in his curriculum. While Nate may not yet be an *active* antiracist ally in his personal life, his curriculum does reflect this mentality. *Steve.* Steve is racially conscious of and sensitive to racial issues. Though he is a self-described ally, he has no personal connection with BIPOC. His ideals and mindset reflect allyship, and he has taken an interest in educating himself about racially charged issues. His curriculum attempts to include equitable teaching of BIPOC history within the United States. Thomas. Thomas’s mentality and thought process reflect allyship. His curriculum reflects this mentality, with multiple racial groups present throughout and mainly depicted from their perspective; BIPOC are often included in the main narrative. Wyatt. Wyatt is a self-proclaimed *not-racist* working through racism issues; he is racially conscious and aware of his privilege but does little to be sensitive to it or move beyond it. His curriculum similarly includes some sensitivity towards and teaching of BIPOC’s contributions to American history, though they are not as consistently present—which reinforces his curriculum reflects his optics. **Teachers’ Racial Optics Omitted from Their Curriculum.** Ellen. Ellen describes herself as an ally, but some of her interview answers reflect her white privilege. She is still learning about antiracism and is racially conscious, looking to learn more and diversify her content despite her homogenous surroundings. However, her curriculum does not reflect her mentality and interest in diversity. Instead, it presents American history as white except for traditionally taught interracial topics. Nolan. Nolan is racially conscious and expresses strong antiracist thoughts and opinions but has not acted on these thoughts. His curriculum minimally includes POC beyond traditional topics. While 78% of participants’ curricula reflected their racial optics, it is essential to note that not all curricula excelled at presenting a multiple racial approach to American history. In fact, none of the participants’ curricula reflected the highest rating on the Curriculum Rubric. Instead, there were three participants at the Emerging level (Charles, Kenny, and Thomas), four participants at the Attempted level (Amina, Wyatt, Nate, and Steve), one at the Unclear level (Nolan), and one at the Unsatisfactory level (Ellen). Figure 19 shows a visual representation similar to that found in Chapter 2 with the racial optics results from the participants. Why social studies teachers do not include multiple races equitably when teaching American history is an important question to ask – and one to which participants easily and readily responded, which is the subject of Chapter 6. **Figure 20** *Racial Optics of Participants* - **Racial Identity** - Which race(s) one identifies as - **Racial Lens** - One’s worldview/perspective because of their race - **Racial Unconscious** - Level of unawareness of/ignorance to race-based issues and disparities - **Racial Subconscious** - Level of indifference of/apathy towards race-based issues and disparities - **Racial Consciousness** - Level of awareness of race-based issues and disparities **Summary of Finding 2** In this chapter, I explored the findings of the phenomenological interview study I conducted to explore if and how a teacher’s racial optics impact what they teach in their social studies classrooms. I specifically answered research questions two and three - *how have secondary social studies teachers interpreted their racial lenses and consciousnesses?* and *how* has that been included or omitted in their curriculum? Ultimately, I discovered that 78% of my participants’ curriculum reflected their racial optics to some degree, though not fully comprehensive of their perspectives on race or entirely inclusive of multiple racial groups throughout their American history curriculum. The remaining 22% did not have a curriculum that reflected their racial optics. Chapter 6 will present the findings regarding the gap between the participants’ pedagogy and racial optics and their curriculum and practice. CHAPTER 6 FINDINGS 3 – RECONCILING THE GAP In Chapter 1, I introduced the study and presented its current context and the gap within the field that this study will fill. In Chapter 2, I reviewed the literature regarding the theoretical framework of this study – critical whiteness theory – and discussed the context and history of secondary social studies education that has existed up through the twenty-first century in the United States. Chapter 3 outlined the study’s qualitative methods, primarily phenomenological interviews with nine secondary U.S. history teachers. I also presented my approach to analyzing the collected data in the semi structured interviews, journals, surveys, and curriculum analyses. Chapter 4 detailed this study’s first set of findings, including how secondary social studies teachers experience(d) learning about race and racism within their educations, careers, and personal lives. Chapter 5 presented the second set of findings for this study, which consisted of determining and understanding the participants’ racial optics and how they may or may not be reflected in their curriculum. This chapter will present the third and final set of findings discussing the gap between secondary social studies teachers’ racial optics and their practice. First, I wanted to determine why these participants are not consistently teaching multiple - if not all - racial groups when teaching the history of America, even though all five racial groups have contributed to America’s history since its foundation. Therefore, I asked each teacher what roadblocks or hindrances exist for them in their situations that prevent them from teaching history how they want to and with multiple races within the main narrative. Each participant cited numerous reasons. The Responsibility of Gatekeeping Gatekeeping in the social studies classroom determines what content is taught and how history content is filtered for student learning (Hawley & Whitman, 2020; Thornton, 1989). While traditionally, teachers have been the gatekeepers of social studies content in schools, there has been significant attention in the last five years in the United States to what teachers teach in social studies classrooms. Parents, politicians, and media pushback, specifically about how students learn about race and racial perspectives within U.S. history courses, has come with an onslaught of regulations, mandates, and laws. These mandates prevent teachers from discussing topics of American history that may cause children of a particular race to feel guilty about atrocities committed by people of their race decades and centuries in the past or even in present-day. The participants that spoke about critical race theory legislations, adapting curriculum to include multiple race narratives, or hindrances in their situations that prevent them from teaching what and how they want all described reasons of government oversight, state/district guidelines, and fear. Guidelines by District/State All nine participants had reservations or outright objections to the state or federal government controlling what is taught in their classrooms. While Thomas and Charles stated they did not believe the government should speak on social studies content, others went into more specific detail as they thought through the question of who, if anyone, should determine what secondary classrooms around the country are teaching. Kenny considered the question carefully: I think there should be some sort of oversight. I don’t know if they should mandate or ban, though I don’t know if they should go to the point where they are dictating curriculum…we go back to this idea of like…as a teacher, I am a professional, and I should be given credit for my professionalism, but then I can think of plenty of teachers who are not professional, who- you know, we might want to have not discussed things with kids, you know? But I would say no. Mandating or banning? No, I don’t think so. Steve had firm and specific opinions about whom he believed should be making content decisions in the classroom. His argument largely echoed those of several other participants: I think it should be up to the school board, the teacher, the professional learning community…here, we have discussions about what we’re going to teach what topics and, especially world geography, but U.S. history has one as well, that they can kind of have conversations about what gets involved…I really want to when I close my door; I want to be able to teach whatever I want. I want to have those discussions. And I’m certainly welcoming anybody in that wants to be a part of that. But I don’t want to be told, ‘Hey, no, you can’t.’ I just want like an overview of like, here are the standards, and there’s lots of leeway, but I don’t want specifically, ‘Hey, you can’t teach this. You can’t teach that.’ Because I know my kids…I think having [an] antiracism or just a race set of standards for discussion questions would be really helpful for most teachers. Ultimately, several participants - Steve, Kenny, Thomas, and Wyatt - believe that social studies content should be determined partly by the teacher or department and partly by the school board or some other local oversight committee. They also believe that standards should define the nature and consistency of multiple racial histories included and/or required within secondary social studies. While Wyatt pondered the idea of having a national set of guidelines under specific parameters: “…like, hey, we pulled educators from all 50 states, and they came together, and they said, ‘This is what you should probably be teaching for geography, history, government, whatever it might be.’ Maybe something loose, not necessarily mandate.” Amina pointed out the danger in having government determine what is taught in social studies classrooms: So, I understand from a country’s standpoint, how you would be concerned about what’s being taught specifically in social studies classrooms, just because you want citizens to grow up and, I guess, respect your country…. like I understand that. However, if you are doing that by banning things or by hiding your own history, then you are not creating citizens who are critical thinkers. You’re going to create just very patriotic [nationalistic] citizens, which is totally fine. They also run the country, but you need people to like, be able to kind of [examine the] nuances of history. The thing is that if you really do cut all that out, then you’re just going to continue the system that *that* built. Participants would describe hesitation and fear of taking bolder steps to include multiple racial perspectives from this same concept throughout their curriculum. **Fear and Overregulation** When asked about oversight and regulations imparted in the social studies classrooms, many participants relayed stories of colleagues who had trouble. Nate discussed a teacher who had Fox News on in his room one day and was reported because he was teaching critical race theory – even though he was not – it was part of a news story on the broadcast. The students told their parents that CRT was brought up in class that day, despite the state’s mandate against it. Nate, however, noted that he was less concerned because he was confident that the history of racism in the United States would cause strong emotion. However, despite the ban, he uses history to protect his job security: ‘if a parent confronts me, I say- “I teach facts. I’m going to talk about the Tulsa Riots because it’s a historical event. I’m going to talk about The Civil Rights Movement- it’s historical. …I teach history.”’ Kenny also described a teacher in his department who received pushback: “The sociology teacher was teaching [something about] BLM, and they got some issues with parents. And the freshman ELA - they teach *The Hate You Give*, and so they got in some issues with parents as well.” Ellen, Amina, and Nolan described their fears about teaching race and racism in America’s history. Ellen has never had pushback from parents, but she expressed her fear: *I want [the students] to see the story from all sides. But I also don’t want to overstep because if I- if I accidentally go too far and make it look like I’m trying to push any sort of agenda, there’s going to be parents down my throat…. parents misinterpreting it as me pushing a liberal agenda or something like that, where I wouldn’t even consider myself necessarily liberal at all.* Amina admitted to outright avoidance of discussing current issues with her students: The pushback from parents is one—really one of the main reasons I don’t do current politics just because I don’t, not that I think that anything that any [of my students]—like I have a really good relationship with them. Not that I think anyone will call anyone out, but I do live in [the South]. Nolan described his fear of the long-term effects of the overregulation of racism events taught in social studies. “It makes me afraid that…teachers are going to backslide out of fear and just not talk about things that they’re supposed to because they don’t want to deal with angry parents…” While this is not the first time in America’s history that classroom content and curriculum have been politicized (i.e., Scopes Trial), it is undoubtedly the first time it has been this rampant and expeditious, likely because of social media. Ultimately, Americans must determine the collective goal of the U.S. history classroom for secondary students—a question that has been asked since the country’s founding. Historian Adam Laats (2015) writes: Is the point of history class to introduce the young Americans to their heritage of heroes, the glories of American history? Or is history class supposed to make young people into critical examiners of their society, a true civic education that teaches American young people to question every bit of received wisdom and be ready to change what needs changing? (para. 16). Indeed, this is the purpose of the NCSS and their National Curriculum Standards for Social Studies. While they are not federally mandated, they are the common guide states use in defining their goals for social studies students. **Lack of Time** For social studies teachers, the lack of time they have in their classroom to cover their content has remained a part of the challenge of the profession’s nature. However in 21st century United States, several factors further impede teachers’ ability to now not only cover their content but to do so in a manner that allows for students to see the full scope of America’s true narrative. Said factors include issues with standards, requirements of standardized testing, student’s disinterest in their own education and learning loss following the COVID-19 pandemic, According to the National Center for Education Statistics (2018) - the most recent statistic on statewide social studies assessments administered by grade - 29 states require some sort of state exam for social studies. Eight states require the U.S. citizenship test; eleven require a U.S. history test; ten require other social studies tests such as government, civics, economics, or generic social studies. Of the nine participants, only Amina, Nolan, and Wyatt resided in states where social studies were a tested subject. Therefore, it is likely not a coincidence that they were the only three participants to have strong opinions on state standards and testing as being a hindrance in what and how they teach. Nolan spoke at length on his state’s testing: The problem is my curriculum - I teach a tested subject… I have to teach…. the state standards. So, while I would love to say, ‘oh, let’s go beyond it and explore these perspectives,’ the reality is, I have enough trouble just getting my kids to understand the things that they need for the test. It’s fucking awful. I hate it. I absolutely hate it. It’s the wrong way to approach teaching history 1,000,000%. But the reality is that I just don’t have the room to incorporate that because I’ve got to get these stupid, bland, unimportant things out of the way. And unless they include it, there’s not much time, I can throw it in. But really, yeah, I’m not….I’m not teaching it [multiple race groups’ history] enough. But I’m not sure how to, given the constraints placed on me….Unless it’s a specific state standard that mentions it….those are the considerations - what’s the best way to get the standard across? Anything else is kind of icing on the cake at the last minute. It’s never really discussed – like, that would be a personal decision on the teacher, but it’s never discussed in planning. Nolan’s concern and frustration were palpable. Amina was the only participant who described her state’s standards as being the cause for white social studies: “People don’t care that most of our standards are white-centered. Just cause I have the freedom to [teach multiple racial perspectives], I don’t have time for it.” Nate lives in a state that just eliminated its standardized testing of U.S. history: “That was a hindrance, but we don’t have that anymore. We rewrote our own pretest and posttest for that. Right now, there’s not really too many hindrances.” Student Apathy and Ability There is no denying that teachers have become exhausted and frustrated. Teaching unlimited content in a limited time, along with the havoc the COVID-19 pandemic has had on student learning, the participants were not reluctant to admit they were exhausted and frustrated. While they would love to incorporate more racial groups in their American history content, teachers are often just concerned with keeping their students at grade level and engaged in their education. Ellen used a word cited enough to be an in-vivo code – apathetic. “The other thing is apathetic children…if it doesn’t pertain directly to them, they’re not very concerned. I’m struggling with a way to make it interesting to them.” Thomas, Wyatt, and Nolan were much blunter in their description of student apathy: Some of these kids get it, but by and large, it’s just trying to get them to care. And that’s been the real big obstacle this year, last year, too, but this year, as well, trying to get them, you know, *just do the work just give a rip – please - about something*. And that’s been the biggest kind of hurdle that me and, you know, every other teacher is facing. (Wyatt) But the reality is, I’m just trying to get them to understand the most basic things to pass and graduate high school at this point. That sounds very sad. And I’m not trying to like demean my students but with all the struggles that are going on with COVID, and the economic problems that so many of them are facing, drug abuse, and mental health. Like, I think the multicultural perspective seems like such a low like it’s something to goal, but we’ve got way more pressing and immediate concerns. I guess. (Nolan) Step one is like, get the kid in their seat, and step two is…we’ll worry about that when we get there… So, you’re very limited in that capacity of how much you can actually get done with the students you have in front of you. So that’s probably the biggest limit to, like, how fast we can move is, you know, cuz some years I have to move faster than others, just depends on the class. (Thomas) While ultimately, student interest and engagement are critical factors impacting student learning (Harackiewicz et al., 2016), there has been an undeniable shift in student engagement in the last three years. Researchers will study the after-effects of the pandemic on education for years to come. However, watching the exodus of educators from the profession clarifies that student apathy has increased (Walker, 2022). In contrast, the ability has decreased to the point that many educators feel their efforts will not have a significant impact (Dorn et al., 2021; Klein, 2021). **Social Studies Content** The most common hindrance shared amongst participants is time. When discussing their lack of time, they most often referred to the depth and length of the social studies content they must cover in a course – as history is ever-expanding. Charles and Thomas noted the value of a survey course to preempt more in-depth content and elective courses. Charles specifically mentioned having seen high school American history spanning a two-year course, which he highly praised. Ellen also commented that a multi-year American history course would be ideal because she struggled to give quality instruction to the period her course requires her to teach: “I’m supposed to cover U.S. history from 1500 to today. So……that’s not happening… that’s just not enough time at all.” This is a common complaint from social studies teachers, and these participants described how they needed to omit material to reach the period of history their course required. Even an elective course in the 1960s that only spans ten years can have such depth that the teacher can struggle, like Steve, for example: Usually, we talk about the space race, but that’s been cut because we just don’t have time. I usually spend two weeks on the counterculture and the protests against the Vietnam War. That’s, that’s been cut. So, it’s- if I had more time, I definitely would be spending more time covering material, the subjects we have, but also the ones that we miss. Charles said he was willing to include more diversity in his curriculum if he was only empowered to do so: Charles: I struggle with that question every single day. And truth be told, there’s not enough time for everything. So, you do have to omit quite a bit. You try your best… Researcher: Do you feel that all of these races *should* be equally represented in social studies? Charles: Yeah, it’d be great! Give me the time. Give me the curriculum; let’s do it! Amina also contemplated the logistics of including more diversity: I don’t think you can equally represent them in American history and still teach the standard like- American history…..Do I think you could do a full-color version of American history? I do think you could. I don’t know if there’s a curriculum out there for it. But you would definitely not be hitting your standards. I just- I don’t see how you could be doing that. But that would be a really interesting elective class. To this last point, the impact of whitewashed social studies is evidenced: American history is commonly known to be white, despite the millions of American-born non-white people who have contributed and resided within the country for hundreds of years. But suppose one wants to learn about the history and contributions of said non-white Americans. In that case, they are better served by taking an elective, re: optional, non-standard course, whereas the history of white Americans is – and always has been – presented as the main narrative in traditional, required U.S. history. **Traditional white Social Studies** It may be tempting for some to argue that American history has not been whitewashed and that the liberal left is trying to erase or replace America’s history with unpatriotic perspectives. However, this study provides evidence that Americans have been taught – if even unintentionally – to consider white people as *the standard* and non-white people as *the other*, thus perpetuating racial strife within the country. Steve, Charles, Ellen, Kenny, and Amina described whitewashed American history as a factor in instructing their students. Steve described having trouble with his Hispanic students not being able to relate to the social studies content: Let’s say…we’re learning about white people in the United States, I’ve had conversations where they [my Hispanic students] just kind of feel like this is just *more of the same*, and they don’t really connect with it because it’s like- ‘not only did it happen in the past, 100 years ago, but like, none of my family members would have even been remotely part of it. Or if they would have been, they wouldn’t have been a voice in it.’ Is this because Hispanics were not residing in or contributing to American history 100 years ago? No. The American history curriculum traditionally focuses on white people and their contributions to the country’s progress. Charles and Ellen also reported their struggle in helping non-white students connect to American history because the curriculum is so focused on white Americans that it may or may not have impacted their students’ lives today. In one very subtle statement in her journal, Amina spoke volumes regarding the whitewashing of American history: ‘I find it hard to put in People of Color [to the curriculum] without keeping it tied to slavery.’ Amina’s difficulty incorporating POC, specifically Black Americans, in American history content other than the traditionally taught events like slavery or Civil Rights revealed that for her, it is not standard to consider Black American history outside of those traditional topics. Because she struggled to include POC without keeping it tied to slavery proves that some Americans have been taught U.S. history with an erroneous perspective that slavery and Civil Rights were the most significant – or maybe only – events involving Black people. **Department and School Complications** A final hindrance of teachers inability to appropriately and fully incorporate multiple racial histories in their curriculum are issues within their respective departments and schools. Most of the teachers interviewed reported that including multiple races in their curriculum may be viewed as problematic in their work environment either due to the levels at which they are required to collaborate with their department, limiting their ability to insert their personal choices in curriculum and content, or issues with racism amongst the staff that convey the level at which their colleagues and employers may be adverse to further diversity inclusion in the content. Collaboration Versus Autonomy Collaboration was cited as a factor in not incorporating more races in their curriculum for several teachers. Because they work with a team of teachers to decide the curriculum, their voice, while valued, is not the only one considered. Ultimately, working together as a team to plan curriculum and courses has been seen as beneficial in multiple regards (Fullan, 2015). However, social studies draws teachers passionate about various issues and aspects of history. Nate noted that for his department, collaboration to create a curriculum is often complicated by varying personalities and opinions, which can be a unique issue in the field of social studies: There, you got the extreme conservative to the extreme liberal. You know, most departments… don’t measure this way. But social studies teachers do… We may disagree as a team, we may disagree on tests and stuff like that, but we can do what we want in our classroom. While Charles reported he does plan autonomously, he has also had experience planning with colleagues. He also was able to articulate the dynamics of having to collaborate with a department and that it can complicate how diverse the curriculum gets to be and how to talk with teachers who are reluctant to imbend more diversity: Perhaps sometimes with our departments, it’s a little difficult because we do have to co-plan, and you have some teachers that are a little, I think, more strict in the content that they want to deliver and have a certain approach that’s a little more inflexible… but I do have co-workers that use the word ‘can’t’ a lot. ‘I can’t do that.’ ‘I can’t teach that.’ And that is, you know, a different thing you have to tackle because that’s something you also see in the students, but you know how adults can be when you talk to them like a student. So, you have to think about switching your minds from teacher-talking-to-student to teacher-talking-to-a-teacher and find the best approach. Teachers like Charles, Thomas, and Kenny, whose curriculum was the most inclusive of multiple races, were also three of the four teachers who reported having complete autonomy in their curriculum planning. While there may be a correlation between teacher curricular freedom and inclusive, multiple-race American history taught at the secondary level, this study is beyond the scope. Racism Amongst Staff While the social studies teachers I interviewed did not report this hindering their teaching, many did comment on how racism within their department or administration seemed to be a factor. For example, Nate described having more students funneled into his class because the other teachers are not able/willing to teach said students: “my classroom’s safe, they can come in, and they know that there’ll be loved and that I won’t judge. So that’s why they funnel kids into my classroom.” Amina reported issues with her principal – who is the same race as her – and the racism against Black students and parents Amina has witnessed: I do not get along with my East Asian principal because she has this- like she- she will never acknowledge it, but if two kids are out of dress code, she’s only going to pick on the Black kids; she will never call out the East Asian kid on dress code. Everybody knows she does that. She doesn’t take parent conferences with a Black parent on her own. But she will with any other parent. Amina also described interactions with her white colleague in her journal, “No one wants to be the token Person of Color. There’s a girl at work who clearly still sees us like that. She’s white and it’s the number one thing that bugs me about her.” Amina described in her interview how her colleague asks for Amina’s input about winter holidays other than Christmas but also decorates teachers’ doors with Christmas decorations, which Amina feels is inappropriate and isolating in a public-school setting. Nolan reported in his journal that while he knows there are some adults in his school that are racist, he also believes this is a fading phenomenon: Pretty much every student I see at my school is very antiracist, and I honestly can’t say in my nine years of teaching here that I’ve seen more than 1 or 2 cases of genuine racism among students - I’ve seen more from staff. This generation doesn’t seem to carry the same notions as the older generation, and it makes me incredibly hopeful for the future. Though Nolan described this amongst the staff in his school, he also describes the racism within the Southern, suburban community he teaches in as a problem. For example, on the day we interviewed, he described a head administrator in his district who had just resigned because of backlash she received for comments she made about systemic racism existing in their district: But she admitted the systemic racism was a problem in all schools in the district, and she made a diversity, equality kind of group. She really wanted to engage on that topic with the community; that was really her big legacy, actually, was trying to combat that. But I think that, along with the mask mandates and the fact that some board members have resigned, but I know she was receiving like death threats on her lawn. And just the absolute insanity of it all...that boggles my mind. So, it’s a raging political issue, as it is all over the country. While her resignation may not have directly hindered Nolan from including multiple racial groups in his curriculum, her impact on the district’s approach to systemic racism and equality will no longer exist. The very act of her sudden resignation and its subsequent news story sent a message of what the community would and would not tolerate. **Summary of Findings 3** This chapter describes the data findings that explain the gap between teachers’ racial optics and their pedagogical practice. Through the data analysis process, I discovered that while 78% of teachers’ racial optics are reflected in their curriculum – whether intentionally included or not – teachers still have difficulty implementing multiple (re: three or more) racial groupings within their curriculum. This is for a variety of reasons, but the main explanations discovered are the limited time to teach unlimited content, the fear of racial inclusion being misinterpreted by parents or administration, and the overall sense of apathy from students and exhaustion from teachers after three years of COVID-19 stress within America’s schools. In the closing chapter, I will explore the implications of the study’s findings on the field and discuss the role social studies classrooms have in perpetuating racism by unintentionally continuing to centralize the white narrative in American history, as well as explore viable options to combat this racism epidemic within one generation. I will also outline the overall limitations of my study and make future recommendations for how a researcher could expand on this study’s foundations. CHAPTER 7 INSIGHTS FROM THE STUDY In Chapter 1, I introduced the current problems with secondary social studies education and its connections to racism issues permeating American society. In Chapter 2, I presented research on the background of social studies education within the United States and described how it has intrinsically been and continues to be whitewashed. Based on a theoretical framework of critical whiteness theory, I posited that should educators critically examine their racial optics, they would be better positioned to present a more diversified narrative of America’s history. In Chapter 3, I outlined the qualitative methodology for this study and described the various methods by which I collected and analyzed data. Chapters 4, 5, and 6 each described a wealth of information on the three main results I found in the data - how social studies teachers themselves learned about race in America’s history, the development of teachers’ racial optics, and the gap between the teachers’ perceptions of race and their social studies curricular choices. In this chapter, I conclude the investigation by describing the insights from the research, discussing the implications, and making recommendations to the social studies field for changes and how it can contribute to moving the United States into a collective, full-color view of America’s multiple (re: poly) racial narrative. Summary Since the establishment of the United States public education system, the social studies curriculum (history, geography, etc..) has highlighted the accomplishments, heroism, and contributions of white Americans (Hughes, 2021). As the field developed and outlined specific courses for social studies during the 20th century, secondary United States History continued to focus on white Americans as the primary population contributing to the country’s success, often ignoring Black, Indigenous, and People of Color (Conwright, 2021; Hughes, 2021). This whitewashing of American history normalized and standardized white people while simultaneously marginalizing or erasing the contributions of People of Color. I posit this has vastly contributed to the ‘othering’ of BIPOC and developing an erroneous and incomplete picture of America where students have learned that BIPOC has not positively shaped the country’s establishment and subsequent success. Additionally, in the mid-20th century, when de facto discriminatory practices prevented People of Color from advancing as educators, administrators, and politicians, the field of education was guaranteed a majority white population (Gladwell, 2021; Kendi, 2019). This may have ensured that educators – having been taught an established whitewashed version of American history – would have been conditioned to hold a white-standardized version of the country’s narrative. Consequently, this would affect how they presented American history to their students, thereby perpetuating this false narrative generation after generation, intentional or unintentional. Of course, this scenario would have been applied to Teachers of Color if they had been educated by white teachers, which is likely the case. With this in mind, I conceptualized this study: teachers need to analyze their educations and how they learned about America’s history – critically examining if the version of history presented to them was whitewashed. They also need to critically assess their racial optics, which have resulted partially from how they learned about race and racial conflict in American history and partially from their upbringing and experiences. This will determine if they are also (unintentionally) perpetuating white social studies to the detriment of their student’s learning. I deduce that an intentional introspective reflection of how teachers view their race and other races and how that intersects with their curricular choices is the only hope for eradicating white social studies. With an increased call to acknowledge diversity in the social studies curriculum in the late 20th century (Banks, 1987; 1991; Ladson-Billings, 1995), the NCSS, states, and national recommendations have significantly revised social studies standards. Regardless, BIPOC’s presence in America’s narrative has increasingly become a focal point for the field of social studies, and there has been progress to normalize BIPOC in American history courses (Au, 2020; King, 2017). That is until the recent backlash against critical race theory has misguided legislators to pass mandates in seventeen states that restrict social studies teachers’ ability to discuss race and racism in America’s history (Schwartz, 2022). Therefore, it is even more vital that educators examine their curricular choices and racial optics to be knowledgeable and systematically incorporate a fuller and more accurate picture of America’s history, despite legislation protecting white social studies. **Limitations** This study’s limitation is the use of various racial lens scales given to the teachers during their journaling. After reviewing dozens of scales utilized in previous studies and widely shared, I selected the three that I found the most compelling (Appendices A, B, and C). However, these scales are not scientifically validated and result from the research and insight of various scholars over time. It would have been ideal for teachers to review racial lens scales that accurately depict the varying aspects of how one views race. However, no single, conclusive scale exists that is scientifically tested and validated. There is extensive research on various types of scales that measure degrees of racism (Henry & Sears, 2002; Neville et al., 2000), levels of whiteness (Helms and Carter, 1990; Knowles & Peng, 2005; Miller, 2017), and beliefs regarding white privilege (Hays et al., 2007). Still, Schooley et al. (2019) articulated the need for more whiteness measurements to be developed, while Worrell et al. (2017) recommended their study introducing a new tool for measuring racial identity to be replicated with larger sample sizes in the varying non-white race subgroupings. **Discussion** I have identified two significant implications from this research that explores how secondary social studies teachers learned about race and how their racial optics may or may not be reflected in their curricular choices. The first implication of the results is that students in the United States receive an extremely watered-down and highly edited version of American history because of the variety of issues and constraints placed on teachers. The second implication of the results is that American history curriculum and standards do not require teachers to present an equitable nor accurate portrayal of BIPOC but continues to allow for the traditional whitewashing of America’s narrative, despite efforts to incorporate terms such as *multicultural* and *diversity*. **Implication 1: Derisory Secondary American History Courses** Coupled with the incomplete picture of teaching America’s narrative due to whitewashing is a vast amount of material teachers work to cover when teaching in the standard nine-month period for a secondary course. Because American history courses cover such an expanse of content, let alone incorporate varying perspectives and teach content knowledge skills, teachers are often unable to cover the entirety of their planned content because there is too much material. This results in a further watered-down and limited presentation of American history, let alone world history. Understanding the context of current issues is an essential part of becoming an active participant in society (NCSS, 2010). One of the main implications of this study is that secondary teachers are set up to fail when delivering pertinent content, particularly regarding race history and racism issues that have and continue to permeate American society. Teachers in this study identified three roadblocks in their respective situations that prevent them from teaching how and what their students most want and need to learn: time constraints, student apathy, and the government as gatekeepers of education. **Time Constraints.** The current model for secondary education in United States social studies typically includes a course in world history and a course in U.S. history at the sixth through the eighth-grade level and again at the ninth through the twelfth-grade level. There is also usually a third year of social studies offered both at the middle school and high school levels, determined by state and district, and includes other social studies content areas such as geography, political science, psychology, sociology, economics, or a course in non-white history such as African American studies. Some districts and states require a fourth year of social studies, but most only offer this as an option. Other states and districts allow for U.S. history and/or world history to be taken over two years. Given that teachers are expected to cover the United States’ 400+ year history in a single year, let alone the entirety of the human experience in a single course of world history (sometimes but not always divided into two sections), teachers have reported much of their content is severely edited. Some even eliminated to cover the expanse of information. Thus, exploring and analyzing race history or even the history of varying racial groups within the United States is virtually impossible, despite their training or desire to do so. Teachers in the study repeatedly reported that they did not have enough time in their day – or over the year – to address race to the extent they knew their students needed and would benefit. Suppose teachers cannot address topics such as racism or incorporate multiple racial groups in the way they factually existed in American history. In that case, there is certainly cause for concern that other essential topics such as gender, LGBTQ+, economic inequality, and more are similarly being overlooked and severely edited if addressed at all. **Apathy.** Teachers in this study also reported that their students often prevent them from going in-depth into content or adding to the existing curriculum because the students have become apathetic since the COVID-19 pandemic. Most teachers reported having difficulty keeping students interested in the lessons or their grades and struggled to find topics on which they could spark student interest. These teachers identified this as a newer phenomenon, even more dire than typical pre- and teenage student apathy, since returning to full-time, in-person learning post-pandemic. They attribute this to a) the learning loss experienced by secondary students over the last three years and b) the lax nature of grading and accountability in schools because of the scramble to develop content, balance mental health, and handle COVID-related loss and illness. Teachers reported that because of these two unique features shared by students across the country over the last three years, they have difficulty engaging students in their learning and progress and therefore focus more on creating dynamic lessons rather than widening the breadth of their racial integration in the content. **Government as Gatekeepers.** “Since January 2021, 42 states have introduced bills or taken other steps that would restrict teaching critical race theory or limit how teachers can discuss racism and sexism\(^{20}\)…Seventeen states have imposed these bans and restrictions either \(^{20}\) As of August 2022, states without any action or bill regarding CRT include California, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii, Massachusetts, Nevada, Oregon, Vermont, as well as D.C., and Puerto Rico through legislation or other avenues\textsuperscript{21},” (Schwartz, 2022, para. 4). While teachers in this study reported they do not use the phrasing “critical race theory” in their classroom (most were unaware of its definition), it has become a phrase that has had many erroneous meanings attributed to it, causing unnecessary panic amongst parents and school boards, prompting state legislators to write bills banning its use in public schools, even at the collegiate level (Arrojas, 2022). Regardless of what a teacher knows or does not know about CRT, they now must use an abundance of caution when presenting racial history topics so they do not violate the laws. States have left their bills ambiguous regarding precisely what cannot be discussed and, if it is, the way it may be presented. However, teachers have stated in this study that they fear being reported, recorded, or removed from the classroom if they discuss historical facts and events centered around racism. Virginia Governor Youngkin recently instituted an anonymous tip line wherein parents, students, or community members can call to report a teacher who may be discussing race and gender history in their classes (NBC12 Newsroom, 2022). Because the government has now taken on the role of gatekeeping social studies content, teachers are left confused and unsure of what they are allowed to teach about, what they cannot say, and how to go about teaching America’s history without talking about laws and actions that have shaped the country - such as the 13th and 15th amendments, the Chinese Exclusion Act, Jim Crow, and redlining, as they are now considered to be history that reflects CRT. \textsuperscript{21} As of August 2022, the states that passed legislation against CRT are Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Idaho, Iowa, Kentucky, Mississippi, Montana, New Hampshire, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, and Virginia Implication 2: America’s Narrative is Polyracial While U.S. history has and continues to normalize white Americans often, some states have included BIPOC in content by adding words like multicultural, ethnicity, or even just diversity to their standards and course titles. However, these terms are either misleading or inaccurate, which may be why whitewashing continues to be an unintentional result for some teachers: they are not being required to think explicitly and systematically about which racial groups they are or are not including. For example, multicultural refers to various (meaning more than two) cultures. This differs from various races, as culture and race are alike but not exchangeable. Even the word multiracial implies one person of multiple races, just as biracial means one person of two races. I posit that the term polyracial social studies or polyracial history reflects a course that presents the collective experiences and perceptions of multiple, if not all, racial groupings. If the field is interested in America’s narrative truly reflecting the contributions and history of all the racial groups who have built and resided in the country, then American history courses need a specific word for the equitable and intentional inclusion of multiple race groups in social studies. While the wording may seem trite, it is vital that the field accurately and explicitly identifies the learning goals it wants growing citizens and residents to be taught, which begins with accurate and specific wording. Polyracial Social Studies. The United States government recognizes five main race groups - African/African-American/Black, Asian, Indigenous (including Native Americans and Alaska), Pacific-Islander/Hawaiian, and white (U.S. Census Bureau, 2022). Arguably, technology and progress have blurred the lines between races (Chou, 2017), as has the discovery and widely agreed-upon notion within the scientific community that race is not based on genetics or biology (Gannon, 2016). Chou (2017) notes that the public remains unconvinced that race is merely a social construct, as some celebrate their racial identity proudly as their blood ancestry and lineage. Others note that the offspring of two people of the same race will result in a child of the same race rather than a random race, indicating biology is indeed a component of race. Regardless of if a race is biological, Chou goes on, it is undoubtedly a social and political concept, which is the main point when considering how it should be addressed and woven throughout the American history curriculum. America always has and continues to define race as associated with one’s skin color, ancestry, and ethnicity. Because laws based on race have systematically and steadily prevented non-white people from the same privileges as white people (re: Slavery, Chinese Exclusion Act, etc..), entire racial groups have generally been excluded from the acknowledgment of their existence and contributions to the United States unless it pertained in some way to white people. Therefore, teachers, teacher educators, curriculum writers, and lawmakers must intentionally and specifically revise America’s history, which I argue is most efficient and effective if done in the social studies classroom. The story that should be told of a country is the story that took place – not pieces of it based on one’s skin color, physical ability, gender (identity), citizenship/economic/immigration status, ethnicity, etc. History remains reliable if it is accurate, and accurate history is not written by the winners but by anyone attending the proverbial battle – the winners, the losers, the innocent bystanders, and all groups who have participated, observed, or been effected by any given historical event. **Recommendations** I recommend several changes to reconceptualize how to systematically and intentionally develop a U.S. history curriculum that accurately and fully presents America’s narrative from each racial group’s angle. These recommendations can be taken independently of one another or combined with others for a more effective and prompt response to the ongoing racial issues plaguing the country. **Independent Educator Changes** Central to this study and my self-inventory of my racial optics was the review of literature, media, and racial optics scales. By reviewing the racial optics scales independently or in a professional development/educational setting, I addressed difficult questions that I had never known to ask myself. Most participants reported the same. Reviewing these scales, evaluating oneself, and stepping back to look objectively and candidly at one’s thoughts, behaviors and experiences can be sobering when done with intention. I, along with most of the participants, also experienced this self-reflective process while listening to podcasts, watching movies, or reading books by People of Color to immerse myself in their experiences, perceptions, and to learn from them. Regardless of how woke one becomes during this process, I argue it would be challenging to undergo such introspection with sincerity and not have it result in some level of self-analysis and racial consciousness. Perhaps there is still work to be done. However, being aware of that work is probably the most challenging part. **Departmental Changes** Secondary social studies teachers are often encouraged to collaborate and plan as a department. I recommend that departments intentionally explore how to implement polyracial curricula and materials in their units. Every unit should incorporate stories, events, and histories of at least three non-white racial groups. While often U.S. history courses already include white and Black history, it would be incredibly transformative to highlight the Native Americans during the Civil War or learn about Asian Americans’ contributions during the Industrial Revolution. *Every unit* should include multiple (re: poly) racial groups because numerous racial groups have existed in the United States since before its foundations. Intentionally instructing students not just about Black and white people during the Civil Rights Movement but also Asian Americans, Indigenous, and Latine ethnic groups would allow students to see that all non-white groups had similar struggles and powerful stories worth being told. Internet access makes researching these untold stories more accessible. **School and District Changes** A natural educator response to the last two recommendations would be regarding the lack of time given to teachers to plan and implement this extra information. While time in the classroom varies from school to school and can change based on any given day or teacher, professional development is one commonality amongst all educators in the United States. Unfortunately, teachers often find this valuable training time inapplicable (Gulamhussein, 2013; Strauss, 2014). However, should each school allow their social studies departments to develop these curricular resources and collaboratively dive into previously unexplored historical events, the benefits to students’ abilities to see the United States through a positive, unifying polyracial lens will only strengthen, thus normalizing People of All Colors in their understanding of American history. Additionally, it would be valuable for educators to implement polyracial social studies into their curriculum by adding professional development with prepared resources centering on People of Color that are ready for teachers to use. Other professional developments could open conversations about implicit bias and help teachers learn to navigate difficult history and controversial discussions in their classrooms. **Higher Educational Changes** To start affecting powerful, lasting change, it is the responsibility of teacher educators – particularly in social studies, but arguably in any discipline – to begin helping students learn to identify their racial optics and expose them to the necessity and benefit of polyracial course content. Each methods course needs to spend time guiding pre-service teachers to become adept at incorporating three or more non-white racial groups, resources, events, and histories into every unit they plan. This same knowledge would also ingrain in pre-service teachers the ability to incorporate other non-traditionally acknowledged marginalized groups into their units. Colleges of Education should work with their university’s office that focuses on equity and diversity to ensure required teacher education courses include a curriculum that guides future teachers through examining their own implicit bias and the systemic policies and institutions that have created and contributed to BIPOC’s inequality. For example, the University of Wisconsin-Madison recently increased the school’s ethnic studies requirement from 3 to 6 credits, which will go into effect for the class of 2026 (Schneider, 2021). Another recommendation is to require pre-service social studies teachers to take half or most coursework focused on non-white history and equitable teaching methods. **Legislative Changes** Teachers, departments, and districts all work under their respective state boards of education, so to think that the changes I mentioned earlier can effectively be implemented without legislative action is unrealistic. Therefore, I first call upon the NCSS to incorporate specific and intentional language in their learning standards that reflect the inclusion of multiple (re: three or more) non-white racial groups in every unit of American history, to make it clear to states and social studies professionals the value of normalizing People of Color throughout every stage of America’s narrative. While the NCSS is not responsible for the state standards, it is the NCSS’s standards many states use as guidance when developing their own. Therefore, such a change would be impactful. I also call on the boards of education in each state to incorporate polyracial social studies as a requirement for secondary American history courses. Should teachers be required and empowered to normalize polyracial American history, the impact on the state’s youth will profoundly reposition their racial optics to normalize others not like themselves potentially within one generation. For example, in Maryland, a bill was recently proposed that would require the local boards of education to develop a curriculum based on the new state standards, meaning the history would need to include multiple groups by the start of the 2023-2024 school year, to include African Americans, Indigenous, Asian American, Pacific Islanders and Hispanic Americans (Wilson, 2021). Additionally, California’s Governor Newsom (2021) signed a bill requiring all public high school students to take an ethnic studies course to graduate. Further, I recommend legislation that subsidizes teacher education bachelor’s, master’s, and doctoral programs specifically for People of Color. With the current low percentages of Teachers of Color in the field due to systematic oppression, this would help address the increasing population of POC in the United States (Geiger, 2018), coupled with the data that shows Students of Color thrive with teachers that share their race (Motamedi, 2019). It would also normalize Teachers of Color for white students, which only stands to improve the racial optics of America’s future adults. **Future Research** I also offer the field my recommendations for future research and strongly encourage social studies experts to repeat this study examining secondary social studies teachers’ racial optics and its effects on their curricular decisions. My suggested demographics with which to repeat the study are specifically on Teachers of Color, teachers in varying regions of the United States (including the Northwest, West, and Southwest, as well as a comparison of mountain/urban/suburban/rural areas), and also a study that accounts for generational variations to see if there are stark differences between age groupings. I also encourage field experts to continue to evaluate racial lens scales and potentially develop a scientifically-tested racial optics lens assessment. A further refinement, critique, and testing of the existing scales will help all people – not just social studies educators – to critically examine their racial optics and open the door to their ability to empathize with people outside of their race. **Conclusion** Ultimately, white social studies is what American history education has been since its establishment in the 18th century. Keeping in mind that social studies education was always purposed to develop and educate the future leaders and participants of America’s democracy, the tradition of centering white America in the classroom has translated into a normalization of whiteness that is now considered to be breaking down the very democracy it intended to strengthen; thus, radical change is necessary. While *multiculturalism* has misleadingly become synonymous with racial diversity, it is the language used in many learning standards. Social studies must be thrust into a 21st and even 22nd-century mentality of polyracial education, intentionally incorporating many racial groups into the narrative to counteract the white normalization that has prevailed for the last 400+ years of American history. The U.S. Census Bureau projects that as early as 2044, white people will no longer be the collective majority of the United States population, but rather, the majority of the population will be comprised of People of Color (U.S. Census Bureau, 2017). The ‘minority white’ (Frey, 2018), which will be seen within the next generation, will undoubtedly shift America’s narrative dramatically and profoundly. It is the responsibility of the social studies field to prepare students for that unprecedented shift and begin to change the mentality of all residents to accept a country in which *whiteness* does not equal *standard*. If white social studies is the problem, polyracial social studies is the solution. Should America want to be a nation of productive and peaceful people, it would make sense that it needs to teach its citizens to be such. Whether human nature and notions of societal good are intrinsic or learned (Urban & Wagoner, 2014), due to the wide variety of cultural variations in the United States, a uniform standard must be taught so that a common baseline is established by which people can grow together. The existing baseline of the Constitution offers ideologies but lacks methodology. However, it is what we have in common and, therefore, the single unifying factor that Americans of all genders, colors, creeds, classes, and identities can use. This work is far from simple – it is analytical, it is critical, it is intentional, and it is systematic. However, public education is the single common denominator of up-and-coming leaders and participants in American society, and social studies is the most appropriate field for this learning. Therefore, this work essentially needs to be accomplished in the social studies classroom. Moreover, its widespread accessibility to the public and students of all racial, socio-economic backgrounds, and geographic locations makes it the ideal medium to create impactful and dynamic progress and shift America from a society of racism to an equitable polyracial community. (1999). *The mis-education of the Negro*. 12th Media Services. Original work published 1933. Worrell, F.C., Mendoza-Denton, R., Wang, A. (2017). (n.d.). *The color line*. https://www.zinnedproject.org/materials/color-line-colonial-laws Zinn, H. (1980). *A people’s history of the United States*. Harper & Row. Zinn, H. (2008). *Zinn Education Project*. https://www.howardzinn.org/related-projects/zinn-education-project/ Appendix A The Eight White Identities Table 1.1 The 8 White Identities By Barror Hesse There is a regime of whiteness, and there are action-oriented white identities. People who identify with whiteness are one of these. It’s about time we build an ethnography of whiteness, since white people have been the ones writing about and governing Others. 1. **White Supremacist** - Believes in a white society that preserves, names, and valued white superiority 2. **White Nationalist** - Wouldn’t challenge a white supremacist; desires non-whiteness because it’s not as pleasurable; seeks to consume, to consume and appropriate non-whiteness; fascination with culture (ex: consuming Black culture to alleviate the burden of Blackness) 3. **White Privilege** - May critique supremacy, but a deep investment in questions of fairness and equality; a deep affection of whiteness and like white rule; sworn goal of ‘diversity’ 4. **White Benefactor** - Sympathetic to a set of issues but only privately; won’t speak/act in support of pursuing racial justice through whiteness in public (some POC are in this category as well) 5. **White Confessional** - Acknowledges the reality of whiteness takes place, but as a way of being accountable to POC after; seek validation from POC 6. **White Traitor** - Take on board critiques of whiteness and invest in exposing/marking the white traitor; refuses to be complicit with white supremacy; speaking but not acting 7. **White Traitor** - Refuses complicity; names what’s going on; intention is to subvert white authority and tell the truth at whatever cost; need them to dismantle institutions 8. **White Abolitionist** - Changing institutions, dismantling whiteness, and not allowing whiteness to reassert itself ## Appendix B ### Spectrum: From Non-Racist to Anti-Racist Advocate (Examples adapted from [Marlon James video](#) & Dr. Gordon Hodson’s article “Being Anti-Racist, Not Non-Racist” *Psychology Today*, 1/20/16) | Passive Non-Racist is: | Active Non-Racist is: | Ally behavior/action is: | Anti-Racist Advocacy is: | |------------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | **Individual:** | | | | | Being friendly & courteous to people of different backgrounds | Not participating in racist or derogatory remarks/humor in person or online | Expressing your surprise or disagreement; asking speaker for clarification; listening without interrupting when someone says to you “That’s racist” or “white people…” | Participating in talks/rallies/protests against injustice & state-sponsored police brutality | | **Communal:** | | | | | Valuing diversity in your community | Extending your home to people of different backgrounds | Living in a neighborhood with people who look different from you; being aware of the imbalance of power and pointing out racism & injustice; | Working to secure equity in your spheres of influence (home, workplace, schools, place of worship, city, etc.); Advocating for equitable distribution of power, resources & opportunities | | **Educational:** | | | | | Building awareness by educating self: reading & learning about racism, white privilege & power | Observing how privilege & racism are in action in your own life and community; talking to family about respecting differences | Evaluating how you contribute to systems of oppression and working to change that in your circles: where do you get your news? How do you include people who don’t look like you in your decision-making? Starting early in education by providing books on diversity to schools and supporting teachers who have culturally-responsive classrooms | Giving public attention to issues of inequity & injustice; advocating that kids in schools/activities are treated justly & have equitable opportunities for success; attending school board meetings & serving on community advisory boards | | **Systemic:** | | | | | Knowing who in power works toward racial justice | Signing online petitions or sharing with others information on Equity track record of companies, candidates & representatives | Building trust & relationships with others who can affect change on larger scale; challenging systemic racism when you experience unearned privileges | Campaigning for candidates who work for racial justice; using your spheres of influence to connect with larger groups & institutions to dismantle racism | Appendix C Racism Scale Racism Scale: Where do you fall? Feelings of White Guilt can lead to | Terrorism | Overt racism | Subconscious racism | Indifference | Defensive | Justification | Denial | |-----------|--------------|---------------------|--------------|-----------|---------------|-------| | I would/ have killed a black person simply for being black | Whites are the superior race | Blacks are naturally more violent less intelligent | I’m not racist but… | How am I privileged if I am poor? | If I just don’t talk about ‘ghettos’ | Racism no longer exists | | I justify fear upon black people “on purpose” | Blacks should be sent back to Africa | The way things are is God’s will | Things that has nothing to do with me | It’s just a joke | What about reverse racism? | Slavery ended 200 years ago | | I inflict fear upon black people “on a joke” | Whites are under attack/ will be murdered soon | That is their problem, not mine | But black on black crime! | If I had they had just… | I have black friends | | | and compound many of these behaviors | | White Savior | “Woke” justification | Performative Ally | Awareness | Allyship | Abolitionist | |--------------|----------------------|------------------|-----------|---------|-------------| | Whites were slaves too | There is only 1 Race, the human race. | I will help you, if I liked | Being involved in this will help my reputation | Systemic racism is very real and needs to be ended | I will make space for POC | | There are plenty of successful POC; they just need to work harder | There is no need for POC to have their own self-policing; we are all one | Love conquers all. | If they want our help they should be nice/respectful | Yes, my life as a poor white person has been hard but I live just as if I were poor AND a POC | I will put my safety, health & freedom on the line to fight for POC. I will let them know and just try to be at their side. | *Note: It is common for many people to move back and forth along the scale regularly, especially the middle parts ** The term “blacks” is used to be more familiar to anyone of those mentalities ***“Blacks” can be substituted with people of any nationality/ethnicity other than european white From “Racism Scale,” by Demnowicz, 2017. The project has been approved for the procedures and subjects described in the protocol. This protocol must be reviewed for renewal on a yearly basis for as long as the research remains active. I created it myself I created it with my department/colleagues This study’s purpose is to examine how teacher’s learned about race and how their experience may or may not have impacted their views of race and curricular choices in their classrooms. As such, it is vital for the integrity of the study that all participants answer honestly and candidly, remembering that their responses will be written under a pseudonym and the results will inform teachers, teacher educators and policy makers.
