Close Appendix F Informed Consent Informed Consent Please read this informed consent carefully before deciding to participate in the study. Consent Form Key Information: - Participate in 3 60-120 minute interviews about experiences and understanding of teaching social studies - Interviews will be conducted in-person or via Zoom, depending on location, level of COVID protocols - Take 1 questionnaire and 1 survey regarding experiences in learning/teaching social studies - Respond to a submit journal prompts given by research - No information collected that will connect identity with responses Purpose of the research study: The purpose of the study is to gain understanding of the participant’s experiences in teaching social studies. The Researcher specifically is examining how the participant’s racial lens and how the participant see the world in relation to the participant’s race may impact the curricular choices the participant make in the classroom. The researcher will investigate the participant’s interpretations, findings, opinions, and conclusions in regards to race, racism, bias, privilege and how the participant understands it to affect the participant’s decision-making process. The researcher is seeking to understand not only the participant’s experiences in teaching social studies, but also the participant’s implementation of race within the participant’s curriculum. What the participant will do in the study: After applying to be a participant for this study, the participant will be sent a selection Process survey via Quatrics XM that will narrow down participant selection for the researcher. The participant has been selected based on technological availability, personal location, willingness to answer honestly and candidly, and the participant’s availability to interview. After obtaining consent and presenting the protocols and expectations of the study, the participant and researcher will schedule dates for interviews. If an in-person interview is not possible (due to location or COVID), or if the participant prefers, a Zoom interview will take place. Once the interviews are scheduled, the Principle Investigator will email the participant a Quatrics XM link to a questionnaire regarding the participant’s understandings and experiences with teaching. The participant is free to skip any questions that make the participant uncomfortable, but please try to answer as completely as possible for the ones the participant is comfortable with. The 60-120 minute recorded interviews will follow a semi-structured interview protocol. After interviews are completed, coding and data analysis will occur. The researcher will assign the participant, or the participant may choose, a pseudonym to ensure the participant’s anonymity. After all the data has been analyzed, the researcher will write up the final report. **Time required:** The study will require a minimum of 10 hours of the participant’s time over the course of the fall 2021 semester. The questionnaire and survey are estimated to take up to 30 minutes each. Each of the three interviews will take no longer than 120 minutes. Journal prompts will take approximately 20 minutes each to respond to, and other work for the study such as submitting curriculum will take the remaining time. **Risks:** This study is considered to be minimal risk to the participants. If at any time the participant become uncomfortable disclosing information, the participant is encouraged to notify the researcher and request the study to stop, which it will. Because the nature of the study is to learn more about the participant’s interpretations and experiences with race and the curriculum, the extent of detail is to the participant’s discretion. **Benefits:** A direct benefit to the participant upon completion of the data collection process is a $200 Amazon gift card. This gift card will be emailed to the participant upon completion of the data collection. The study may potentially be shared in the field of education, in particular, social studies education, in order to gain greater understanding of teachers’ experiences. **Confidentiality:** Participants will be assigned a pseudonym in order to maintain their anonymity and their location will be generalized; coded data will be used and master list of study codes will be kept separately from the data to ensure participant anonymity. Electronic data gathered will be stored on a password-accessible file, will be coded, with a master list kept separately, and will be collected under a secured network. Hardcopy data will be kept in a locked office and will be coded, with the master list secured and kept confidential. For heightened security, the researcher will utilize UA Box in order to ensure a secure, cloud-based system of storage. All data are encrypted. **Data linked with identifying information:** The information that the participant gives in the study will be handled confidentially. The participant’s information will be assigned a code number. The list connecting the participant’s name to this code will be kept in a locked file. When the study is completed, and the data have been analyzed, this list will be destroyed. The participant’s name will not be used in any report. Recordings of interviews will be destroyed after the study is completed. Data not linked to identifying information: The information that the participant gives in the study will be handled confidentially. The participant’s name and other information that could be used to identify the participant will not be collected or linked to the data. Confidentiality cannot be guaranteed: Because of the nature recordings via Zoom, the researcher cannot guarantee the participant’s data will be confidential and it may be possible that others will know what the participant has reported. The researcher is not able to ensure confidentiality because she cannot be present on the participant’s end to be sure there is no one else present in the room or within listening distance. The researcher encourages the use of earphones, and will herself utilize earphones to heighten her ability to ensure confidentiality on her end. Voluntary participation: The participant’s participation in the study is completely voluntary. Right to withdraw from the study: The participant has the right to withdraw from the study at any time without penalty. If the participant chooses to withdraw from the study, the participant’s recordings and data will be destroyed. How to withdraw from the study: If the participant choose to withdraw from the study, the researcher will require written notification (email is acceptable) or the participant may verbally request the researcher to stop the interview. There is no penalty for withdrawing, though compensation is reserved until the data collection is complete. Compensation/Reimbursement: Compensation is being offered upon the completion of the data gathering. The compensation is a $200 Amazon gift card emailed to an account of the participant’s choosing. Using data beyond this study: The researcher would like to make the information collected in this study available to other researchers after the study is completed. The participant’s information will be stored, used and shared for future research studies, including but not limited to continued research on racial lens and decision-making in social studies or other content fields. Once the research is completed and written up, the researcher may use the study in relation to future studies, still utilizing all the anonymity that was utilized in this current study. The researcher does not need to inform and obtain consent from participants when referring or using this study in future studies or publications. Additionally, researchers of future studies will not ask the participant’s permission for each new study or publication. However, the information the participant provide will be combined with the information provided by others to create a large data set. The participant’s name and other information that could potentially identify the participant will not be connected to the information shared with other researchers nor will they attempt to identify the participant. If the participant have questions about the study or need to report a study related issue please contact, contact: Name of Principal Investigator: Julie Johnson Title: Doctoral Candidate Department Name: Curriculum and Instruction Telephone: 847 309-8884 Email address: email@example.com Faculty Advisor’s Name: Cory Callahan Department Name: Curriculum and Instruction Telephone: 205-348-9272 Email address: firstname.lastname@example.org If the participant have questions about the participant’s rights as a participant in a research study, would like to make suggestions or file complaints and concerns about the research study, please contact: Ms. Tanta Myles, the University of Alabama Research Compliance Officer at (205)-348-8461 or toll-free at 1-877-820-3066. The participant may also ask questions, make suggestions, or file complaints and concerns through the IRB Outreach Website at http://ovpred.ua.edu/research-compliance/prco/. Sincerely, Julie Johnson Doctoral Candidate The University of Alabama firstname.lastname@example.org Appendix I First Interview Protocol Background and Personal Social Studies Education 1. Please describe what learning social studies was like for you in middle school (6-8)? 2. How did social studies impact you at that age? What do you remember standing out? 3. Please describe what learning social studies was like for you in high school. How did you feel about your classes? Your teachers? 4. What impact did social studies have on you then? 5. What was the nature of your learning about various racial groups? 6. Do you feel your teachers spent more time, less time, or about the same describing historical events as they pertained to white people versus how they pertained to People of Color? a. Please describe if People of Color were included when you learned about traditionally non-racial topics like World War 1 or The Great Depression. b. Please explain if feel your own race was represented enough when you learned social studies in 6-12 grade? 7. When you took history courses in college, did you find the experience and learning content in regard to race was very similar, very different, or pretty much the same as it was in 6-12? Please describe. 8. Did you get a sense that any of your teachers had any of their own racial bias? Please describe. 9. Did any of your social studies educators present a particular racial view when teaching American history? Did it seem like you learned more about one race than another? Appendix J Second Interview Protocol Racial Lens and World View Personal Experiences with Race 1. Please describe your understanding of your own race and how you view it in society/the world. 2. What are some common stereotypes you have heard regarding your race? Do you find these stereotypes to be largely true or untrue? Do they apply to you? 3. Describe any experiences with discrimination against you because of your race. 4. What is your opinion on how people of your race view those outside your race? 5. Describe your __________ relationships with people outside your race. (Personal, professional, Interactions with strangers) 6. Generally speaking, how do you regard your own views on peoples of other races? How would you describe the way you see peoples outside your race? Opinions on Dominant vs. Non-Dominant Culture 7. The past few years within the U.S. has revealed a racial polarization some didn’t realize existed before. What have been your reactions to the racial strife that has occurred in the last 5 years? 8. There has been an increased call for people of the dominant white race to acknowledge the oppression inflicted upon People of Color and non-dominant cultures. Do you believe these calls are appropriate? 9. Please respond to the following words and phrases with any thoughts or knowledge you have on them: a. White fragility b. White privilege c. Critical race theory d. Confederate monuments e. All lives matter Journal Prompts 10. Please discuss with me your experience with the documents from the journal prompts. a. Race Forward b. Invisible Knapsack c. Race IAT d. Racial Lens Scales 11. The initial two and concluding two journal prompts are identical. I’d like to compare and contrast them. a. In regard to journal questions 1 & 11 (*To what extent do you believe you include varying races in your curriculum? Please discuss how and why you choose to include race within your lessons*), please describe any variation you have experienced between the two responses you recorded. b. In regard to journal questions 2 & 12 (*Please describe how you view yourself in regard to race and racism. Do you see your own racial identity as oppressed, oppressive, or neutral? Do you try to be colorblind? Would you describe yourself as not racist, antiracist, or having bias toward certain races? How comfortable are your interactions with people not within your race? How frequently do they occur?*) please describe any variation you have experienced between the two responses you recorded. 12. What do you believe contributed to the variations you’ve observed in your answers? a. at the beginning of the journaling versus at the end? 13. Have you learned anything else about yourself throughout the course of this exercise? Appendix K Project Implicit Race IAT Samples Race (‘Black’ ‘White’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to distinguish faces of European and African origin. It indicates that most Americans have an automatic preference for white over black. Gender – Career. This IAT often reveals a relative link between family and females and between Sexuality (‘Gay’ ‘Straight’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to distinguish words and symbols representing gay and straight people. It often reveals an automatic preference for straight relative to gay people. Disability (‘Disabled’ ‘Abled’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to recognize symbols representing able-bodied and disabled individuals. Age (‘Young’ ‘Old’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to distinguish old from young faces. This test often indicates that Americans have automatic preference for young over old. Religion (‘Religious’ IAT). This IAT requires some familiarity with religious terms from various world religions. Asian American (‘Asian’ ‘European American’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to recognize White and Asian American faces, and images of places that are either American or Foreign in origin. Skin-tone (‘Light Skin’ ‘Dark Skin’ IAT). This IAT requires the ability to recognize light and dark-skinned faces. It often reveals an automatic preference for light-skin relative to dark-skin. Gender – Science. This IAT often reveals a relative link between liberal arts and females and between Appendix L Third Interview Protocol Interview 3a Curricular Decision-Making 1. Please describe your process when you sit down to plan a unit. 2. I’m going to mention a few generic units or topics, and I’d like to you to give me a yes or no if you think of examples of non-white people/topics/resources that you have included in your curriculum and teaching. a. 13 Colonies b. American Revolution c. The Industrial Age/Turn of the 20th Century d. Progressivism & Imperialism e. WWI f. The Great Depression g. The Cold War h. Modern America (post 1990s) 3. When planning a unit or lesson, to what extent do you consider how race will be incorporated into it, if at all? 4. Which particular race or races do you tend to gravitate towards when using contextual examples in history? Please describe. 5. There are six categories of race the U.S Census Bureau uses to classify people in the U.S. – Asian, Black, Indigenous/Alaskan Native, Latine, Pacific Islander/Native Hawaiian, and white. Do you feel various races are equally represented in your lessons? Explain. Do you think they should be? Explain? 6. How might having racial representation in their learning affect your students’ comprehension and interest in the content? 7. I’m going to give you a series of phrases, and I’d like to know your first response/reaction to them – even if you are unaware of their meaning, it’s ok to say that. a. Whitewash b. Racial Consciousness c. Gatekeeping d. Antiracism e. White social studies 8. How does reflecting on whiteness in social studies impact your future curricular decisions, if at all? 9. When planning your units, what aspects do you most take into account when deciding what you should teach and what should be omitted? 10. What hinderances/roadblocks exist in your situation that may prevent you from teaching what or how you want, if any? 11. Social studies by nature is a subject wherein students are able to explore their positions on topics pertaining to their everyday lives. To what degree, if at all, do you disclose your political, personal, and social ideology to your students? Explain. 1. What were your first encounters with the term *antiracist*? 2. Please describe, if you can, the difference between antiracist and not racist. Or if there isn’t any, you can say that too. 3. To your recollection, did any of your educators ever demonstrate antiracist curricula, lessons, or thinking, even if it wasn’t called that at the time? Please describe how. 4. Did you receive any training or information in college or your teacher education program about antiracism? 5. Have you read books, studies, listened to podcasts, or had personal experiences with antiracism as a subject? Please describe. Did these impact you? How? 6. Have you ever received professional development on antiracism or racism awareness? Please explain. 7. Are you familiar with *The 1619 Project* or the *1776 Commission*? What are your impressions of these? 8. Have you intentionally included any antiracist curriculum in your lessons? a. If so, please describe. b. If not, do you feel professionally prepared to implement antiracist curriculum if you were asked to do so in the coming weeks? 9. Do you believe antiracism should be incorporated in social studies curriculum at a district, state, or national level if at all? Why or why not? 10. What were your first encounters with the term *anti-racist*? 11. Please describe if you can the difference between *anti-racist* and *not racist*. 12. To your recollection, did any of your educators ever demonstrate anti-racist curriculum, lessons, or thinking? 13. Did you receive any training or information in college and/or your teacher education program regarding anti-racism? 14. Have you read books, studies, or had individual experiences with anti-racism as a subject? Please describe. 15. Have you ever been given any professional development on anti-racism or racism-awareness? Please explain. 16. Have you intentionally included any anti-racist curriculum in your lessons? a. If so, please describe. b. If not, do you feel professionally prepared to implement anti-racist curriculum in your curriculum? Appendix M Journal Prompt Samples Initial Prompts 1. To what extent do you believe you include varying races in your curriculum? Please discuss how and why you choose to include race within your lessons. 2. Please describe how you view yourself in regard to race and racism. Do you see your own racial identity as being oppressed, oppressive, or neutral? Do you try to be colorblind? Would you describe yourself as not-racist, anti-racist, or having bias towards certain races? When it comes to people not within your race, how comfortable are your interactions? How frequent are they? Race Forward Prior to being given the document: 3. Describe your knowledge of race in regard to the generation of students you teach. What do you know about the students in general and their opinions/experiences/thoughts on race and racism? After reading the document 4. Please describe any new information you discovered from the Race Forward Information. Invisible Knapsack Prior to being given the document: 5. What is your experience with the term “white privilege”? What do you know about it? What thoughts come to mind? What are your opinions about the term privilege? After reading the document 6. Please describe any new information you discovered or emotional responses you had from the McIntosh essay. Race IAT Prior to being given the website link 7. Remembering that your responses are anonymous, please describe if you feel you have any racial bias or racist ideals. After taking the Race IAT 8. Please describe the outcome/results you received from the Race IAT. What is your reaction? Do you agree with the results? **Racial Lens Scales** 9. What is your response to reviewing these scales? Knowing that these scales have not been scientifically validated, do you believe people can use these scales to measure themselves? 10. When considering where you would plot yourself on these scales, what is your emotional reaction to where you fall? Are you surprised? **Concluding Prompts** 11. To what extent do you believe you include varying races in your curriculum? Please discuss how and why you choose to include race within your lessons. 12. Please describe how you view yourself in regard to race and racism. Do you see your own racial identity as being oppressed, oppressive, or neutral? Do you try to be colorblind? Would you describe yourself as not-racist, anti-racist, or having bias towards certain races? When it comes to people not within your race, how comfortable are your interactions? How frequent are they? 1. I can if I wish arrange to be in the company of people of my race most of the time. 2. If I should need to move, I can be pretty sure of renting or purchasing housing in an area which I can afford and in which I want to live. 3. I can be pretty sure that my neighbors in such a location will be neutral or pleasant to me. 4. I can go shopping alone most of the time, pretty well assured that I will not be followed or harassed. 5. I can turn on the television or open to the front page of the paper and see people of my race widely represented. 6. When I am told about our national heritage or about “civilization,” I am shown that people of my color made it what it is. 7. I can be sure that my children will be given curricular materials that testify to the existence of their race. 8. If I want to, I can be pretty sure of finding a publisher for this piece on white privilege. 9. I can go into a music shop and count on finding the music of my race represented, into a supermarket and find the staple foods which fit with my cultural traditions, into a hairdresser’s shop and find someone who can cut my hair. 10. Whether I use checks, credit cards, or cash, I can count on my skin color not to work against the appearance of financial reliability. 11. I can arrange to protect my children most of the time from people who might not like them. 12. I can wear, or dress in secondhand clothes, or not answer letters, without having people attribute these choices to the bad morals, the poverty, or the illiteracy of my race. 13. I can speak in public to a powerful male group without putting my race on trial. 14. I can do well in a challenging situation without being called a credit to my race. 15. I am never asked to speak for all the people of my racial group. 16. I can remain oblivious of the language and customs of persons of color who constitute the world’s majority without feeling in my culture any penalty for such oblivion. 17. I can criticize our government and talk about how much I fear its policies and behavior without being seen as a cultural outsider. 18. I can be pretty sure that if I ask to talk to “the person in charge,” I will be facing a person of my race. 19. If a traffic cop pulls me over or if the IRS audits my tax return, I can be sure I haven’t been singled out because of my race. 20. I can easily buy posters, postcards, picture books, greeting cards, dolls, toys, and children’s magazines featuring people of my race. 21. I can go home from most meetings of organizations I belong to feeling somewhat tied in, rather than isolated, out-of-place, outnumbered, unheard, held at a distance, or feared. 22. I can take a job with an affirmative action employer without having co-workers on the job suspect that I got it because of race. 23. I can choose public accommodation without fearing that people of my race cannot get in or will be mistreated in the places I have chosen. 24. I can be sure that if I need legal or medical help, my race will not work against me. 25. If my day, week, or year is going badly, I need not ask of each negative episode or situation whether it has racial overtones. 26. I can choose blemish cover or bandages in “flesh” color and have them more or less match my skin. ISSUE-SPECIFIC TIPS: CHANGING DEMOGRAPHICS & MILLENNIALS • Familiarize yourself with changing racial and ethnic demographics in the geographic areas you’re covering, and report with them in mind. • By 2044, the United States is projected to be a plurality nation, as no race or ethnic group is projected to have greater than a 50 percent share of the nation’s total. • Beware of using terms like “majority-minority” as they do not give a nuanced or accurate picture of the demographic changes occurring in a particular community. Instead describe the change, with figures and a sense of how things have changed over time and why. • The “Millennial Generation” (born 1981-1993) is the largest, most racially and ethnically diverse generation the U.S. has ever known. • The fastest growing racial groups are “Two or More Races,” (expected to triple in size by 2060), Asian population, and Hispanic population (expected to comprise 29% of the United States population by 2060). • More than half the growth in total population of the U.S. between 2000 and 2010 was due to the increase in the Hispanic population. (continued on following page) • There were 11.2 million unauthorized immigrants in the U.S. as of 2012. • In 2014, 13.3% of the U.S. population was foreign-born, with a rate of growth expected to account for an increasing share of the total population, reaching 19 percent in 2060. • The federal government is considering allowing those of Middle Eastern and North African descent (MENA) to identify as such on 2020 Census (previously classified as White by default). ONLINE RESOURCES See Race Forward’s reports on Millennials http://raceforward.org/millennials SOURCES https://www.census.gov/content/dam/Census/library/publications/2015/demo/p25-1143.pdf http://www.census.gov/prod/cen2010/briefs/c2010br-04.pdf http://www.pewresearch.org/key-data-points/immigration/ Four Dimensions of Racism, from Race Forward Trainings: **INDIVIDUAL-LEVEL RACISM** | INTERNALIZED RACISM | INTERPERSONAL RACISM | |---------------------|-----------------------| | The private racial beliefs held by individuals. | How we act upon our racial beliefs when we interact with others. | | EXAMPLE: Prejudice, internalized oppression and internalized privilege | EXAMPLE: Bias, bigotry, hate speech and violence | **SYSTEMIC-LEVEL RACISM** | INSTITUTIONAL RACISM | STRUCTURAL RACISM | |----------------------|--------------------| | Racial inequities within institutions and systems of power. | Racial bias across institutions and society. | | EXAMPLE: School systems that provide unequal opportunities for people of different races | EXAMPLE: The racial wealth gap reflects the cumulative effects of racial inequities. | **ONLINE RESOURCES** See Race Forward’s Racial Equity Impact Assessment Toolkit http://raceforward.org/racial-equity-impact-assessment-toolkit Appendix O Criteria for Curriculum Analysis Criteria: - Must be actual curricular documents you utilize - Minimum 1 document from each unit - PDF or Microsoft Word files only - Please include your pseudonym, date, and the unit somewhere on the document Types of documents to submit: Preferred Acceptable Unit overview Rubrics Lesson plan Readings that are significant Assignment Sheet Media resources Assessments Activity sheet Webquests Suggested Time Frames: Pre-colonial Americas WWI, WWII Colonization and American Revolution The Great Depression/New Deal 1800s and Expansion The American Dream (1950s) Turn of the Century Recent history (1980-today) It is ideal you select the best representation of your curricular resources that reflect multi-cultural perspectives, critical thinking, diversity, and equity. However, if your documents do not contain these things, that’s ok! Just send your best samples most representative of your chosen curriculum. ## Appendix P ### Rubric for Curriculum Analysis | Criteria | Beginning – 1 | Emerging – 2 | Progressing – 3 | Excelling - 4 | |--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Diversity | Race is not a factor or topic in this document | 1 racial group is represented in the document | 2 racial groups are represented in the document | 3 or more racial groups are represented in the document | | Equity | white people are only present in the document | BIPOC are present in the document | BIPOC are present and discussion of their oppression is present | BIPOC are highlighted as a focus in the document, giving specific indication of how they have been oppressed | | Critical Lens| The document does not require critical thinking | The document asks students to think critically but not about varying racial populations | The document alludes to critical examination of various perspectives of different populations | The document requires that students critically consider the multiple perspectives of various populations | | Impact | The document does not give evidence of one or two of the following: impactfulness, relevance, or effectiveness | The document somewhat reflects impactful, relevant, and effective approaches toward racial diversity | The document reflects a somewhat impactful, relevant and effective approach towards racial diversity | The document reflects highly impactful, relevant, and effective approach towards racial diversity | 6-12 SS Teacher Survey Start of Block: Informed Consent Q1 Informed Consent for Survey Please read this consent form carefully before you decide to participate in the survey. Key Information: • Total time commitment: about 10 minutes • Participate in a survey to inform the field about how teachers include race in their content and pedagogy • Take 1 survey per person • No information collected will connect the identity with the responses • Participation is voluntary Purpose of the research study: The purpose of the study is to gain understanding of the participant’s experiences in teaching social studies. Specifically, the researcher is examining how the participant’s racial lens and how the participant see the world in relation to the participant’s race may impact the curricular choices the participant make in the classroom. The researcher is seeking to understand not only the participant’s experiences in teaching social studies, but also the participant’s implementation of race within the participant’s curriculum. What you will do in the study: The participant will complete an anonymous ~10 minute survey via Qualtrics XM based on their current teaching experience. Time required: The survey will require about 10 minutes of your time. Risks: There are no anticipated in this study. Benefits: There are no direct benefits to you for participating in this research study. The study may inform the field to understand how social studies teachers perceive and teach race, and how they may or may not incorporate it into their curricula. Confidentiality: The information that you give in the study will be handled confidentially. Your name and other information that could be used to identify you will not be collected or linked to the data. Email addresses collected are only for clarification for the researcher and will not be published or shared. Voluntary Participation: Your participation in the study is completely voluntary. Right to withdraw from the study: You have the right to withdraw from the study at any time without penalty. How to withdraw from the study: To withdraw from the study, you may stop the survey at any time. There is no penalty for withdrawing. Compensation/Reimbursement: You will receive no payment for participating in the study. Using data beyond this study: The researcher would like to make the information collected in this study available to other researchers after the study is completed. The participant’s information will be stored, used and shared for future research studies, including but not limited to continued research on racial lens and decision-making in social studies or other content fields. Once the research is completed and written up, the researcher may use the study in relation to future studies, still utilizing all the anonymity that was utilized in this current study. The researcher does not need to inform and obtain consent from participants when referring or using this study in future studies or publications. Additionally, researchers of future studies will not ask the participant’s permission for each new study or publication. However, the information the participant provide will be combined with the information provided by others to create a large data set. The participant’s name and other information that could potentially identify the participant will not be connected to the information shared with other researchers nor will they attempt to identify the participant. If you have any questions about the study or need to report a study-related issue, please contact: Name of Principal Investigator: Julie Muhlenfeld-Johnson, NBCT If you have questions about your rights as a participant in a research study, would like to make suggestions or file complaints and concerns about the research study, please contact: Ms. Tanta Myles, the University of Alabama Research Compliance Officer at (205)-348-8461 or toll-free at 1-877-820-3066. You may also ask questions, make suggestions, or file complaints and concerns through the IRB Outreach Website at http://ovpred.ua.edu/research-compliance/prco/. You may email the Office for Research Compliance at email@example.com. Briefly explain your answer. - [ ] Yes _______________________________________ - [ ] No _______________________________________ End of Block: Pedagogy Appendix R Pathways of Teacher Certification in the United States Graduate/TEP/NBCT: Graduate school for secondary educators usually indicates a master's degree in education. I have listed "MA" or "MAT" to represent if the participant identified their graduate degree as a master's versus a Master of Arts in teaching, which is a program in which undergraduates with a non-education degree in a content field (such as a bachelor's in Liberal Arts with a history major) will enroll in to complete their initial teaching certification in conjunction with earning a master's degree. For example, the traditional pathway for educators has been to earn a teaching certificate in the bachelor's program with a major in secondary social studies. They then go on to a master's program to further advance their knowledge in education while simultaneously advancing up the teacher's pay scale, which typically gives salary increases for teachers with more education in their field. Additionally, "TEP" is the common abbreviation for "teacher education program," occurring at the undergraduate or graduate level in the United States. Finally, "NBCT" is a "Nationally Board-Certified Teacher," an additional certification beyond the standard teaching certificate. Becoming an NBCT is rigorous and usually includes several criteria to show applicants as reflective and effective educators. One of these criteria includes proving that the candidate can address, accommodate, and incorporate diversity in their classroom. I hold a MAT (achieved in 2007) and National Board Certification (since 2010). ## Appendix S ### Completed Curriculum Profiles and Rubrics for All Participants | Criteria | Unsatatisfactory: 0 | Under |-----------------|---------|-----------|----------|-----------| | 0 – 4 pts. | This curriculum appears to whitewash history. This curriculum presents American history from a primarily white perspective, as it has traditionally been taught. It doesn’t allow sufficient availability to and teaching of the value and contributions People of Color have made to the country. There is little intentionality – if any – to include People of Color in America’s history beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included. | 10 – 14 pts. This curriculum includes People of Color alongside the traditionally taught white narrative. However the white narrative remains the featured story, thereby marginalizing People of Color. Further intentionality needs to be contributed to offer equity to People of Color in America’s history. | 15 – 19 pts. This curriculum includes People of Color to a similar extent as white people in the teaching of American history. This curriculum reflects equality in that it offers a balanced content that allows People of Color to be normalized in the telling of American history. Further intentionality is needed to truly offer an equitable perspective of the impact People of Color have had on American history. | 20 – 24 pts. This curriculum elevates People of Color above the white narrative in order to give equitable perceptions of the value BIPOC have had in the telling of America’s history. Utilizing this curriculum will guide learners to see People of Color as valuable stakeholders in the progress and success of the country and presents history from their perspective, thus positively impacting how the learner understands various racial groups. | ## Rubric for Curriculum Analysis ### Participant: CHARLES **Rubric measures level of inclusion of non-white perspectives and history in | | | Bonus points: -2 | | | Bonus points: -3 | | Unsatysfactory | Unclear | Attempted | Emerging | Excelling | |----------------|---------|-----------|----------|-----------| | 0 – 4 pts. | 5 - 9 pts. | 10 - 14 pts. | 15 - 19 pts. | 20 - 24 pts. | | This curriculum appears to whitewash history. Anyone who receives the information taught with this content will likely not learn the valuable contributions or rich history People of Color have within the United States. The curriculum – regardless of intentionality – will likely lead its students into falsely believing that the white narrative is the only one important enough to be taught beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included. | This curriculum presents American history from a primarily white perspective, as it has traditionally been taught. It doesn’t allow sufficient availability to and teaching of the value and contributions People of Color have made to the country. There is little intentionality – if any – to include People of Color in America’s history beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included. | This curriculum includes People of Color alongside the traditionally taught white narrative. However the white narrative remains the featured story, thereby marginalizing People of Color. Further intentionality needs to be contributed to offer equity to People of Color in America’s history. | This curriculum includes People of Color to a similar extent as white people in the teaching of American history. This curriculum reflects equality in that it offers a balanced content that allows People of Color to be normalized in the telling of American history. Further intentionality is needed to truly offer an equitable perspective of the impact People of Color have had on American history. | This curriculum elevates People of Color above the white narrative in order to give equitable perceptions of the value BIPOC have had in the telling of America’s history. Utilizing this curriculum will guide learners to see People of Color as valuable stakeholders in the progress and success of the country and presents history from their perspective, thus positively impacting how the learner understands various racial groups. | ## Rubric for Curriculum Analysis Rubric measures level of inclusion of non-white perspectives and history in social studies content | Criteria | Unatisfactory 0 | Unclear 1 | Attempted 2 | Emerging 3 | Excelling 4 | |-------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-----------|-------------|------------|-------------| | **Content: Variety** | | | | | | | The variety of BIPOC present in content is limited, for example, Indigenous Hawaiians and Alaska Natives | Only white people are discussed throughout the content with or without race being an explicit factor | One non-white racial group is discussed along with white group within the content | Two or more racial groups are included in less than half of the content | Two or more racial groups are discussed | Three+ racial groups are discussed with or without white groups as a factor of the content | | **Content: Perspective** | | | | | | | BIPOC are depicted with negative connotations and cannot be trusted to be accurate | BIPOC are depicted with the depiction of BIPOC are given from a traditional standard perspective | Histories of BIPOC appear neutral, factual and present no bias towards or against racial groups | The depictions of BIPOC are given somewhat from their perspective | The depictions of BIPOC are made from their perspective | | **Content: Equity** | | | | | | | Equitable discussions and learning about the oppression of BIPOC in history highlights their previously unacknowledged contributions to human progress | The content regularly generalizes the history and doesn’t discuss people and/or white people are only present | BIPOC are present and significant examples are shown but there are few to no examples of positive contributions to history | BIPOC are included throughout but not as a main narrative | BIPOC are present. Many examples of oppression and positive contributions are learned; some focus is given | 3 & BIPOC are highlighted as a focus, giving specific examples of various types of oppression and/or positive contributions | | **Normalization:** | | | | | | | BIPOC are weaved seamlessly as the main of equivalent narrative | BIPOC are notably absent from the content | BIPOC are singled out as a special exception to the main white narrative | BIPOC are included as a main narrative | BIPOC are singled out but rather are naturally included in the main narrative | BIPOC are not singled out but rather are naturally included in the main narrative of history | | **Critical Lens:** | | | | | | | Students are guided through information and discussions that lead them to examine the content closely and consider the inclusion of gaps in the information regarding race | Does not require critical examining content through a critical racial lens | Asks students to think critically but not about varying racial populations | Students are led to examine content and consider the inclusion of gaps, but lacks in effort or efficacy | Reflects impactful, relevant, and effective approaches towards racial diversity | Requires students to critical examination of various perspectives of different populations | | **Impact on multicultural perspectives:** | | | | | | | Potential to impact students to reflect on the relevance of multicultural groups and takes an effective approach towards racial diversity | Does not offer impact, relevance or effectiveness in challenging perspectives of multicultural groups | Does not give evidence of one or two of the following: impact, relevance, or effectiveness | Somewhat impactful, relevant and effective approach towards racial diversity | Highly impactful, relevant and effective approach towards racial diversity | | **Bonus Points:** | | | | | | | Resources authored by BIPOC | | | | | | | Many resources authored by BIPOC | | | | | | **Bonus points:** -7 **Bonus points:** +5 | Unsatisfactory | Unclear | Attempted | Emerging | Excelling | |----------------|---------|-----------|----------|-----------| | 0 – 4 pts. | 5 – 9 pts. | 10 – 14 pts. | 15 – 19 pts. | 20 – 24 pts. | | This curriculum appears to whitewash history. Anyone who receives the information taught with this content will likely not learn the valuable contributions or rich history People of Color have within the United States. The curriculum – regardless of intentionality – will likely lead its students into falsely believing the white narrative is the only one important enough to be taught. | This curriculum presents American history from a primarily white perspective, as it has traditionally been taught. It doesn’t allow sufficient availability to and teaching of the value and contributions People of Color have made to the country. There is little intentionality – if any – to include People of Color in America’s history beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included. | This curriculum includes People of Color alongside the traditionally taught white narrative. However, the white narrative remains the featured story, thereby marginalizing People of Color. Further intentionality needs to be contributed to offer equity to People of Color in America’s history. | This curriculum includes People of Color to a similar extent as white people in the teaching of American history. This curriculum reflects equality in that it offers a balanced content that allows People of Color to be normalized in the telling of American history. Further intentionality is needed to truly offer an equitable perspective of the impact People of Color have had on American history. | This curriculum elevates People of Color above the white narrative in order to give equitable perceptions of the value BIPOC have had in the telling of America’s history. Utilizing this curriculum will guide learners to see People of Color as valuable stakeholders in the progress and success of the country and presents history from their perspective, thus positively impacting how the learner understands various racial groups. | | Criteria | Unsat satisfactory | Unclear | Attempted | 2 | Emerging | 3 | Excelling | 4 | narrative | BIPOC are present, giving specific examples of various types of oppression and or positive contributions | | **Critical Lens:** | | | | | | | | | | Students are guided through information consider the impact of various perspectives of various populations and identify gaps in the resources | | **Impact on multicultural perspectives:** Potential to impact students to reflect on the relevance or effectiveness in challenging perspectives of multicultural groups and takes an effective approach towards racial diversity | Does not offer impact, relevance or effectiveness in Anyone who receives the information taught with this content will likely not learn the valuable contributions or rich history People of Color have within the United States. The curriculum – regardless of intentionality – will likely lead its students into falsely believing the white narrative is the only one important enough to be taught. | This curriculum presents American history from a primarily white perspective, as it has traditionally been taught. It doesn’t allow for sufficient availability to and teaching of the value and contributions People of Color have made to the country. There is little intentionality – if any – to include People of Color in America’s history beyond the traditional topics and perspectives in which they have been included. | This curriculum includes People of Color alongside the traditionally taught white narrative. However the white narrative remains the featured story, thereby marginalizing People of Color. Further intentionality needs to be contributed to offer equity to People of Color in America’s history. | This curriculum includes People of Color to a similar extent as white people in the teaching of American history. This curriculum reflects equality in that it offers a balanced content that allows People of Color to be normalized in the telling of American history. Q18 - HOW YOU SEE RACE: Remembering that this survey is anonymous, of the options below, select which statements best describe your personal outlook on race. Teacher's Self-Identification of Their Outlook On Race - I believe white people are superior to other racial demographics - I enjoy the music, sports and/or culture of non-white people but I don't ha... - I believe being white in the United States is easier than being a Person of... - I think People of Color have had it rough but I've never participated in a ... - I am disheartened that white people have benefited over People of Color and... - I acknowledge whiteness has been oppressive and look for ways to expose it - I refuse to comply with racism and look to speak the truth to dismantle rac... - I am actively involved in changing institutional racism and dismantle white... - I am not personally close with many/any People of Color - I try not to see color - I acknowledge whiteness has been oppressive but there's not much I can do a... - I'm not racist but..... - Slavery ended a long time ago; I had nothing to do with it - Click to write Choice 14 Q19 - To the best of your ability, select the one with which you self-identify. Teachers’ Self-Identification of Racial Optics Other category - fill in the blank Other - Text I teach History factually and honestly. I hope to someday be considered an ally. I continue to grow and change as I learn more about racism and my own racist ideology. Classic liberal “content of character” type of person against racism I have racist thoughts created by my upbringing. I actively work to counter them and consider myself an ally. To say I am not racist is to deny unconscious bias, which I know I still have. I wouldn’t call myself an ally as that label has not been given to be by the BIPOC community. I try my best to be antiracist, but I wouldn’t call it an identity. I don’t know if I’ve “shown up” enough to call myself an “ally”. The work is not a central part of my day or identity, so I can’t say I’m an ally. I do believe they exist, but acknowledge that dismantling racist institutions isn’t a focus of my everyday life. I’ve read several anti-racist text, and I think I get it, but I also try to listen to black voices who critique some of the most popular anti-racist authors. And I read these dialogues, try to learn from them, think about what’s said, and then stay out of the conversation because I’m a white person. I don’t know if that makes sense. In the classroom, I choose to expose the accomplishments and failures of all races through each others’ lenses. Like my politics and religion, my personal racial beliefs are immaterial and I shield them from my students. always trying to do better each day Merit and character are how I choose to see all my students I think that all people have some form of bias but that there can also be good in the world. We are all humans and that is what needs to taught. Q20 - On the political/ideological spectrum, where do you identify yourself? Unsure (please explain) unsure (please explain) - Text Libertarian I’ve always voted Democrat, and that’s associated with being liberal. But I’m also skeptical of what the Democratic Party has really done to make our society more equitable? It’s a lot of bullshit. I honestly would have voted against anyone but Trump.. His racism, misogyny, the potential he had to destroy any of our systems that are somewhat democratic, and the Supreme Court balance were enough to make me vote against him. I would vote against him a million times if I could. I was a Liz Warren girl in the primary, and believe emphatically that we need to expand the Supreme Court to try and protect any voting rights that we have in place. These choices are too simple. I have many libral beliefs, especially in areas of personal dignity, but they are grounded in a profound admiration for the possibilities offered by the Constitution, which leads me to some more moderate views on areas like economics and personal religious freedom. I am very familiar with anti racism and do not include it in my classroom. I believe that antiracism is a misnomer as it often creates racism from what I have seen. Therefore I will not incorporate it into lessons. I lead and/or encourage discussions on the impact of racism and encourage students to fight against it So I teach post classical world history to 7th graders. I emphatically believe that one of many reasons that my curriculum matters (in terms of a survey world history course) is that ALL students needs to see themselves in history, and I try to work hard to make that happen (I probably face the same dilemma as any world history teacher in that it’s hard to feel like you do any part of the world “justice” when teaching world history). But I have fought colleagues again and again who just want to teach about Europe all year. I’ve fought colleagues who want to “take Africa out” of our curriculum time and again. Sometimes I get questioned over the importance of teaching history that’s as old as this time period. The idea that Black students will only or first see themselves in history when learning about slavery is nauseating bullshit. I enjoy teaching about a world before colonization. I’ll never feel like I do a good enough job making it truly global, but I’m always going to try to do so as much as possible. I teach Ethnic Studies, so we use specific vocabulary to discuss anthracite behavior I am teaching history through documents and not through a crafted lens Q28 - Do you ever share your own political or social ideologies/opinions in your classroom? 6-12 Social Studies Teachers Sharing Their Political/Social Ideologies in Class Other - fill in the blank 6-12 Social Studies Teachers Sharing Their Political/Social Ideologies in Class Other - Text Yes, because I believe those I teach need to know my leanings. They also know my political thought are more complicated than on political ideology. I try to be as ambiguous as possible We teach guidelines. If I ever inject opinions, my kids know that. My place is to train my kids to think for themselves! It is wrong to let students know your beliefs. It is our responsibility to teach them to become critical thinkers I play both sides of the fence. My students consistently identify me a little further left of center, though I find myself a little more right of center. I remain neutral and show understanding for both sides Political --no; social -- yes Rarely We have an obligation as professionals to maintain objectivity at all times as much as possible. I never identify my political or religious views, but my students are able to ascertain them by how I teach. For example, I teach about fairness to all - not just to a protected class, so my students can guess that I am Democrat. Only when asked by students and after clarifying that they want my personal opinions, not that of the school or education system itself Sometimes; when I do, I make it clear that it is my opinion, and provide context and reasons for my beliefs. Learners should be given the opportunity to analyze multiple sources in order to have a vast understanding of race in multiple contexts. For instance, in teaching the Enlightenment and Revolutionary era, it is important to facilitate scholars’ learning with sources from European and American Enlightenment thinkers, while also providing scholars the ability to see historical complexities as it relates to US-Native American relations, slavery and freedom as expressed by Black Enlightenment thinkers such as Phillis Wheatley and Benjamin Banneker, and the global revolutions during this period. One strategy I have used while teaching this period is exploring the role of Black and Native Americans in the revolutionary war as Patriots and Loyalists and used two Wheatley poems, “On Being Brought from Africa to America” and “His Excellency General Washington,” and Benjamin Banneker’s Almanack and Ephemeris to discuss race, nation, war, neoclassicism, constitutionalism, and foreign policy. In order to create relevance, yes Races and ethnic groups, past and present. It’s easy for me with the school I’m at, but yes every event should have a focus on how it affected all races involved. It might be difficult to include every group into every unit. Modern history can easily be very Eurocentric in nature. No Pacific Islander in constitution era. It is not true history if we don’t include all racial groupings in our teaching. We should learn about various experiences that are not our own. Students should be able to connect with lessons as much as possible. Only, If relevant to the content to get different perspective. However, it should not be forced just to be included. For example, Asians and Pacific Islanders do not need to be included in Early US Colonial history. It's a nice theory, but time restrictions limit how much each group can be covered. Treating them like a buffet line with a taste of this and that is more disrespectful than choosing to emphasize different groups in different classes. What is the probability that each box contains two marbles? What is the formula used for this type of problem? Date: 09/17/98 at 11:21:12 From: Doctor Anthony Subject: Re: Probability You can model the situation as follows: |****| |**| where the | | represent boxes and the * are marbles. In the above diagram we have 4, 0, and 2 marbles in the three boxes, respectively. Now this diagram must begin and end with a | but otherwise the two remaining |'s and six *'s can be arranged in any order, and each of these orders is considered equally likely. So we have 8 objects to arrange in every possible way, 2 of one kind being alike and 6 of a second kind being alike. This can be done in: 8! ------ = C(8,2) = 28 ways. 2!6! [ If you haven't done permutations and combinations yet you should obtain a textbook on elementary probability and read up on the topic. It is essential knowledge for this type of question. There is also some information in this archive's FAQ to get you started. http://mathforum.org/dr.math/faq/faq.comb.perm.html ] So there are 28 possible arrangements of the marbles in the 3 boxes and only one of these will have two marbles in each box, so the required probability is: 1/28 - Doctor Anthony, The Math Forum http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Search the Dr. Math Library: Ask Dr. MathTM © 1994-2013 The Math Forum | <urn:uuid:e0ed4231-db6f-4fb5-93a4-292200b59f91> | http://mathforum.org/library/drmath/view/56180.html | HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu | What The First Amendment Protects And What It Doesn T? What is the First Amendment and what does it protect? What does the 1st Amendment mean in simple terms? freedom of speechThe First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution protects the freedom of speech, religion and the press. It also protects the right to peaceful protest and to petition the government. … The meaning of the First Amendment has been the subject of continuing interpretation and dispute over the years. How does the First Amendment affect us today? Why is defamation not protected by the First Amendment? The Court ruled that the First Amendment protected critics from libel actions of public officials in significant ways. … Today, public figures as well as public officials must prove that a defamatory statement was published with knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth. What is considered hate speech? Generally, however, hate speech is any form of expression through which speakers intend to vilify, humiliate, or incite hatred against a group or a class of persons on the basis of race, religion, skin color sexual identity, gender identity, ethnicity, disability, or national origin. Why is freedom of speech limited? Those who favor the limited liberty to speech do not deny its benefits of allowing people to express their thoughts but all they desire is to protect all those rights e.g. right to life, privacy and security of a person that has been largely violated due to excessive power of speech specifically the hate speech or … What the First Amendment protects and what it does not Newsela answers? Press Freedom The Supreme Court has made clear that the First Amendment protects statements made about public officials unless they are false and intended to defame. Only “reckless disregard for the truth” is not protected. Furthermore, the media can publish information from classified documents. Does the First Amendment protect lies? In United States constitutional law, false statements of fact are an exception from protection of free speech under the First Amendment. In United States law, a false statement of fact will not be exempt from some civil or criminal penalty, if a law has imposed one. What does the First Amendment mean in your own words? First Amendment. An amendment to the United States Constitution guaranteeing the rights of free expression and action that are fundamental to democratic government. These rights include freedom of assembly, freedom of the press, freedom of religion, and freedom of speech. What is the most important amendment? How is the First Amendment used today? Is dangerous speech protected? United States,[1] the Supreme Court announced the “clear and present danger” test; that is, speech is not protected when it is used “in such circumstances and… of such a nature as to create a clear and present danger that they will bring about substantive evils that [the government] has a right to prevent.” The opinion … What does the 1st Amendment not protect? What is not protected speech? “Not all speech is protected. … The Supreme Court has called the few exceptions to the 1st Amendment “well-defined and narrowly limited.” They include obscenity, defamation, fraud, incitement, true threats and speech integral to already criminal conduct. What does the 2st Amendment mean? What are the limits to freedom of speech? What is a real life example of the First Amendment? 1st Amendment Example Involving the Establishment Clause One notable case example on the 1st Amendment is that of Everson v. Board of Education, 330 U.S. 1 (1947). A New Jersey school authorized reimbursement by school boards for transportation to and from school, including private schools. Is it illegal to lie about who you are? It’s illegal to knowingly falsify material information to authorities in the course of their duties. This class of offenses includes perjury, false identification to police, official fraud, &c. It’s illegal to lie to someone for the purpose of taking their money or stuff, that is called theft by deception. | <urn:uuid:a46ed18f-00b0-4773-9f7e-23fd58245723> | https://cardactivationinfo.com/qa/what-the-first-amendment-protects-and-what-it-doesn-t.html | mlfoundations/dclm-baseline-1.0-parquet | This paper must 1) examine and critique a definition of money found in an economics textbook, and 2) examine and critique one or two contemporary “discourses about money” in light of what we learned in class and your own research. The point of the final paper is to research a topic of your own, like a time period and a place and what you can learn about money and what that tells you about the time, place, and if possible our time and place. It should be around ten pages or so. Annotated bibliography is needed. Short Description:This course examines monetary systems as material and politically contingent phenomena, from palace economies, cowrie shells, and metallic coinages to modern money and student debt. Long Description:What is money? Orthodox theories, from Karl Marx to Econ 101, tend to see money as a veil over what is really going on, and the goal of the critique of money to look past or through money to the play of Value and Utility going on behind it. When this has been done, in Marxs words, the mystery of money will immediately disappear. In this course, we explore an alternative view according to which money can be better understood not by looking beyond it, but directly at it: what happens if we examine money as a material and politically contingent phenomenon in its own right, rather than merely as a metaphor or a sign of something besides itself? Rather than begin by assuming uncritically that the classic three functions of money (unit of account, means of exchange, and store of value) are naturally united in a single thing called money, we examine these functions separately with an eye to the tensions that arise between them, and trace a deep history of monetary systems as the outcome of a process of negotation and contestation. Topics considered include palace economies, cowrie shells, metallic coinages, the modern monetary revolution, and contemporary struggles over student debt. Learning Outcomes:1. Compare and contrast different aspects of the monetary system.2. Analyze social and political struggles over monetary systems.3. Whether you are a long-time St. Matthews resident or new to the community, the city’s new website, at www.stmatthews ky.gov, provides the resources to answer many of those questions. Launched in February, the website is easy to navigate with pull-down menus and quick links on its home page. The Government tab provides links to city officials and local, state, and federal representatives, as well to the city’s budget and council meeting minutes. Want to know how to properly park vehicles or vote in St. Matthews? A city ordinance link and a voting link are also found under the Government tab. Links under the Service tab provide information on MSD, Louisville Water, LG&E, ambulances, hospitals, fire, and police. If you have a teen who needs a driver’s license, want to know what you can put in your recycling bin, or need senior care services or a TARC bus route, the Service tab is the place to look. Getting plugged into the neighborhood is as easy as clicking on the Community tab to find area organizations and churches. Under that same tab, you can rent the St. Matthews Community Center for your parents’ 50th wedding anniversary or your daughter’s wedding reception. Area schools and shopping venues may also be found there. If you are making summer plans, check out the city’s parks and pavilion reservations or arrange to attend an event at the local library, also under the Community tab. Stay on that tab to find the local farmers’ markets and a map of St. Matthews you can use to find your way to such points of interest as Burks Cemetery, a hidden, hallowed ground where early 1800s settlers of St. Matthews are buried. For answers to occupational questions about taxes and zoning and for links to permits and license forms, look under the Business tab. Answers regarding business licensing and solicitation registration may also be found there. Quick Links at the bottom of the home page take you directly to some of the most frequently used pages such as city events, parks, and police. Next to those links, you can even check the daily weather. The “How Do I” tab features answers to popular concerns voiced by St. Matthews residents, continued on page 7 A Message From... In March, St. Matthews celebrated 65 years as an incorporated city, but its history goes back much further. In 1774, surveyor John Floyd, after whom Floyd’s Fork is named, purchased 2,000 acres that encompasses what is present day St. Matthews. Floyd is buried in the Floyd-Breckenridge Cemetery on Jamestown Court in St. Matthews. During the summer and fall of 1865, General William T. Sherman’s Union forces were stationed in St. Matthews. A few years later, German and Swiss immigrants began to arrive and farm potatoes and onions on the land, using the railroad to market their produce. The James Brown residence is one of a few early buildings remaining in St. Matthews. Built prior to 1882, the residence is now the club house for Mallard Creek Apartments. Well-known St. Matthews residents have included Bill Schroeder, the second recipient of the Jarvic 7 heart, magician Lance Burton, who gave free performances at St. Matthews Baptist Church, and actor Ned Beatty, who grew up on Grandview Avenue. In its 65 years of incorporation, the city has had only five mayors, including Mayor Richard Tonini, who took office this year. For more on the history of St. Matthews, see Samuel W. Thomas’ book, *St. Matthews – The Crossroads of Beargrass*. The book is available for purchase through the St. Matthews Beargrass Historical Society and the St. Matthews Eline Library, or at local book stores. More information regarding the city’s incorporation may be found on the city’s new website at www.stmatthewsky.gov under the Community tab. Hometown History - 1953 In his *St. Matthews: The Crossroads of Beargrass*, Samuel W. Thomas commented on this photograph: “For decades, this property had been farmed by the Seiderman family, and in later years by the Seiderman sisters. Carolyn Rudy Barth, who lived nearby, remembers that the sisters worked long and hard on their truck farm. Carolyn was sent by her mother to buy vegetables, and she recalls that the cellar rooms were full of potatoes, turnips, onions, and squash. ‘They never went to the bank, and they had drawers of money.’” See page 11 to learn what is located on this land today. Louisville Gas & Electric 502-589-1444 Fast path options are: 1-1-1 (gas) and 1-1-2 (electric). Emergency Contact Numbers Louisville Water Company 502-583-6610 To report emergencies like water main breaks or broken fire hydrants. No plastic bags for yard waste Please remember that, as a matter of law, reusable containers or biodegradable bags must be used for yard waste. Free stickers for your designated yard waste containers are available at city hall. For better identification, two or three stickers for each container are recommended. Paint disposal Your newly painted room looks great. Now what to do with the leftover paint? Dried latex paint can be disposed of with your regularly scheduled trash collection. Just remove the lid and allow the paint to dry in a safe area out of the reach of children and pets. You may also stir in cat litter, sand, shredded paper, or saw dust to help absorb the excess paint. You may recycle oil-based and lead paints through Metro Solid Waste Management’s HAZ BIN disposal program. HAZ BIN is located at 7501 Grade Lane and is open Wednesdays and Saturdays from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. Prescription drug disposal at City Hall Prescription drugs may be dropped into the drug disposal box at St. Matthews City Hall. Due to the overwhelming success of this service, we find it necessary to allow disposal Monday through Friday from 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. only. Drugs must be placed loosely in sealed plastic bags. Please remember that sharps, needles, liquids, and nonprescription drugs may NOT be deposited in the box. Use permeable pavers When deciding to install or replace concrete or asphalt features, such as sidewalks, driveways, or patios, consider using specialized asphalt or permeable pavers. These materials allow water to pass through into underlying soil, reducing runoff into gutters, streets, and waterways. In winter months these materials reduce icing by letting the water pass through, rather than pool on the surface. Fertilizer usage Don’t apply fertilizers before a rain storm as they will simply run off into the city’s waterways rather than being absorbed into your lawn or garden. Consider using organic fertilizers as they release nutrients more slowly. Apply fertilizer to the lawn at the proper time and in the proper amount by following the package directions. This can save time, effort, and money through reduced mowing and watering. Recycling is collected every other week on the same day as trash on weeks highlighted in red. Holidays marked in blue will delay recycling and/trash one day. Police Beat Maintaining excellence The St. Matthews Police Department is currently in the process of its fourth reaccreditation through the Kentucky Association of Chiefs of Police. Accreditation allows an outside agency to evaluate the standards and policies of the department to ensure that it is following the best practices for modern law enforcement. Being an accredited agency also provides the city with a reduction in the cost of its liability insurance. SMPD has been accredited for 15 years. CIT training The department is pushing forward to have all of its officers certified in Crisis Intervention Training. CIT gives officers training in alternative means of assisting citizens who are going through crises due to mental illness or substance abuse. Currently, approximately half of SMPD’s officers have had the training. Other officers are being trained as schools become available. New tasers SMPD recently purchased new tasers for every officer in the department. The tasers will be issued once all officers have been trained and certified in their use. Free computer tune-up and repair For several years Rae Taylor, Ray Reese, and Brad Pebble have been gifting the community with their technological know-how. If you have need of a computer tune-up or repair, they will be meeting the first Saturday of each month at the St. Matthews Library from 1-4 p.m. If you have a desktop computer, bring only the computer, as they will have a mouse, keyboard, and monitor on site. There is no charge for the service, and if you need parts to complete a repair, they will show you where you can purchase them and do the installation for you. The service will take place in the second floor meeting room. For more information, visit www.computertuneupandrepair.weebly.com. 2-1-1 United Way: Find resources with one call We all need a little help from time to time and finding the right information quickly is vital. With Metro United Way’s 2-1-1, the answers you are looking for are just a three digit phone call away. Metro United Way’s 2-1-1 directs people looking for resources to help them face life challenges, often for the first time, to the appropriate service provider. A trusted and caring call specialist will listen to the full scope of your issue and connect you immediately to the right source. This easy-to-remember phone number saves time and frustration by eliminating the need to navigate a maze of agencies and help-lines. With 2-1-1 services, there is no longer a wrong number when seeking to find or give help. Should the 2-1-1 number not work from your phone, use 1-877-566-4968. Metro United Way provides many services including those involving basic needs, individual and family life, consumer services, criminal justice and legal services, mental health care and counseling, education, organizational/community/international services, environmental quality, support groups, health care, income support, and employment. Knock, knock. Who’s there? Before you open your door to anyone, find out who is on the other side. If the person knocking is a salesperson or charity worker, ask to see a current City of St. Matthews certificate of registration. Don’t open the door if they say they do not have one with them. Even in this era of online shopping, door-to-door soliciting is common. All commercial solicitors operating within the city – even those just leaving brochures or business cards at residences or businesses – must carry a certificate of registration issued by the City of St. Matthews. A certificate does not imply that the city endorses the business or organization – it simply means they are registered. Any person representing a nonprofit group, whether charitable or religious, must also carry a permit when seeking donations door-to-door or in any public place – and must show it to you when asked. Charitable and religious organizations are exempt from this requirement if they’re just providing information. All Solicitors must respect “No Solicitors” signs and may not solicit prior to 9 a.m. or after 7 p.m. any day of the week, nor at any time on Sunday or on a state or national holiday. Persons under the age of 16 or any person exempted by Louisville Metro Ordinance are not required to have a certificate of registration from the City of St. Matthews. If someone comes to your door and does not have a City of St. Matthews certificate of registration, arrives prior to or after the allowed solicitation time, is unusually persistent, or has ignored a posted “No Solicitation” sign, call the St. Matthews Police Department at 893-9000. Make a habit of protecting your family – ask to see permits! Lock it up Warm weather is coming! Please remember to shut and lock doors and windows whenever you leave your home. Most burglaries are crimes of opportunity. Don’t give the bad guys the opportunity. Hot car warning Never leave children or pets in your car. Cars can reach unsafe temperatures in a matter of minutes when the sun is shining bright. You can be cited or even arrested for leaving children or animals in a hot vehicle. Fireworks safety City of St. Matthews Ordinance § 11.04 states: “It shall be unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or other entity to use or detonate any display fireworks within the City of St. Matthews, except for professionally staged and permitted events.” Never give fireworks – not even sparklers – to children. They are dangerous devices that can cause severe injury and even death. Sparklers cause 16% of fireworks injuries. Whereas water boils at 212 degrees, cakes bake at 350 degrees, wood burns at 575 degrees, and glass melts at 900 degrees, a sparkler burns at 1200 degrees. If you decide to ignore the law and your clothes catch on fire, remember to Stop, Drop, and Roll. Grilling safety There’s nothing like outdoor grilling. It’s one of the most popular ways to cook food. But a grill placed too close to anything that can burn is a fire hazard. Grills can be very hot, causing burn injuries. They should never be left unattended, and children should be kept away from them at all times. To safely use propane grills, check the gas tank hose for leaks before using it at the start of the grilling season. Apply a light soap and water solution to the hose. A propane leak will release bubbles. If you have determined that your grill has a gas leak and there is no flame, turn off the gas tank and grill. If the leak stops, get the grill serviced by a professional before using it again. If the leak does not stop, call the fire department. If you smell gas while cooking, immediately get away from the grill and call the fire department. Do not move the grill. If the flame goes out, turn the grill and gas off and wait at least 15 minutes before relighting it. Always keep the lid open when lighting the grill. Only use starter fluid with charcoal grills, and never apply it to an open flame. When you are finished grilling, allow the coals to cool completely before disposing of them in a metal container.
